Native eastern woodland; rhizome for joint/connective-tissue support (year 4+ harvest). Arching stems with paired leaves. Don't confuse with toxic False Solomon's Seal (Maianthemum).
Sticky-leaf annual; lymphatic tonic tea/tincture (use fresh — dried loses activity). Velcro-like burrs cling to clothing.
European herb of historic use; tannic astringent for digestive/skin issues. Yellow flower spikes; clinging hooked seeds.

windows.notes: Cold stratify seed for germination

entries.notes: Native eastern US; white tubular flowers in early summer. 'Husker Red' is the AAS-winning burgundy-foliage cultivar. Bumblebee favorite.
Western US native; brilliant red tubular flowers, hummingbird magnet. Drought-tolerant for southwestern xeriscape gardens.
Western US native; deep blue-purple tubular flowers. Drought-tolerant; the easiest penstemon for inland mountain gardens.
Native moist-meadow perennial; gold-orange daisy flowers, late summer-fall bloom. Misnamed - doesn't cause hayfever (uses pollinators not wind).
entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension - Native Pollinator Plants

entries.notes: Compact (3 ft) hybrid; orange-red-yellow speckled daisies. Long bloom (mid-summer through frost). Pinch in spring for bushier habit.
Native prairie goldenrod; flat-topped flower clusters. Late-season pollinator essential. Doesn't cause hayfever (often blamed; ragweed is the actual culprit).
Cultivar with arching gold flower sprays; less aggressive than wild goldenrods. Mt. Cuba 2008 pollinator-attractor trial winner.
Tall (4-6 ft) native; deep purple/pink fall daisies. Critical late-season nectar for migrating monarchs. 'Purple Dome' is a compact cultivar.
Native eastern US; many cultivars available (Wood's Pink, Wood's Blue). Pinch in early summer for bushier autumn bloom. Mildew-prone in poor air circulation.
entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension - Native Pollinator Plants

entries.notes: Drought-tolerant native; aromatic foliage when crushed. October Skies, Raydon's Favorite are top cultivars. Latest-blooming aster.
Native woodland aster; sky-blue clouds of flowers in part shade. Self-seeds without becoming weedy. Heart-shaped basal leaves.

windows.notes: Plant corms 4-6 in deep

entries.notes: Native prairie corm; purple bottle-brush flower spikes. Excellent cut flower; bumblebees and butterflies favorite. Top-down bloom.
THE monarch magnet — researchers consider it the top monarch nectar flower. Open buttons (vs spike) of L. spicata. Wet to medium soil.
Native tall (5-8 ft) wetland aster relative; deep purple fluffy fall flowers. Late-season butterfly nectar. Requires moist soil.
Native moist-meadow perennial; tall (5-7 ft) dome-shaped pink flower clusters. Top butterfly nectar plant. 'Gateway' is a compact cultivar.
Native blue-flowered hardy ageratum (often confused with annual ageratum). Late-season nectar. Spreads by rhizome - contain or use in informal areas.

windows.notes: Cold stratify 60 days

entries.notes: Western counterpart to common milkweed; large pink star-shaped flower clusters. Monarch host. Less aggressive than A. syriaca.

windows.notes: Cold stratify 60 days

entries.notes: Slender native milkweed (1-3 ft); whorled needle-like leaves. White flowers. Monarch host. CAUTION: toxic to grazing livestock.
Native wet-meadow perennial; white turtle-head shaped flowers. Sole host plant of Baltimore Checkerspot butterfly. Wet to medium soil.
Native eastern US; pink-rose turtle-head flowers, late summer. More drought-tolerant than white turtlehead. Bumblebee favorite.
Native moist-meadow perennial; brilliant scarlet flower spikes. Hummingbird magnet. Short-lived (3-5 years) but self-seeds readily.
Native moist-soil perennial; deep blue flower spikes. Bumblebee specialist. Hybridizes with cardinal flower (purple offspring).
Native legume; deep blue lupine-like spikes. Long-lived (50+ years), drought-tolerant. Don't transplant established clumps. Year 3 best bloom.
Native southeastern US; white pea-like flower spikes on tall stalks. Same long lifespan and drought tolerance as B. australis.
Southeastern native; yellow lupine-like spikes. More heat-tolerant than true lupines (Lupinus). Long-lived, deer-resistant.
Native eastern US shrub; white flower clusters. Nitrogen-fixer; deep roots. Leaves used as tea substitute during American Revolution.
California native shrub; clouds of fragrant blue flowers in spring. Pollinator powerhouse. Drought-tolerant once established. Skylark, Concha cultivars.
California native annual; pink/coral cup-shaped flowers. Self-seeds. Cool-season; bolts in heat (hence the name).
California native; ruffled pink/lavender/white flowers in tall spires. Self-seeds. Cool-spring annual.
California desert annual; deep blue bell-flowers. Distinct from the lacy phacelia (P. tanacetifolia) cover-crop entries; this one is purely ornamental.
Native plains perennial; red-and-yellow daisies, drought-tolerant. Parent of garden Gaillardia x grandiflora hybrids.
Garden hybrid; large multi-color daisies, blooms first year from seed. Arizona Sun, Goblin, Mesa series. Short-lived (3 years) but self-seeds.
Native prairie perennial; drooping yellow petals around dark coneflower head. 4-5 ft. Long bloom (June-September).
Native shortgrass-prairie perennial; tall thimble-shaped centers with drooping yellow/red petals. Drought-tolerant.
Native tallgrass-prairie perennial; perfoliate leaves form 'cups' that hold rainwater (bird drinking station). 6-10 ft, sunflower-like blooms.
Native tallgrass-prairie perennial; deep tap root, basal leaves orient north-south (the compass habit). 7-10 ft yellow flower spires year 3+.
Native umbel; gold flat-topped flower clusters in late spring. Larval host for Black Swallowtail butterfly. Beneficial-insect favorite.
Native prairie umbel; spiny yucca-like leaves, white globe flowers. Drought-tolerant; Native Americans used as snakebite treatment.
European herbaceous perennial; metallic blue flower clusters with thistly bracts. Salt-tolerant. Excellent dried flower.
Asian Agastache; lavender-blue spikes, anise-mint aroma. Both ornamental pollinator and culinary use (Korean perilla-mint banchan).
New Mexico native; pink flower spikes, long bloom. Hummingbird magnet. Drought- and heat-tolerant; deer-resistant.
Perennial Plant of the Year 1997; deep violet-blue flower spikes. Cut back after first flush for late-summer rebloom. Drought-tolerant.
Tender perennial Salvia hybrid; deep purple-black flowers. Massive pollinator magnet. Annual outside zone 7; treat as houseplant indoors in winter.
European perennial sage; blue-purple flower spikes. Caradonna, Marcus, Rose Marvel are top cultivars. Excellent for cottage and meadow gardens.
Native annual Monarda (texas/oklahoma); whorled lavender-pink flowers, lemon scent. Self-seeds. More heat-tolerant than M. didyma.
Perennial Plant of the Year 1998; horizontal-petal cultivar (vs reflexed in standard E. purpurea). Long-blooming, butterfly favorite.
White-petal cultivar with orange-bronze cone. Comes true from seed (rare for Echinacea cultivars). Bumblebee, butterfly, finch favorite.
AAS winner; compact (24 in) deep-pink coneflower that blooms first year from seed. Top choice for fast meadow establishment.
AAS Heritage; tall (4-5 ft) cosmos with 4 in white/pink/crimson flowers. Self-seeds; cut for vase use stimulates bloom.
Mexican native annual cosmos; orange-yellow-red daisies. Bright Lights, Cosmic series. More heat-tolerant than C. bipinnatus.

windows.notes: Plant tubers; lift in zones below 7

entries.notes: Mexican tuberous perennial cosmos; deep chocolate-maroon flowers smelling literally of cocoa. Hardy zone 7+; lift like dahlia in colder zones.
Mexican annual; the standard zinnia. California Giant, Lilliput, Cut & Come Again seed strains. Powdery mildew if crowded.
Compact disease-resistant series (mildew/leaf spot tolerant). 12-18 in mounding habit. AAS winner; reliable in humid southeastern gardens.
Tall (3-4 ft) cultivar with 5-6 in flowers. Strong stems for cutting. Mix or single colors available.
entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension - Cut Flower Zinnias

entries.notes: The flower-farmer standard; 4-6 in fully double blooms in 13 colors. Long stems (40 in), excellent vase life. Most bouquet-grade zinnia.
entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension - Cut Flower Zinnias

entries.notes: Classic 8-12 ft single-head sunflower; large edible seeds. Russian Mammoth, Mammoth Grey Stripe. The kid-garden gateway flower.
Branching multi-headed sunflower; mahogany-bronze-yellow color blend. 5-7 ft. Pollen-free strains exist for cut flower use.
Dwarf (2-3 ft) double-flowered sunflower; fluffy yellow pompoms. Good for containers and small gardens.
Branching 5-6 ft sunflower; deep maroon-velvet flowers. Cut-flower favorite. Continuous bloom from mid-summer through frost.
Heirloom branching sunflower; small (4 in) cream-white flowers with chocolate centers. Multi-headed, 5-7 ft.
Tall (6-8 ft) branching annual; pale lemon-yellow petals around chocolate centers. Top pollinator-attractor in Great Sunflower Project research.
Tall (2-3 ft) double-flowered African marigold; mixed yellow/orange/gold colors. Deer-resistant; pest-suppressive root exudates (nematodes).
Compact (12-14 in) French marigold; double mixed colors. Long bloom; reliable companion for tomatoes/peppers.
Heirloom French marigold; bright orange-and-mahogany ruffled crested flowers. Compact 12 in; classic edging plant.
Cultivated form of Flanders poppy; pink/red/white tissue-paper petals. Self-seeds in cool gardens. Source seed from extension/seed-bank, not roadside (could be invasive form).
Subarctic perennial usually grown as biennial; orange/yellow/pink/white papery flowers. Cool-summer climates only.
Heirloom breadseed/culinary poppy; seeds for baking, dried pods for crafts. Latex contains opium alkaloids - cultivation legal but extraction is federally controlled.
Perennial poppy; large 4-6 in red/orange/pink flowers in late spring. Foliage dies back in summer (interplant with later perennials to fill gap).
Trailing crimson amaranth; 4-6 ft tall, dramatic dangling flower tassels. Self-seeds. Edible grain like other amaranths.
Cottage-garden biennial; large 2 in bell flowers in pink/blue/white. Sow year 1 for year 2 bloom. Cut spent stalks for self-seeding.
entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension - Cottage Garden Annuals

entries.notes: Biennial dianthus; clusters of pink/red/white spice-clove-scented flowers. Edible petals (remove white heel). Self-seeds.
entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension - Cottage Garden Biennials

entries.notes: Tall (2-3 ft) annual with apple-green calyces stacked along stem; tiny white flowers within. Excellent cut and dried flower.
entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension - Cut Flower Annuals

entries.notes: Australian everlasting; papery petals dry without losing color. Premier dried flower; cut at half-open stage.
entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension - Dried Flower Annuals

entries.notes: Mediterranean annual; papery winged flower clusters in purple/blue/white/pink. Salt-tolerant. Excellent cut and dried.
entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension - Cut Flower Annuals

entries.notes: Brain-coral-shaped fasciated flower heads in red/yellow/pink/orange. Heat-loving. Excellent cut and dried.
Feathery plume-shaped flower heads; same colors as cockscomb. Forest Fire and Castle series are reliable bedding cultivars.
Wheat-shaped narrow flower spikes in pink/cream. Flamingo Feather and Pink Candle cultivars. Cut-flower farmer favorite.
Tropical perennial often grown as annual; flowers open mid-afternoon (4 PM), fragrant. Multi-color blooms on same plant. Tubers hardy zone 7+.

windows.notes: Nick seed coats for germination

entries.notes: Tropical climbing vine; large 5-6 in white fragrant flowers open at dusk. Hawkmoth-pollinated. Trellis 10+ ft.

windows.notes: Soak seed 24 hours

entries.notes: Heavenly Blue, Grandpa Ott's, Scarlett O'Hara. Climbing vines (8-10 ft); flowers open at dawn. Self-seeds; can naturalize aggressively.

windows.notes: Soak seed 24 hours

entries.notes: 1699 Sicilian heirloom — the original bicolor purple-and-maroon sweet pea. Strongest fragrance of any cultivar. Toxic seeds (do not eat).
entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension - Heirloom Sweet Peas

windows.notes: Plant bulbs after first frost

entries.notes: Tall (24-30 in) classic-shape tulip; the standard 'cut flower' tulip. Treat as annual in zones 8+ (no winter chill); perennializes 3-5 years in zones 3-7.
Wild species tulip ('water lily tulip'); 6-10 in dwarf, naturalizes well. Earlier bloom than Darwin types. Long-lived in zones 3-7.
Long-lived perennial; deer- and rodent-proof (toxic alkaloids). Naturalizes in lawns. Allow foliage to die back naturally for next year's bloom.
Earliest spring bulb (often blooms through snow). Naturalizes in lawns. Distinct from saffron-yielding C. sativus (autumn-blooming, separate entry).
Small (6-9 in) blue grape-cluster flowers; naturalizes aggressively. Foliage emerges in fall, persists through winter. Spreads by bulb offsets and seed.

windows.notes: Plant 'in the green' if possible (right after bloom) for best establishment

entries.notes: Earliest spring bulb in many zones; nodding white bell flowers through snow. Long-lived; spreads slowly. Toxic to humans (alkaloids).
Naturalizing woodland bulb; blue/pink/white nodding bells. More vigorous than English bluebell - can crowd out natives in shade gardens.
Shade groundcover; intensely fragrant white bell flowers in May. ALL PARTS HIGHLY TOXIC (cardiac glycosides). Spreads aggressively by rhizome.

windows.notes: Pre-soak corms; plant 2-3 in deep, claws down

entries.notes: Persian buttercup; fully-double rose-like flowers in jewel tones. Cool-season cut flower; lift after foliage dies back. Annual outside zone 8.
entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension - Cut Flower Bulbs

windows.notes: Pre-soak corms; plant 2 in deep

entries.notes: Mediterranean annual corm; jewel-toned poppy-like flowers with black centers. De Caen and St Brigid series. Lift in colder zones; perennial in zone 7+.
entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension - Cut Flower Bulbs

entries.notes: Shade-tolerant evergreen perennial; nodding flowers in late winter/early spring. Deer-resistant (toxic). Long-lived; resents transplanting.
Shade-tolerant perennial; pink heart-shaped flowers on arching stems in spring. Foliage dies back in summer. Mildly toxic.
Native eastern US woodland; red-and-yellow tubular spurred flowers, hummingbird favorite. Self-seeds. McKana hybrids and Songbird series for color range.
Native woodland spring ephemeral; pink buds open to sky-blue bells. Foliage dies back by midsummer (interplant with hostas to fill gap).
Woody peony (3-5 ft shrub) vs herbaceous; large dinner-plate flowers in late spring. 50+ year lifespan; resents transplanting.

windows.notes: Plant eyes 1-2 in deep — too deep prevents blooming

entries.notes: Long-lived herbaceous perennial; massive fragrant blooms in late spring. Sarah Bernhardt, Festiva Maxima, Karl Rosenfield are top heirlooms. Stake doubles.

windows.notes: Plant rhizomes shallow, top exposed to sun

entries.notes: Drought-tolerant perennial; thousands of colors and patterns. Divide every 3-4 years to prevent rot. Self-cleaning for spent bloom-spike removal.
Beardless iris; narrow grass-like foliage, smaller flowers in blue/purple/white/yellow. More moisture-tolerant than bearded. Caesar's Brother is a classic.
Compact (12-18 in) reblooming daylily; gold flowers from May through frost. Most-planted perennial in US gardens. Edible buds and flowers.
Shade groundcover/specimen; thousands of named cultivars (Sum and Substance, Patriot, Halcyon). Edible spring shoots in Asian cuisine. Slug magnet.
Shade perennial; feathery plume flowers in pink/red/white/lavender. Needs consistent moisture (drought-intolerant). Bressingham Beauty, Fanal cultivars.
Native woodland coral bells; ornamental purple/lime/burgundy foliage, airy flower spikes. Hummingbird-attracting bloom. Many cultivars (Palace Purple, Caramel).
Mat-forming evergreen; pink/blue/white flower carpet in spring. Drought-tolerant; for slopes and rock gardens.
entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension - Native Pollinator Plants

entries.notes: Native eastern US tall (3-4 ft) phlox; fragrant white/pink/purple flower clusters in mid-late summer. David is the classic mildew-resistant white. Butterfly favorite.
Mildew-resistant cultivar of bee balm; deep red whorled flowers, hummingbird and bumblebee favorite. Distinct from existing bee-balm entry by mildew resistance.
Native eastern US perennial; star-shaped sky-blue flower clusters in spring, gold fall foliage. Long-lived, drought-tolerant. Hubricht's amsonia (A. hubrichtii) has finer foliage.
Native west/midwest US perennial flax; sky-blue flowers each open one morning then drop. Long bloom (May-July). Self-seeds; meadow/xeriscape plant.
Ornamental allium hybrid; 4-in purple drumstick flower heads on tall stalks. Drought-tolerant, deer-resistant (allium chemistry). Naturalizes well.
1991 University of Minnesota release; explosive crisp texture, balanced sweet-tart. Cold-hardy. Bitter pit prone - calcium foliar sprays help. Plant pollinator partner.

maritime.notes: Excellent maritime apple; cool wet falls develop deep crisp texture. Scab pressure heavy in Pacific NW; choose Liberty or Cosmic Crisp for resistance.

continental.notes: 1991 UMN release ideal for cold continental Zones 3 to 5; chill hours 800 plus. Stores 4 to 6 months at 32F.

humid_subtropical.notes: Marginal in Southern zones; chill hours requirement (800) limits to mountain South. Bitter pit pressure on calcium-deficient soils.

mediterranean.notes: Marginal in coastal CA; needs cooler nights than zone 9 typically provides. Try in inland valleys with mountain microclimate.

entries.notes: 1962 Japanese cross of Red Delicious x Ralls Janet; crisp, very sweet, top US-market apple. Long season - October pick. Excellent storage (6+ months refrigerated).

mediterranean.notes: Premier CA apple; long warm Mediterranean falls develop full Fuji sweetness. Chill hours 500.

humid_subtropical.notes: Lower chill (500) makes Fuji viable in upper South zones 7 to 8. Scab and fire blight pressure heavy; spray schedule mandatory.

maritime.notes: Marginal in Pacific NW; needs warmer fall than maritime typically provides. Try in inland valleys; scab pressure heavy.

continental.notes: Productive in mid-Atlantic and Northeast continental orchards; harvest Oct through Nov before first hard freeze.

entries.notes: 1934 New Zealand cross; sweet mild flavor, crisp texture. Early-mid season pick. Lower chill requirement (~500-600 hours) - works in mild-winter zones.
1868 Australian seedling; bright green, very tart, the canonical pie apple. Long season - late October pick. Excellent storage (8+ months).

mediterranean.notes: Late-ripening (Oct through Nov) tart green apple; thrives in long warm Mediterranean falls. Chill hours 400; minimal winter requirement.

humid_subtropical.notes: Lower chill requirement (400) makes Granny Smith viable in upper South zones 7 to 8. Scab and fire-blight pressure heavy; spray schedule mandatory.

maritime.notes: Late-ripening; harvest Oct through Nov before fall storms. Scab pressure heavy in Pacific NW; integrated pest management.

arid.notes: Productive in Sonoran high-desert orchards; the defining inland CA / NM apple. Drip-irrigate consistently to prevent watercore.

entries.notes: Cornell-bred (1978); resistant to apple scab, cedar-apple rust, fire blight, mildew. Best low-spray choice for organic east-coast orchards.
Purdue/Rutgers/Illinois disease-resistant cultivar; spicy-tart, late season. Resistant to scab + immune to fire blight in many regions. Good keeper.
1600s Danish heirloom; tart-sweet aromatic apple, the Sebastopol-CA heritage cider apple. Triploid - needs 2 OTHER pollinator cultivars (it doesn't pollinate them).
1811 Ontario heirloom; tart, soft when cooked (cider/sauce favorite). Cold-hardy; the parent of Cortland, Empire, Spartan, Macoun.
1770 English heirloom (called 'Williams' in UK); the standard canning pear. Pick green-ripe; ripens to yellow at room temp. Plant pollinator partner.
French heirloom; egg-shaped, mild sweet flavor, stays green even when ripe. Excellent storage (3-4 months refrigerated). Late-season pick.
Belgian heirloom; russet-skinned long-necked pear with dense crisp flesh. Holds shape baked. Late pick; 3-month storage.
1800s French heirloom; squat round shape, the canonical 'gift basket' pear (richest flavor). Short season; doesn't store as well as Bosc/Anjou.
Japanese cultivar; bronze russet skin, juicy crisp white flesh. Fire-blight resistant - top choice for east-coast asian pear orchards.
entries.source: OSU Extension Service - Asian Pear Cultivars

entries.notes: 1875 Oregon Lewelling heirloom; the canonical dark sweet cherry. Self-INFERTILE - needs Rainier/Lambert/Black Tartarian as pollinator. Cracks in heavy rain at ripening.
entries.source: OSU Extension Service - Sweet Cherry Cultivars

entries.notes: 1952 WSU cross of Bing x Van; yellow with red blush, sweet mild flavor. Self-INFERTILE; needs pollinator. Birds find them irresistible - net.
French heirloom sour cherry; the standard pie cherry. Self-fertile (one tree sufficient). Smaller, more compact than sweet cherry trees.
entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension - Sour Cherry Cultivars

entries.notes: 1968 Canadian self-fertile sweet cherry; the 'one tree' option. Bing-quality dark sweet fruit. Sets without a pollinator partner.
European prune-plum; oval purple fruit, freestone, dries to prunes. Self-fertile. Late September pick. The canonical PNW plum.
entries.source: OSU Extension Service - European Plum Cultivars

entries.notes: 1906 Luther Burbank Japanese-plum hybrid; red-purple skin, amber-red flesh. Partially self-fertile but heavier crops with pollinator partner. Late June - early July pick.

mediterranean.notes: Premier CA stone fruit; chill hours 300 met in coastal zones. Late June ripening Japanese plum; the defining California plum.

humid_subtropical.notes: Lower chill (300) makes Santa Rosa viable across the Southeast. Brown rot pressure heavy; spray schedule mandatory.

arid.notes: Productive in inland CA / AZ; drip-irrigate consistently during fruit fill. Self-fertile, no pollinator needed.

maritime.notes: Productive in Pacific NW; cool wet springs reduce brown rot vs Eastern climates. Bacterial canker pressure on older trees.

entries.notes: Ancient European plum group (named after Damascus); small tart purple fruit, the canonical jam/preserve plum. Self-fertile. Cold-hardy.
1700s English heirloom (originally French Reine Claude); small green-yellow fruit, intensely sweet honey flavor. Connoisseur's plum.
1940 Michigan State release; the standard freestone peach. Self-fertile. Early-mid season; requires 800-1000 chill hours.

continental.notes: Late-July to early-August ripening freestone; cold-hardy to Zone 4. The defining mid-Atlantic and Northeast home orchard peach.

humid_subtropical.notes: Productive in upper South Zones 7 to 8; chill hours 950. Brown rot pressure heavy in humid summers; spray schedule mandatory.

maritime.notes: Marginal in Pacific NW; peach leaf curl pressure heavy. Spray copper at bud break; choose Frost or Q-1-8 for PNW resistance.

mediterranean.notes: Productive in inland CA; chill hours 950 met in most zones. Drip-irrigate consistently during fruit fill.

entries.notes: 1875 Georgia heirloom; freestone, the historic southern canning peach. Self-fertile. Late-season pick. 800 chill hours.
NC State cold-hardy peach; bud-hardy to -25F. Top choice for marginal northern peach growing (NY, MI, OH). Late August pick.
UNH cold-hardy peach; bud-hardy to -20F. The canonical zone-4 peach. Mid-August pick. 1000 chill hours.
1849 Massachusetts heirloom; deep blue slip-skin grape, the Welch's juice/jelly cultivar. Self-fertile. Cold-hardy native American grape.
entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension - American Grape Cultivars

continental.notes: Cold-hardy American slipskin grape; the defining Northeast and Midwest juice and jelly grape. Tolerates Zone 4 winters.

humid_subtropical.notes: Disease-resistant Vitis labrusca; productive across the upper South. Black rot pressure heavy in humid summers; spray schedule helpful.

maritime.notes: Productive in Pacific NW with full sun; the defining home-garden juice grape. Powdery mildew pressure on tight clusters; train on a high cordon.

mediterranean.notes: Productive in coastal CA; the home juice grape of choice. Drip-irrigate during fruit fill.

entries.notes: 1872 white sport of Concord; intense foxy-grape flavor. Used for white juice and white wine. Cold-hardy.
entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension - American Grape Cultivars

entries.notes: 2006 University of Minnesota cold-hardy red wine grape; bud-hardy to -35F. The canonical northern red-wine grape (Pinot-Noir-style).

continental.notes: University of Minnesota 2006 release; cold-hardy red winegrape to Zone 3. The defining cold-climate red for Midwest and Plains orchards.

maritime.notes: Productive in Pacific NW; cool maritime summers do not stress the vine. Lower brix than warmer-zone Marquette; harvest late September.

humid_subtropical.notes: Marginal in deep South; cold-climate hybrid prefers crisp continental falls. Try in upper South mountain zones.

entries.notes: 1996 University of Minnesota cold-hardy red wine grape; bud-hardy to -36F. Berry-cherry flavors, Port-style sweet wine.
French-American hybrid red wine grape; disease-resistant. Top choice for east-coast (PA, NY, VA) wineries. Late season pick.
entries.source: Penn State Extension - Wine Grape Cultivars

entries.notes: Cornell-bred (1972) hybrid white wine grape; disease-resistant, cold-hardy to -15F. Riesling-style. Good Finger Lakes / east-coast cultivar.
entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension - Wine Grape Cultivars

entries.notes: Most cold-hardy mandarin (zone 8b with protection); seedless, easy-peel. Owari is the standard cultivar. Late October-November pick.
Lemon-mandarin hybrid; sweeter, less acidic than common lemon. Improved Meyer is the disease-cleaned cultivar. Container/houseplant culture in zones below 8.
Mexican lime; small tart fruit, the canonical Key lime pie ingredient. Frost-tender — container culture in cooler zones, bring indoors below 30F.
Persian/Tahitian lime; larger and more cold-tolerant than key lime. Seedless. The standard US grocery-store lime.
1820s English heirloom; classic California heirloom apricot, intensely flavored. Self-fertile. Bloom-frost prone in zones 6-7.
Yellow freestone nectarine; large red-blushed fruit, sweet rich flavor. Self-fertile. Early-mid August pick. 500-600 chill hours.
1885 California heirloom; large freestone apricot, the canonical drying cultivar. Self-fertile. Late bloomer (less frost-prone than Blenheim).
UC Davis day-neutral cultivar (2006); produces continuously through the season once established. Conical large fruit, sweet. Top choice for matted-row systems.
UC Davis day-neutral cultivar (1991); large bright-red fruit, holds quality in heat. Disease-resistant. Long fruiting from June through frost.

windows.notes: Plant corms in late summer for fall bloom

entries.notes: Source of saffron spice; harvest red stigmas (3 per flower) in fall. ~50 flowers per gram of dried saffron. Sterile - propagate by corm divisions. Distinct from spring-blooming Crocus vernus.
Tropical climbing orchid; pods cured 6 months for vanilla beans. Hand-pollinate flowers (no native US pollinator). Greenhouse only outside Hawaii/Puerto Rico.
Middle Eastern oregano; thyme-savory-mint cross flavor. The fresh-leaf base of za'atar spice blend (also includes sumac, sesame, salt).
Thai/Indonesian rhizome (kha); peppery-piney heat (different from ginger). Container culture in cool zones. Distinct lesser galangal (A. officinarum) is hotter.
Australian native tree; intensely citrus-scented leaves (highest citral content of any plant). Container culture in cooler zones.
Australian native tree; intense anise-aniseed-licorice aroma in leaves. Container culture for most US zones.
Chinese cinnamon; thick rough bark, the common US 'cinnamon'. Higher coumarin content than Ceylon - moderate use only. Coppice-harvested.
Sri Lankan true cinnamon; thin papery bark layers, sweeter milder flavor. Lower coumarin than cassia — safer for daily use.
Indian/Sri Lankan ginger relative; green pods harvested for spice. World's third most expensive spice (after saffron, vanilla). Container culture only outside zone 10.
Indian climbing vine; same plant for black (unripe sun-dried), white (ripe peeled), and green (immature) peppercorns. Trellis 12+ ft.
Source of all true tea (green/white/oolong/black/pu-erh - processing differs, plant doesn't). Acid soil, partial shade. var. assamica is the warm-climate strain.
Highland tropical shrub; cherry fruit contains the bean. 4-5 years to first crop. Hawaii (Kona, Ka'u) is the only US coffee-producing region. Container houseplant elsewhere.
Caribbean tree; dried unripe berries combine flavors of cinnamon-clove-nutmeg (hence the name). Dioecious - need male and female. Container culture in cool zones.
Source of chocolate; Mesoamerican rainforest understory tree. Pollinated by tiny midges (challenging in cultivation). Hawaii (zone 11) only US production region.
Mediterranean tree; thousands of years in cultivation. Drought-tolerant; cold-hardy to ~15F. Self-fertile but better with pollinator. Mission, Manzanillo for California; Frantoio for oil.
Catalan high-density olive cultivar; small fruit, top choice for super-high-density orchards. Self-fertile, more cold-hardy than Mission. Bears young (year 3).
Mediterranean sprawling shrub; flower buds harvested at green stage and brined into capers. Drought- and salt-tolerant. Slow to establish from seed.
Named pawpaw cultivar from Florida; large mango-flavored fruit. Distinct from existing generic pawpaw entry. Plant 2 grafted cultivars for cross-pollination.
Most widely-planted US jujube cultivar; large round fruit, eaten fresh like a small apple-pear. Self-fertile but heavier with Lang as pollinator.
entries.source: Texas A&M AgriLife Extension - Jujube Cultivars

entries.notes: Pear-shaped jujube; classic drying cultivar (Chinese 'red date'). Pollinator partner for Li. Self-fertile but better cropping with cross-pollination.
entries.source: Texas A&M AgriLife Extension - Jujube Cultivars

entries.notes: 1969 Cornell-bred everbearing red raspberry; the canonical fall-bearing cultivar. Mow canes to ground in late winter for once-a-year fall crop.
OSU-bred (1953) summer-bearing red raspberry; nearly thornless, classic PNW cultivar. Bears on year-2 floricanes.
Cold-hardy 6-8 ft cultivars (Bluecrop, Patriot, Northland, Bluejay). Distinct from existing blueberry by climate adaptation. Plant 2+ cultivars.
Southeastern US species; better heat-tolerant and lower chill (300-600 hours). Climax, Tifblue, Premier cultivars. Self-INFERTILE — plant 2+ cultivars.
Native northeast US wild blueberry (Maine staple). Spreads by rhizome to form mats; best harvested with hand-rake. Cold-hardy to zone 2.
USDA-bred thornless semi-erect blackberry; the canonical thornless cultivar for home gardens. Heavy yields, large sweet fruit.
Wild woodland strawberry species; tiny intensely flavored fruit. Doesn't run (forms clumps); excellent edging plant. Mignonette, Alexandria, White Soul cultivars.
Two crops per year (June and September); Quinault, Ozark Beauty are common cultivars. Distinct day-length response from June-bearing and day-neutral.
1899 Luther Burbank introduction; pineapple-flavored quince fruit (closer to fresh-eating than other quince cultivars). Self-fertile.
Tall (4-7 ft) forage-bred soybean cultivars (Tyrone, Big Fellow); cut for silage/hay before pod fill. Distinct from grain soybean entries.
Mediterranean annual legume; reseeding cover/forage. Burr medic (M. polymorpha) and barrel medic (M. truncatula) common. Pasture interseeded.
White lupin; high-protein edible bean (Italian lupini). MUST be debittered (long water-soak) - raw seeds contain toxic alkaloids. Distinct from ornamental Lupinus.
1700s English heirloom fava cultivar; large 5-7 seed pods. Distinct from fava-bean and fava-broad-bean by specific cultivar lineage.
Largest seeded type; runner habit, used for ballpark roasted peanuts. Distinct from existing peanut entry by botanical variety.
Small round seeds with red skins; bunch habit, shorter season. The peanut for peanut butter and candy; high oil content.
entries.source: Texas A&M AgriLife Extension - Peanut Cultivars

entries.notes: 3-4 seeds per pod; sweet flavor, classic boiled-peanut cultivar. New Mexico organic specialty. Bunch habit.
Drought-tolerant warm-season oilseed; tall (3-6 ft) plants. Indeterminate pod set - cut whole plants when bottom pods just split. Heat-loving.
entries.source: Texas A&M AgriLife Extension - Sesame Production

entries.notes: Mexican mint-family annual; tiny seeds high in omega-3 ALA, mucilage. Day-length sensitive — bolts when nights lengthen, so southern US production.
Brown/golden oilseed flax cultivars (NorMan, Linnea); ground for omega-3 ALA. Distinct from fiber-flax entry by cultivar selection (shorter, branchier).
Edible-oil rapeseed (Canadian Oil, Low Acid; <2% erucic acid). Distinct from rapeseed-cover entry. Spring or winter cultivars depending on region.
Black-oil sunflower; hybrid cultivars bred for high seed-oil yield. Distinct from existing sunflower (ornamental) and sunflower cultivars by industrial-oilseed use.
Same species as safflower-dye; this entry covers high-oleic oilseed cultivars (Saffire, Centennial). Cooking oil with high smoke point.
Pale yellow seed for ballpark mustard. Same species as yellow-mustard-cover; this entry covers seed harvest. Cold-tolerant short-season cool crop.
Brown/oriental mustard seed; the spice base of dijon, English mustard, Chinese hot mustard. Distinct cultivar group from existing mustard-greens.
Wild ancestor of corn; tiny 6-10 kernel ears, hard shell. Mostly grown for ethnobotany/breeding. Conservation interest — nearly extinct in the wild.
Asian/African annual grass; pearly tear-shaped grains for craft beads (decorative form) or food (Coix lacryma-jobi var. ma-yuen). Distinct from corn family.
Open-pollinated purple-skin orange-flesh carrot; sweeter and more uniform than Dragon. Distinct from carrot-purple/dragon-carrot by lineage.
Campbell Soup Co. heirloom (1950s); smooth round (vs ribbed) deep-green pods. Tender even at 7-8 inches. Reliable in humid summers.
Rutgers all-male hybrid; vigorous, rust-resistant, longer productive life than older Jersey series. The current standard for new asparagus beds.
Anthocyanin-rich purple spears (cooks to dark green); 20% sweeter, less stringy. Mixed-sex (M and F) — produces some berries unlike Jersey hybrids.
1837 English heirloom; green stalks with red blush, classic baking rhubarb. Productive and reliable; the most widely-grown cultivar.
Very cold-hardy rhubarb; deep red stalks throughout (no green core). Sweeter than Victoria. Top choice for far-north gardens.
Common-type horseradish; smaller leaves and finer roots than Common (Maliner Kren) cultivar. Less prone to leaf disease.
Purple-skinned tomatillo (vs green). Sweeter than green types when fully ripe; husks split as fruit fills. Distinct from existing tomatillo entry.
Polish heirloom ground cherry; sweet pineapple-tomato flavor. Ripe fruit drops to ground in husks - rake up daily. Self-seeds prolifically.
entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension - Heirloom Ground Cherries

entries.notes: 1925 American heirloom; deeply savoyed (crinkled) dark-green leaves, sweet flavor. Long Standing Bloomsdale is bolt-resistant strain.
Pre-1860 southern heirloom; large cabbage-like 30-36 inch plants. Heat-tolerant for southern gardens; sweetest after frost.
1935 southern AAS heirloom; bright-green frilled-edge leaves, mildly hot mustard flavor. The standard southern table-mustard cultivar.
Burgundy-purple curly kale F1 hybrid; extra sweet after frost (color deepens). Ornamental enough for flower beds.
British overwintering broccoli; small purple sprouts after a cold winter. Plant in fall for spring harvest. Cold-hardy to ~10F under row cover.

entries.scientificName: Brassica oleracea var. botrytis 'Purple of Sicily'
entries.notes: Italian heirloom; deep purple anthocyanin-rich heads. Color cooks to green when boiled - steam or roast briefly to retain.
Cornell-bred orange cauliflower (1980s); 25x the beta-carotene of white. Color holds when cooked. Tighter heads than purple types.
Modern F1 deep-purple cauliflower; brighter and more uniform than Italian heirloom. Holds color when steamed (vs boiled).
Two-stage crop: grow root in field summer, force in dark sand/cellar in winter for 'chicons' (white tight 6-inch heads). Bitter-sweet salad green.
Italian Roman classic; hollow asparagus-like flowering shoots cut from rosette. Slice thin, soak in ice water for the famous Roman salad.

windows.notes: Best as cold-frame overwintering crop

entries.notes: Cold-hardy mache cultivar; long narrow leaves. Tolerates -5F under cold frame. Very nutty mild flavor; doesn't bolt in cool weather.
1984 AAS dwarf snap pea; 24 in plants, no trellis needed. Earliest snap pea (~50 days). Small space and container favorite.
entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension - Snap Pea Cultivars

entries.notes: Heat-tolerant shelling pea; produces in warmer weather than most peas. 7-8 peas per pod. Reliable for late spring sowing in mid-Atlantic.
entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension - Shelling Pea Cultivars

entries.notes: Ornamental and edible grain amaranth; deep burgundy-purple foliage and flower plumes. Distinct from amaranth-grain by ornamental coloring.
Multicolor quinoa from Wild Garden Seed (Frank Morton); cream-pink-cherry seeds and brilliant flower-head colors. Distinct from existing quinoa entry.
French heirloom moschata pumpkin; flat lobed wheel shape, tan skin ripening to deep brown. Sweet smooth flesh; vine borer resistant (C. moschata trait).
Long pale-green ribbed melon used as cucumber; the snake-like form distinct from existing armenian-cucumber entry. Striped Armenian is the variant.

windows.notes: Hybrid F1; 6 to 7 weeks indoors is plenty for an early type
Indeterminate; cage or stake

entries.notes: Workhorse early indeterminate; first ripe fruit weeks ahead of beefsteaks. Crack-prone if watering swings.
entries.source: UC ANR; Petoseed/Seminis cultivar trials; The Old Farmer's Almanac

windows.notes: 8 weeks indoors for a stocky transplant

entries.notes: Classic indeterminate slicer; VFN disease resistance package makes it forgiving for new gardeners.
entries.source: Burpee cultivar release records; Cornell Cooperative Extension home tomato guide

windows.notes: Determinate; one good cage is enough

entries.notes: All-America Selections 1984 winner; determinate so fruit ripens in a concentrated window. Excellent disease resistance (VFFNTA).
entries.source: All-America Selections winner records; Texas A&M AgriLife Extension

entries.notes: 1934 Rutgers University and Campbell Soup release; the canning and paste standard for decades. Determinate, balanced sweet/acid.
entries.source: Rutgers New Jersey Agricultural Experiment Station cultivar release notes

windows.notes: Cold-tolerant; can transplant a touch earlier with row cover

entries.notes: Czech heirloom; sets fruit in cool soil where heat-loving cultivars sulk. Potato-leaf, indeterminate, golf-ball-sized fruits.
entries.source: Seed Savers Exchange catalog; OSU Extension northern-grower tomato variety notes

entries.notes: Swedish breeder release; semi-determinate, prolific in cool maritime and short northern summers. 2 to 3 oz fruits.
entries.source: Seed Savers Exchange; Washington State University Extension cool-season tomato evaluation

windows.notes: Sets fruit down to 38 to 40 degrees F nighttime

entries.notes: Russian heirloom; one of the few tomatoes that sets fruit in genuinely cold nights. Determinate, 3 to 5 oz fruit.
entries.source: University of Alaska Fairbanks Cooperative Extension; Seed Savers Exchange

entries.notes: Beaverlodge AB Research Station release for the Canadian prairie; arguably the earliest open-pollinated tomato available. Compact determinate, 2 oz cherries clustered heavily.
entries.source: Agriculture and Agri-Food Canada Beaverlodge Research Farm cultivar release records

entries.notes: Russian heirloom despite the name; mahogany-purple pear shape, smoky flavor. Indeterminate. Crack-resistant for a black tomato.
entries.source: Seed Savers Exchange; SSE 2008 trial notes

entries.notes: Italian heirloom; deeply ribbed, lobed, almost pleated red beefsteak. Heat tolerant, performs well in long Mediterranean summers. Indeterminate.
entries.source: University of California Vegetable Research and Information Center; Italian seed-saver collections

windows.notes: 10 weeks indoors; needs bottom heat
Wait for soil at 65 F

entries.notes: Bird's-eye Thai chili. Small upright fruits, 50000 to 100000 SHU. Highly productive; one plant feeds a household.
entries.source: UF/IFAS Extension small-fruit pepper trial; Chiltepin & Capsicum Center, NMSU

windows.notes: 12 weeks indoors; very slow germinator, 75 to 85 F bottom heat

entries.notes: St. Augustine FL regional heirloom in the habanero species; 100000 to 300000 SHU with a fruity flavor. Heat-loving but tolerates Atlantic humidity.
entries.source: UF/IFAS Extension St. Johns County; Datil Pepper Center, Flagler College

windows.notes: Tall plant; allow 3 ft spacing

entries.notes: Peruvian baccatum; 30000 to 50000 SHU, fruity berry-citrus undertone. The defining flavor of huancaina sauce. Plants grow tall, sprawl.
entries.source: International Potato Center (CIP) Capsicum baccatum collection notes; UF/IFAS hot pepper variety trial

entries.notes: 5 to 7 inch tapering yellow fruits ripening to red; 5000 to 10000 SHU. Productive in short seasons; classic pickling pepper.
entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension; OSU Extension Hot Pepper Variety Trial

entries.notes: Italian / Cuban frying pepper; thin walls, mild (100 to 1000 SHU), turns yellow then red. Holds heat well, perfect for blistering in a pan.
entries.source: UF/IFAS Vegetable Variety Trial; Cornell Cooperative Extension

entries.notes: Italian heirloom from the Nardello family of CT; sweet red 8 to 10 inch slender frying pepper. Slow Food Ark of Taste listed. Heat sweetens dramatically on a hot pan.
entries.source: Slow Food Ark of Taste; Seed Savers Exchange

windows.notes: Compact plants suit containers and small beds

entries.notes: Snack-size 2 to 3 inch sweet pepper series in red, yellow, orange. AAS 2017 winner. Heavy producer; container-friendly.
entries.source: All-America Selections 2017 winner records; Johnny's Selected Seeds variety notes

windows.notes: Bare-root plant while dormant; potted any time soil works

entries.notes: Late-ripening (Oct to Nov); crisp, balanced sweet-tart. Needs a long season; chill hours ~700. Cross-pollinate with a mid- or late-blooming cultivar.
entries.source: WSU Tree Fruit Research and Extension; New Zealand Plant & Food Research cultivar notes

entries.notes: Trademark name for cultivar Cripps Pink; very late ripening (Oct to Nov). Needs a long warm autumn for full pink color and flavor. Chill hours 400 to 500.
entries.source: WSU Tree Fruit Research; University of California Cooperative Extension Pomology

mediterranean.notes: Premier Mediterranean apple; very late ripening (Nov) develops deep pink color in long warm CA falls. Chill hours 400 to 500.

humid_subtropical.notes: Lower chill (400 to 500) makes Pink Lady viable in upper South zones 7 to 8. Excellent fire blight resistance.

arid.notes: Outstanding inland CA / Phoenix / Tucson apple; drip-irrigate consistently. Late ripening matches dry warm desert SW falls.

maritime.notes: Marginal in maritime; needs longer warm fall than Pacific NW typically provides. Try in inland valleys with mountain rain shadow.

entries.notes: Cornell 1968 Jonathan x Golden Delicious cross; large, crisp, honey-tart. Triploid: cannot pollinate other trees, requires two other diploid cultivars nearby.
entries.source: Cornell University NYSAES cultivar release records; WSU Tree Fruit Research

entries.notes: Cornell 1898 Ben Davis x McIntosh cross; cold-hardy, slow-browning white flesh prized for salads. Mid-September ripening.
entries.source: Cornell University NYSAES cultivar release records

entries.notes: Late ripening, holds shape when baked; the standard baking apple. Hardy and reliable bearer.
entries.source: Ohio State University Cooperative Extension Pomology; USDA-ARS heritage apple notes

entries.notes: Cornell 1966 McIntosh x Red Delicious; firmer than McIntosh, with the McIntosh flavor. Excellent eating apple, stores 3 months.
entries.source: Cornell University NYSAES cultivar release records

entries.notes: Canadian Summerland 1936 McIntosh-line release; deep red with white flesh. Cold-hardy, popular in BC and the maritime Northeast.
entries.source: Agriculture and Agri-Food Canada Summerland Research and Development Centre records

entries.notes: Trademark name for cultivar Scifresh; Royal Gala x Braeburn New Zealand release. Dense, juicy, tangy. Late ripening.
entries.source: New Zealand Plant & Food Research cultivar release records

entries.notes: BC Canada chance seedling, 1980s; pink-orange blush over yellow, sweet and aromatic, slow to brown. Mid-October ripening.
entries.source: Agriculture and Agri-Food Canada Summerland Research Centre records; BC Tree Fruits cultivar notes

entries.notes: Late 1800s Winesap seedling; complex sweet-tart, rich flavor for cider blends. Mid- to late-October ripening. Triploid: needs two other cultivars nearby.
entries.source: Penn State Tree Fruit Production Guide; USDA-ARS heritage cultivars

entries.notes: American colonial heirloom (1800s); spicy wine-like flavor, dense yellow flesh, late ripening. Classic Appalachian cider variety, stores all winter.
entries.source: USDA-ARS National Clonal Germplasm Repository heritage apple records

entries.notes: Japanese 1948 Golden Delicious x Indo cross; large green-yellow late dessert/cooking apple. Triploid: needs two other diploid cultivars nearby.
entries.source: Aomori Prefectural Apple Research Center cultivar release records; Cornell NYSAES variety notes

entries.notes: Cultivar WA 38; WSU 1997 Honeycrisp x Enterprise cross released 2019. Very late ripening (late October), exceptional storage life. Slow-browning, very firm. Trademark licensed to WSU growers.
entries.source: Washington State University Tree Fruit Research; WSU cultivar release records 2019

entries.notes: Purdue / Rutgers / Illinois PRI breeding program release; small bright red dessert apple with strong scab resistance. Backyard grower favorite where fungal pressure is high.
entries.source: Purdue University Horticulture PRI disease-resistant apple breeding program records

entries.notes: 1893 Arkansas chance seedling; deep purple-red skin, dense yellow flesh, spicy wine flavor. Outstanding heritage cider apple.
entries.source: USDA-ARS heritage cultivar records; University of Arkansas Cooperative Extension

entries.notes: 1870 Arkansas heirloom; near-black mahogany skin, very firm, mellows with two months of cold storage. Classic Southern cellar apple, stores into March.
entries.source: University of Arkansas Cooperative Extension Pomology; USDA-ARS heritage cultivar notes

windows.notes: Dormant bare-root vines; full-sun southern exposure

entries.notes: Virginia / Missouri heritage red wine grape; Vitis aestivalis ancestry, disease-resistant, makes a deep tannic dry red. The defining Missouri/Virginia wine grape.
entries.source: University of Missouri Grape and Wine Institute; Virginia Tech Viticulture

humid_subtropical.notes: Premier Southern red wine grape; Vitis aestivalis ancestry confers disease resistance through Florida humidity. The defining Virginia / Missouri winegrape.

continental.notes: Cold-hardy to Zone 5; harvest in mid-September before first frost. The home red winegrape for Eastern continental orchards.

mediterranean.notes: Marginal in coastal CA; Vitis aestivalis ancestry prefers humid summers over dry. Try inland zones where summer rain occurs.

entries.notes: Cornell 1965 Joannes Seyve 23-416 x Gewurztraminer cross; cold-hardy aromatic white. Indiana state signature grape; Gewurztraminer character without the disease pressure.
entries.source: Cornell University Grape Breeding Program; Purdue University Viticulture

entries.notes: Ugni Blanc x Rayon d'Or French hybrid; vigorous, cold-hardy, late-season white. The defining ice-wine grape in Canada and the Finger Lakes.
entries.source: Cornell University Viticulture & Enology; Brock University Cool Climate Oenology and Viticulture Institute

entries.notes: Seibel 5656 x Seibel 4986 French hybrid; widely planted in eastern North America. Crisp, mineral, citrus-driven dry white.
Kuhlmann 1921 French hybrid; one of the most cold-hardy reds in commerce. Early ripening, deep color, Burgundy-style red.
entries.source: University of Minnesota Grape Breeding; University of Vermont Viticulture

entries.notes: Mexican Hidalgo wild cerasiforme line; tiny half-inch red cherries with intense old-tomato flavor. Indeterminate, disease-resistant, prolific to a fault.
entries.source: Seed Savers Exchange; Cornell University tomato evaluation

windows.notes: Color develops with direct sun on shoulders

entries.notes: OSU 2011 anthocyanin-rich purple-black cultivar; black where exposed to sun, red where shaded. Indeterminate. First widely available high-anthocyanin tomato.
entries.source: Oregon State University vegetable breeding program; Myers et al. release notes 2012

entries.notes: Italian heirloom drying tomato; small plum-shaped 1.5 oz fruits in heavy clusters. Determinate. Low moisture content makes it the classic sun-dried tomato.
entries.source: Seed Savers Exchange; Italian heirloom seed catalogs

entries.notes: AAS 1943 winner; the original yellow-orange slicer that mainstreamed non-red tomatoes. Indeterminate, low-acid, mild flavor.
Kentucky bicolor heirloom beefsteak; yellow with red marbled flesh, low acid, fruity sweet. 1 to 2 lb fruits, indeterminate. Sprawler; cage with strong support.
entries.source: Seed Savers Exchange; Southern Exposure Seed Exchange catalog

entries.notes: Belgian breeder Pascal Moreau release; green-purple shouldered beefsteak with marbled rainbow flesh. Indeterminate.
entries.source: Seed Savers Exchange; Tomato Growers Supply variety notes

entries.notes: Compact bright-yellow determinate; 4 oz round fruits, mild flavor. Good container choice. AAS-style early yellow.
entries.source: Johnny's Selected Seeds variety notes; Cornell vegetable variety database

entries.notes: Harris Seeds 1969 hybrid; indeterminate red slicer, low-acid, crack-resistant. Long the Northeast home-garden default.
entries.source: Harris Seeds cultivar release notes; Cornell Cooperative Extension

entries.notes: NCSU Randy Gardner release; campari-type 2 oz red cocktail tomato with late-blight resistance. Indeterminate, prolific. Excellent for the rainy Northeast/Appalachia.
entries.source: NC State University Mountain Horticultural Crops Research Station release records

windows.notes: Heavy fruit; double-stake or cage with strong support

entries.notes: Hungarian / Russian heirloom; large 1 to 2 lb heart-shaped pink-red beefsteak. Indeterminate, low seed count, dense meaty flesh ideal for fresh eating.
entries.source: Seed Savers Exchange; Tomato Growers Supply variety notes

entries.notes: Early Brandywine-type pink heirloom; potato-leaf indeterminate. Brandywine flavor in a shorter season; good Northeast pick.
entries.source: Seed Savers Exchange; SSE 1992 trial notes

entries.notes: Yellow potato-leaf sibling of pink Brandywine; sweet, slightly tropical, large lobed beefsteak. Indeterminate.
entries.source: Seed Savers Exchange; Heirloom Tomatoes Carolyn Male reference

entries.notes: Oaxaca Mexico heirloom; yellow-orange bicolor beefsteak streaked with red. 1 to 2 lb, indeterminate, low acid sweet.
Minnesota bicolor heirloom; yellow with red interior streaks, 1 to 2 lb. Indeterminate, prolific.
entries.source: Seed Savers Exchange; University of Minnesota Extension

entries.notes: Russian dark-shoulder heirloom; brick-red with green-bronze shoulders, smoky sweet flavor. Indeterminate. Beloved at heirloom tomato tastings.
entries.source: Seed Savers Exchange; Heritage Harvest Festival tasting notes

windows.notes: Pick when fruit has a pink blush at the blossom end

entries.notes: Greene County Tennessee German-American heirloom; large beefsteak that stays green at maturity with a pink-yellow blush. Sweet spicy flavor, indeterminate. SSE distributed 1991.
WV German-American heirloom; yellow with pink streaks, 1 lb beefsteak. Mild, fruity, low acid. Indeterminate.
Polish heirloom paste; long banana-shaped 4 to 6 inch fruit, dry meaty flesh, ideal for sauce. Indeterminate, wispy potato leaves; cage early.
entries.source: Seed Savers Exchange; Carl Swidorski / Polish-American heritage seed collection

entries.notes: Avery Island LA Capsicum frutescens; 1 inch upright orange-red fruits ripening to red, 30000 to 50000 SHU, watery flesh ferments into the namesake sauce.
entries.source: LSU AgCenter Hot Pepper Variety Trial; McIlhenny Company cultivar records

windows.notes: 14 weeks indoors; pubescens needs heat plus a long season

entries.notes: Andean Capsicum pubescens (manzano / locoto); apple-shaped 2 inch fruits, black seeds, 50000 to 100000 SHU. Tolerates cool nights better than other hot species; perennial overwinter where frost-free.
entries.source: International Potato Center (CIP); UF/IFAS Capsicum species notes

entries.notes: Texas / northern Mexico semi-wild glabriusculum; tiny pea-sized fiery-pungent 50000 to 100000 SHU. Slow germinator; perennial subshrub in zones 9 plus.
entries.source: USDA-ARS Capsicum collection; NMSU Chile Pepper Institute

entries.notes: Caribbean (Venezuela / Cuba) chinense without the chinense heat; habanero aroma at 0 to 500 SHU. The defining sofrito pepper.
entries.source: UF/IFAS Tropical Research and Education Center variety trial; UPR Mayaguez Capsicum collection

entries.notes: South American baccatum with three-lobed bell shape; 5000 to 30000 SHU. Mild ring around base, hotter at center. Tall plants.
entries.source: USDA-ARS Capsicum collection; NMSU Chile Pepper Institute

entries.notes: Philadelphia Italian-American heirloom from Horace Pippin; cayenne-type long pepper ripening through purple to red. 5000 to 30000 SHU.
entries.source: Seed Savers Exchange; Truelove Seeds heritage seed catalog

entries.notes: Italian heirloom bullhorn sweet pepper; long curved 8 inch fruits in red or yellow strains. Sweet, thick walls, excellent for grilling and frying.
entries.source: UF/IFAS Vegetable Variety Trial; Italian heritage seed collections

windows.notes: Slow germinator; 4 weeks to germinate is normal

entries.notes: The wild ancestral Capsicum annuum of the Sonoran Desert; tiny pea-shaped 50000 to 100000 SHU fruits with bird-dispersed seeds. Perennial subshrub in zone 8 plus.
entries.source: Native Seeds/SEARCH; USDA-ARS Capsicum wild-relative collection

entries.notes: Peruvian baccatum; bright yellow 2 inch tapered fruits, 30000 to 50000 SHU with intense lemon-citrus aroma. Tall plants.
entries.source: International Potato Center (CIP) Capsicum baccatum collection; UF/IFAS hot pepper trials

windows.notes: Variegated leaves; striking ornamental edible

entries.notes: Chesapeake African-American heirloom; cream and green striped fruits ripen through orange to red, 5000 to 30000 SHU. Variegated foliage. Preserved by Horace Pippin and reintroduced by Michael Twitty.
entries.source: Seed Savers Exchange; Truelove Seeds; Michael Twitty Cooking Gene research

windows.notes: Cucurbits transplant if root-disturbed minimally; pot up only

entries.notes: Cornell 1968 release in the Marketmore series (76, 80, 86, 97); the slicer standard for the Northeast. Disease-resistant, dark green, 8 inch fruits.
entries.source: Cornell University vegetable breeding program; NYSAES variety release records

maritime.notes: Cool maritime soils slow germination; transplant only after night temps hold above 55F. Powdery mildew pressure heavy in August; choose Marketmore 97 for resistance.

humid_subtropical.notes: Direct sow in early spring; trellis vertical for airflow against downy mildew. Cucumber beetle and bacterial wilt pressure peaks in May.

continental.notes: Direct-sow after last frost; harvest standing through August. Mid-summer succession sow for September picking before powdery mildew shuts plants down.

arid.notes: Plant in early spring; harvest by mid-July before triple-digit heat triggers bitterness. 30% shade cloth above 95F preserves fruit quality.

entries.notes: Chinese heirloom; 12 to 18 inch ribbed slim cucumber, burpless, mild sweet. Best trellised so fruit hangs straight.
entries.source: Seed Savers Exchange; Kitazawa Seed Company variety notes

entries.notes: Mesoamerican Melothria scabra (different genus from cucumber); tiny grape-sized fruits that look like watermelons. Trellis on cattle panels; drought-tolerant once established.
entries.source: Native Seeds/SEARCH; UF/IFAS minor cucurbit notes

entries.notes: Indian heirloom; ripens through white, yellow, to russet brown like an Asian pear. Sweet and crisp at every stage. Heat tolerant.
entries.source: Seed Savers Exchange; Baker Creek Heirloom Seeds catalog

entries.notes: Vigorous trellising cucumber; 8 to 9 inch dark green slicer, good disease tolerance. Selected for cool maritime productivity.
entries.source: Kitazawa Seed Company; Seed Savers Exchange

entries.notes: 1877 American heirloom; the classic short blocky pickler. 3 to 6 inch fruits, crisp, productive. Pick small for cornichons, larger for dills.
entries.source: Seed Savers Exchange; Cornell vegetable variety database

entries.notes: Asian-line slicer; 7 to 9 inch dark green fruits, thin skin, low cucurbitacin (the bitter compound). Cool-summer productive.
entries.source: Cornell vegetable variety database; UC ANR cucumber notes

entries.notes: AAS 1935 winner; 8 inch perfectly straight blocky slicer. Long the home-garden default in North America. Vining habit.
Compact bush slicer for containers and small gardens; 7 to 8 inch fruits on 2 to 3 ft vines. Good early producer.
entries.source: Burpee cultivar release notes; Cornell variety database

entries.notes: AAS 2002 winner; parthenocarpic (self-fertile) hybrid producing seedless cucumbers without pollination. Smooth thin skin, sweet, productive under cover.
Cornell-revived heirloom Cucurbita pepo winter squash; small cylindrical cream-and-green-striped fruits, thin edible skin, sweet flesh. Bush-type, productive in short seasons.
entries.source: Cornell University vegetable breeding; Cornell Cooperative Extension

maritime.notes: Excellent producer in cool maritime; bush habit with quick 90-day finish. Black plastic mulch and row cover speed germination and early growth.

humid_subtropical.notes: Plant early to beat July vine borer pressure. Diatomaceous earth around base of stems; row cover until female flowers open.

continental.notes: Direct-sow after soil hits 65F; finishes reliably before first frost. Excellent storage squash for short-season zones where Hubbard would not mature.

arid.notes: Plant in early spring; harvest by August before triple-digit heat stalls fruit set. Drip-irrigate consistently to prevent blossom-end rot.

entries.notes: Japanese Cucurbita maxima; squat round green-skinned fruits with dense sweet golden flesh. Sun, Cha-cha, Sweet Mama are standard cultivars. Excellent keeper.
entries.source: OSU Extension cucurbit variety trials; Kitazawa Seed Company

entries.notes: 1850s New England heirloom; huge 10 to 30 lb teardrop-shaped warty squash. Dense yellow-orange flesh, the original Thanksgiving pie squash. Stores 6 months plus.
entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension; UMaine Extension cucurbit notes

entries.notes: 1932 ND Agricultural College release; turban-shaped dark green squash with distinctive button. Dense, sweet, dry flesh, excellent for pie. 4 to 5 lb.
entries.source: North Dakota State University Extension; Cornell variety notes

entries.notes: Cucurbita pepo with stringy flesh that pulls apart in spaghetti-like strands when cooked. Yellow oblong 3 to 5 lb fruits. Vegetable Spaghetti, Tivoli are standard cultivars.
entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension; UF/IFAS cucurbit notes

entries.notes: Slate-blue-skinned Hubbard selection; also used as a trap crop for cucumber beetle on commercial farms. 10 to 20 lb.
entries.source: UMass Extension Vegetable Program (cucumber beetle trap crop research); Cornell Cooperative Extension

entries.notes: Cucurbita argyrosperma from the American Southwest / Mexico; long-necked striped squash, vine borer resistant, heat tolerant. Green-striped and white-striped strains. The traditional Three Sisters squash south of Pueblo country.
entries.source: Native Seeds/SEARCH; USDA-ARS Cucurbita argyrosperma collection

entries.notes: 1893 American heirloom; long pink, blue, or jumbo white banana-shaped Cucurbita maxima fruits to 20 to 30 lb. Sweet dry flesh.
entries.source: Seed Savers Exchange; USDA-ARS heritage cultivar collection

entries.notes: Distinctive multi-color turban-shaped fruits; Turk's Turban, French Turban strains. Ornamental and edible. Stores well.
Single-serving 4 inch round Cucurbita pepo; cream with green stripes, sweet golden flesh. Bush habit, productive in short seasons.
entries.source: Cornell vegetable variety database; Johnny's Selected Seeds notes

windows.notes: Direct sow 100 days before first fall frost for Halloween harvest

entries.notes: Carving-pumpkin types: Howden, Connecticut Field, Autumn Gold. 10 to 25 lb round orange Cucurbita pepo, ridged. Hollow for carving, marginal eating quality.
entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension; UNH Extension pumpkin guide

entries.notes: Small (4 to 8 lb) sugar pumpkins for cooking. New England Pie, Sugar Pie, Winter Luxury are standard cultivars. Dense flesh, low water content.
entries.source: UNH Extension; Johnny's Selected Seeds variety notes

entries.notes: Rouge Vif d'Etampes type Cucurbita maxima; flat red-orange ribbed pumpkin, 15 to 25 lb. Excellent eating, dramatic appearance.
entries.source: Seed Savers Exchange; Cornell variety database

entries.notes: Provencal Cucurbita moschata; flat ribbed 20 lb fruit, mottled green ripening to deep tan. Sweet dense flesh, vine borer resistant, the gold-standard French cooking pumpkin.
entries.source: Seed Savers Exchange; French heritage seed catalogs

entries.notes: AAS 1963 butterhead winner; loose dark green head, slow to bolt, the durable home-garden butterhead.
entries.source: All-America Selections 1963 records; Cornell vegetable variety database

entries.notes: Loose-leaf lettuce with deeply lobed oak-shaped leaves; green and red strains. Heat tolerant for a loose-leaf, slow to bolt.
entries.source: Johnny's Selected Seeds variety notes; Cornell vegetable database

entries.notes: Batavian / summer crisp lettuces (Nevada, Magenta, Sierra); crisp like iceberg, bolt-resistant like butterhead. The summer lettuce pick.
entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension; Johnny's Selected Seeds bolt-resistance trials

entries.notes: American heirloom (Matchless); tongue-shaped pointed loose-leaf lettuce, green and red strains. Heat-tolerant.
entries.source: Seed Savers Exchange; Cornell vegetable variety database

entries.notes: AAS 1985 winner; bronze-red ruffled loose-leaf, slow to bolt, the durable colored loose-leaf.
Wine-red glossy loose-leaf; the darkest red lettuce in common circulation. Holds color well in heat.
entries.source: Johnny's Selected Seeds variety notes; Cornell vegetable variety database

entries.notes: British mini-romaine; 5 to 6 inch upright single-serving heads. Sweet, crisp, the gold-standard small lettuce. 12 inch spacing or tighter.
entries.source: Johnny's Selected Seeds; Royal Horticultural Society lettuce trials

entries.notes: 1850s English heirloom mini butterhead; tennis-ball sized heads on tight 6 inch spacing. Excellent container or windowsill crop.
entries.source: Seed Savers Exchange; Cornell vegetable variety database

entries.notes: Austrian heirloom (the name means trout-back); romaine with red speckles on green leaves. Same family as Flashy Trout's Back.
entries.source: Seed Savers Exchange; Austrian heritage seed collections

entries.notes: Kentucky 1865 Major John Bibb release; small butterhead, the bibb-type namesake. Tender, sweet, slightly cupped leaves.
entries.source: Seed Savers Exchange; University of Kentucky Cooperative Extension

entries.notes: 1890s MI greenhouse loose-leaf; the original greenhouse / cool-temperature lettuce. Frilly green leaves, very fast.
entries.source: Michigan State University vegetable variety records; Cornell vegetable database

entries.notes: 1840s French winter romaine; flat red-tinged heads, cold-hardy through mild winters. Excellent overwinter lettuce in Mediterranean and maritime climates.
entries.source: Seed Savers Exchange; UC ANR overwinter lettuce notes

entries.notes: British heirloom cos-butterhead cross; cold hardy, the standard overwinter lettuce under low tunnels in Zones 6 to 8.
entries.source: Johnny's Selected Seeds; Royal Horticultural Society lettuce notes

entries.notes: Danvers MA 1871 heirloom; 6 to 7 inch tapered orange roots, adapts to heavy clay soils where Nantes types misbehave.
entries.source: Seed Savers Exchange; Cornell vegetable variety database

entries.notes: Long, slim, deep-orange supermarket-style carrots (Imperator 58, Sugarsnax). Need 10 to 12 inches of loose deep soil.
French Nantes-type cylindrical carrots; sweet, crisp, the home-garden flavor standard. Yaya, Scarlet Nantes, Bolero are common cultivars.
entries.source: Johnny's Selected Seeds variety notes; Cornell vegetable variety database

entries.notes: French heirloom round carrot; 1 to 2 inch baseballs ideal for shallow / rocky soils and container culture.
entries.source: Seed Savers Exchange; French heritage seed catalogs

entries.notes: AAS 2006 winner; purple skin, orange core, Imperator type. Anthocyanin-rich, sweet, cooks to muted color.
Bright canary-yellow Imperator-type carrot; lutein-rich, sweet, holds color when cooked.
entries.source: Johnny's Selected Seeds variety notes; OSU vegetable variety database

entries.notes: French heirloom short stocky carrot; 5 to 6 inch broad-shouldered roots, the heavy clay / shallow soil pick.
entries.source: Seed Savers Exchange; Cornell vegetable variety database

entries.notes: French Nantes type; 6 to 7 inch coreless cylinders, very sweet. Classic 1920s release.
entries.source: Seed Savers Exchange; French heritage seed catalogs

entries.notes: French heirloom (pre-1885); 10 to 12 inch tapered red-orange, sweet and tender. Needs deep loose soil.
entries.source: Seed Savers Exchange; French heritage seed catalogs

entries.notes: Purple-skinned yellow-fleshed carrot; spicy-sweet flavor with peppery notes. Tapered Danvers-shaped.
entries.source: Seed Savers Exchange; Bountiful Gardens variety records

windows.notes: Short-day onion; Vidalia GA growers transplant late October
Northern growers transplant in early spring for a quicker but smaller crop

entries.notes: Short-day Granex-type onion grown in 20-county Vidalia GA area for low-sulfur soil sweetness. Geographic indication protected; growers outside the AOC use other Granex strains.
entries.source: Vidalia Onion Committee growing standards; University of Georgia Cooperative Extension

windows.notes: Long-day type for northern lats; transplant 4 to 6 weeks before last frost

entries.notes: Long-day yellow storage cultivars: Patterson, Cortland, Copra, Yellow Globe. Stiff papery skin, 6+ month storage. The Northeast staple.
entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension; Johnny's Selected Seeds long-day onion trials

entries.notes: Intermediate-day deep-burgundy onion; mild sweet for fresh use, modest storage life. Good crossover variety for the central US.
entries.source: UF/IFAS short- and intermediate-day onion trials; Cornell variety database

windows.notes: Plant topset bulbils whenever soil is workable

entries.notes: Perennial Allium x proliferum; produces topset bulbils at the top of flowering stalks. The stalk falls over, the bulbils root, and the patch walks across the bed. Cut greens like scallions year-round.
entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension perennial allium notes; USDA-ARS Allium collection

windows.notes: Sow seed densely; small bulbs are the goal

entries.notes: Small 1 inch white onion; classic pickling and stew onion. Direct-sown thickly, harvested young.

windows.notes: Short-day; fall transplant in the deep South

entries.notes: Short-day flat sweet white or yellow onion; old Texas-Florida market type. Crystal Wax, White Bermuda strains.
entries.source: Texas A&M AgriLife Extension; UF/IFAS short-day onion notes

entries.notes: Long-day red storage onion; deep-red rings throughout, 4 to 6 month storage. Northeast / Midwest favorite.
entries.source: Johnny's Selected Seeds long-day onion trials; Cornell variety database

entries.notes: Classic round yellow long-day storage onion; standard cooking onion. Selections include Yellow Globe Danvers, Southport.
entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension; USDA-ARS Allium collection

entries.notes: Short-day flat red Louisiana heirloom; pungent, stores longer than most short-day types. Deep South choice for a storage onion.

windows.notes: Plant cloves 6 weeks before hard freeze

entries.notes: Porcelain hardneck strain; large 4 to 6 clove white bulbs, strong long-keeping garlic. The Northeast / Midwest standard.
entries.source: Cornell Garlic Crop Profile; The Garlic Seed Foundation

entries.notes: Rocambole hardneck heirloom (1900s NY immigration); 8 to 10 large brown-purple cloves, classic robust garlic flavor. Short shelf life (3 to 4 months).
entries.source: Cornell Garlic Crop Profile; The Garlic Seed Foundation

entries.notes: Glazed Purple Stripe hardneck; metallic purple skin, sweet roasted flavor. Cold-hardy.
entries.source: USDA-ARS Allium sativum collection; The Garlic Seed Foundation

entries.notes: Republic of Georgia Purple Stripe hardneck; widely planted, complex sweet roasted flavor. 9 to 10 cloves per bulb.
entries.source: USDA-ARS Plant Genetic Resources Unit collection records; The Garlic Seed Foundation

entries.notes: Republic of Georgia Marbled Purple Stripe strain; intense pungent heat, large 6 to 8 clove bulbs.
entries.source: USDA-ARS Allium collection; The Garlic Seed Foundation

entries.notes: Botanically a leek (Allium ampeloprasum), not garlic; huge 4 to 6 oz bulbs, mild garlic-onion flavor. 4 to 6 large cloves.
entries.source: OSU Extension; The Garlic Seed Foundation

entries.notes: Creole softneck for warm climates (mild winter zones); deep red skin, small bulbs, intense flavor, excellent storage. The Deep South / Gulf Coast / desert SW choice.
entries.source: Texas A&M AgriLife Extension; The Garlic Seed Foundation southern garlic notes

entries.notes: Colville Reservation WA softneck; Rodale Press taste-test winner. 8 to 20 cloves per bulb, mild creamy flavor, excellent 9-month storage.
entries.source: Seed Savers Exchange; The Garlic Seed Foundation; Rodale Institute garlic taste trials

entries.notes: Silverskin softneck; tightest braiding cultivar, very long storage (up to 12 months), papery silver skin.
entries.source: Cornell Garlic Crop Profile; The Garlic Seed Foundation

entries.notes: Artichoke softneck; the canonical Italian cooking garlic. 12 to 20 cloves in concentric rows, rich flavor, 8+ month storage.
entries.source: Seed Savers Exchange; The Garlic Seed Foundation

windows.notes: Needs acidic soil (pH 4.5 to 5.2); amend before planting

entries.notes: USDA 1952 northern highbush; the global benchmark blueberry. Mid-season ripening, high yield, firm fruit, cold-hardy to -25 F.
entries.source: USDA-ARS blueberry breeding program records; OSU blueberry variety trials

maritime.notes: Ideal northern highbush for cool wet maritime; acidic soils favor blueberry naturally. Slug pressure heavy; iron phosphate baits around base.

continental.notes: Cold-hardy to Zone 4; the defining Northeast and Midwest commercial blueberry. Mulch heavily with pine needles or oak leaves to maintain pH 4.5 to 5.2.

humid_subtropical.notes: Marginal in deep South; choose rabbiteye (Vaccinium ashei) or southern highbush instead. Cold requirement 800 hours met in upper South only.

arid.notes: Challenging in alkaline arid soils; amend with sulfur and pine bark mulch to drop pH. Drip-irrigate with collected rainwater where municipal water is alkaline.

entries.notes: USDA 1986 northern highbush; early-ripening, large firm berries, frost-tolerant late-blooming. Now the dominant early commercial blueberry.
entries.source: USDA-ARS blueberry breeding records; Michigan State University blueberry trials

entries.notes: USDA 1973 northern highbush; very late ripening (extends season 3 weeks past Bluecrop), firm berries that ship well.
1928 NJ release; very vigorous late-ripening northern highbush, sweet wild-blueberry flavor. The pollinator of choice for many other cultivars.
1976 University of Maine release; half-high cross extending cold-hardiness to zone 3. Early to mid ripening, the northeast / Canadian commercial standard.
entries.source: University of Maine blueberry breeding records

entries.notes: Semi-evergreen low-chill southern highbush; compact 3 to 4 ft, container-friendly, 150 chill hours. Self-fertile. The warm-zone container blueberry.
entries.source: USDA-ARS southern highbush breeding records; UF/IFAS Extension blueberry variety guide

humid_subtropical.notes: Premier low-chill (150 hour) Southern highbush; container-friendly for patio gardens. Productive zone 5 to 10.

mediterranean.notes: Self-fertile semi-evergreen for CA gardens; 150 chill hours met in coastal zones. The defining low-chill home blueberry.

arid.notes: Container culture in arid soils; use peat / pine bark mix for pH 4.5 to 5.2. Drip-irrigate with non-alkaline water.

maritime.notes: Compact bush ideal for Pacific NW patio gardens; productive but lower yield than northern highbush. Choose Bluecrop or Duke for in-ground.

entries.notes: USDA 2007 pink-fruited rabbiteye / highbush hybrid; pale pink ripe berries, mild sweet flavor. Ornamental edible.
Michigan State 1980s dwarf half-high; 18 to 24 inch container bush, full-size fruit. Top container blueberry for small spaces.
entries.source: Michigan State University blueberry breeding records

entries.notes: 1955 Georgia rabbiteye release; the dominant southern commercial cultivar for half a century. Vigorous 8 to 10 ft, drought tolerant, 350 chill hours.
entries.source: University of Georgia Cooperative Extension; USDA-ARS rabbiteye blueberry breeding records

humid_subtropical.notes: Premier Southern rabbiteye; chill hours 350 met across the Southeast. Vigorous 8 to 10 ft bush, drought-tolerant once established.

mediterranean.notes: Productive in coastal CA with acidic soil amendment; chill hours met in most zones. Vigorous habit needs annual pruning.

arid.notes: Challenging in alkaline arid soils; amend with sulfur and pine bark to drop pH to 5.0. Container culture often more reliable.

entries.notes: Southern highbush; semi-evergreen, very productive, 150 to 300 chill hours. Container-friendly in warm climates. Self-fertile.
entries.source: USDA-ARS southern highbush blueberry breeding records; UF/IFAS Extension

entries.notes: Cornell 1998 primocane-bearing yellow raspberry; large pale golden berries, sweet tropical flavor, fall crop on first-year canes.
entries.source: Cornell University NYSAES raspberry breeding records

maritime.notes: Primocane-bearing yellow raspberry; ideal Pacific NW cool maritime climate. Fall crop on first-year canes ripens July through October.

continental.notes: Cold-hardy primocane raspberry; mow canes flat in winter for next year's fall crop. The premier short-season raspberry strategy.

humid_subtropical.notes: Marginal in deep South; choose Caroline or Heritage for higher heat tolerance. Anthracnose pressure heavy in humid summers.

arid.notes: Drip-irrigate consistently in arid heat; spider mite pressure heavy without overhead misting. Choose a partial shade location.

entries.notes: University of Maryland 1999 primocane-bearing red; the dominant fall-bearing red raspberry on the East Coast. High yield, intense flavor.
entries.source: University of Maryland Extension raspberry breeding records

entries.notes: University of Maryland 2002 primocane-bearing red; heat tolerant, root-rot resistant, large firm berry.
entries.source: University of Maryland Extension raspberry breeding records

entries.notes: Cornell 1998 floricane-bearing red; the earliest commercial summer raspberry. Cold-hardy, disease-resistant.
entries.source: Cornell University NYSAES raspberry breeding records

entries.notes: Cornell 1973 Rubus occidentalis black raspberry release; vigorous, disease-resistant, the modern black raspberry standard.
entries.source: Cornell University NYSAES black raspberry breeding records

entries.notes: 1890s OH heirloom black raspberry; large glossy black fruit, the historic mid-Atlantic commercial standard before modern releases.
entries.source: USDA-ARS heritage cultivar collection; Ohio State Extension

entries.notes: UNH 1980 yellow primocane-bearing raspberry; one of the first widely-grown yellow raspberries. Very sweet, mild.
entries.source: University of New Hampshire raspberry breeding records

entries.notes: Cornell 1998 floricane-bearing red; very late summer bearer, extends summer season 2 weeks past Heritage. Large firm berry, cold hardy.
entries.source: Cornell University NYSAES raspberry breeding records

entries.notes: Cornell 1934 release; vigorous, productive black raspberry. The historic mid-Atlantic / NE commercial standard.
entries.source: Cornell University NYSAES black raspberry breeding records

entries.notes: James Hutton Institute Scotland release; floricane-bearing, spineless canes, large pale red berries. Dominant UK commercial cultivar.
entries.source: James Hutton Institute soft fruit breeding records

entries.notes: Cornell 1979 June-bearing release; large bright red berries, cold-hardy to zone 3, the Northeast / Midwest standard.
entries.source: Cornell University NYSAES strawberry breeding records

maritime.notes: Productive Pacific NW June-bearer; gray mold pressure heavy in wet springs. Black plastic mulch and straw between rows.

continental.notes: Cold-hardy to Zone 3; the defining Northeast / Midwest home garden June-bearer. Plant in matted-row system for highest yields.

humid_subtropical.notes: Marginal in deep South; plant in fall for spring harvest. Anthracnose pressure heavy; choose Chandler or Camarosa instead for annual plasticulture.

entries.notes: Cornell 1985 mid-season June-bearer; large firm berries, the gold-standard flavor strawberry in the Northeast.
entries.source: Cornell University NYSAES strawberry breeding records

entries.notes: USDA-ARS 1981 mid-late June-bearer; large light-red berries, disease-resistant, vigorous runners. Mid-Atlantic standard.
USDA-ARS 1975 early June-bearer; intense flavor, the gold-standard for early strawberry flavor. Modest yields.
