1942 NJ June-bearer; classic flavor, cold-hardy, late-season berry. The Northeast heritage cultivar.
entries.source: Rutgers NJ Agricultural Experiment Station strawberry records

windows.notes: Southern annual plasticulture: plant in fall, harvest in spring

entries.notes: UC Davis 1983 short-day; the SE / mid-Atlantic plasticulture standard. Large bright red firm berries; biennial-bed crop in NC, GA, FL.
entries.source: UC Davis strawberry breeding program records; NC State Extension plasticulture guide

humid_subtropical.notes: Premier short-day cultivar for SE / mid-Atlantic plasticulture; plant in October for May harvest. Anthracnose-resistant; the defining North Carolina commercial strawberry.

mediterranean.notes: Premier CA short-day cultivar; plant in November for April through June harvest. The defining California plasticulture cultivar.

continental.notes: Marginal in cold continental; works in zones 7 plus or in protected winter culture. Choose Honeoye for zone 5 and colder.

arid.notes: Plant in October for spring harvest; drip-irrigate consistently. Pine straw or wood-chip mulch keeps fruit clean.

entries.notes: UC Davis 1992 short-day; CA / FL plasticulture dominant cultivar. Large firm red berries, very productive, ships well.
entries.source: UC Davis strawberry breeding program records

entries.notes: French 1991 day-neutral; intense wild-strawberry aroma and flavor. Medium-small berries, the flavor benchmark for everbearer strawberries.
entries.source: INRA Bordeaux strawberry breeding records; French heritage seed catalogs

entries.notes: USDA-ARS 1981 day-neutral; the classic Northeast everbearing strawberry. Continuous small-medium berries June through frost.
WSU 1967 everbearer; large soft berries, the home-garden everbearer for the Pacific Northwest.
entries.source: Washington State University strawberry breeding records

entries.notes: USDA-ARS 1981 day-neutral; sister cultivar of Tristar with larger berries, slightly later bearing.

windows.notes: Bare-root in early spring while dormant; container any time soil is workable

entries.notes: 1870s Georgia heirloom; large white-fleshed freestone, ripens late August. About 850 chill hours; self-fertile. Excellent fresh eating and old-fashioned pies.
entries.source: USDA-ARS GRIN Prunus collection; University of Georgia Extension stone fruit cultivars

humid_subtropical.notes: Classic Southern peach; chill hours 850 met across the Southeast. Late-August ripening freestone; brown rot pressure heavy.

mediterranean.notes: Productive in inland CA orchards; chill hours met in most zones. White-fleshed sweet peach; one of the canonical Southern dessert peaches.

continental.notes: Cold-hardy to Zone 5; late-August ripening matches mid-Atlantic harvest window. Choose for orchards needing a freestone after Redhaven.

arid.notes: Productive in inland AZ / NM at elevation; chill hours met in mountain zones. Drip-irrigate consistently.

windows.notes: Highly leaf-curl resistant; one of the best PNW choices

entries.notes: Yellow freestone; strong peach leaf curl resistance, ideal for wet coastal climates. About 700 chill hours; self-fertile, ripens mid to late August.
entries.source: Oregon State University Extension home orchard cultivar guide; Raintree Nursery release notes

entries.notes: Flat (peento) form; white flesh, very sweet low-acid flavor. About 400 chill hours, ripens early to mid July. Self-fertile; sometimes sold as Saturn or Stark Saturn.
entries.source: Stark Bro's cultivar records; University of Florida IFAS low-chill peach guide

windows.notes: Genetic dwarf; great for containers or small yards

entries.notes: Genetic dwarf to 5 to 6 ft; yellow freestone, very sweet. About 500 chill hours; self-fertile, ripens mid July. A Zaiger Genetics introduction.
entries.source: Dave Wilson Nursery cultivar records; Zaiger Genetics release notes

entries.notes: USDA Fresno 1959 release; yellow freestone, the cultivar made famous by David Mas Masumoto's Epitaph for a Peach. About 600 to 700 chill hours; ripens late July.
entries.source: USDA-ARS Fresno cultivar records; UC ANR home orchard cultivar list

entries.notes: Michigan State 1963 release; yellow freestone with deep red blush, excellent for canning. About 850 chill hours; self-fertile; late-ripening (early September) and frost-hardy buds.
entries.source: Michigan State University AgBioResearch cultivar release notes; Cornell stone fruit cultivars

entries.notes: Large flat peento type, sibling to Saturn; white flesh, very sweet. About 400 to 500 chill hours; self-fertile, ripens mid to late July. USDA-ARS Kearneysville release.
entries.source: USDA-ARS Appalachian Fruit Research Station cultivar records

entries.notes: Paul Friday Michigan series (PF lines); yellow freestone with cold-hardy buds bred for Great Lakes climate. About 850 chill hours; self-fertile; ripening varies by PF number.
entries.source: Michigan State University tree fruit cultivar guide; Flamin Fury breeder release records

entries.notes: USDA Beltsville release; yellow freestone with nearly fully red skin, firm enough for shipping. About 750 chill hours; self-fertile; ripens late August.
entries.source: USDA-ARS Beltsville cultivar release records; Penn State Extension peach cultivar list

entries.notes: Missouri 1946 release; very large yellow freestone, mild flavor, popular commercial midseason variety. About 750 chill hours; self-fertile; ripens late August.
entries.source: University of Missouri Extension stone fruit cultivar guide; USDA-ARS GRIN records

windows.notes: Late-blooming; tolerates spring frosts better than most

entries.notes: Virginia Tech release; yellow freestone bred for late bloom and frost tolerance. Outstanding choice for marginal northern sites. About 850 chill hours; self-fertile.
entries.source: Virginia Cooperative Extension home fruit cultivar list; USDA-ARS GRIN records

windows.notes: Genetic dwarf to 4 to 6 ft; container-friendly

entries.notes: Genetic dwarf yellow freestone; large fruit on a small tree. About 400 to 500 chill hours; self-fertile, ripens early to mid July. Good for containers and patios.
entries.source: Dave Wilson Nursery cultivar records; UC ANR home orchard guide

entries.notes: South African heirloom Japanese plum; red-purple skin with deep red flesh, very sweet. About 250 chill hours; self-fertile and a strong pollinator for other Japanese plums.
entries.source: University of Georgia Extension home orchard fruit cultivars; USDA-ARS GRIN records

entries.notes: NY Agricultural Experiment Station 1926 release; dark blue European prune plum, freestone, excellent for drying and canning. About 800 chill hours; self-fertile, ripens September.
entries.source: Cornell NY Agricultural Experiment Station cultivar records; Penn State Extension plum cultivars

entries.notes: European prune plum; oval blue-purple fruit, yellow-green freestone flesh, very sweet when fully ripe. About 800 chill hours; self-fertile, ripens September. PNW orchard standard.
entries.source: Oregon State University Extension home orchard guide; WSU Extension plum cultivars

windows.notes: Plant Methley or Santa Rosa nearby for best cropping

entries.notes: Luther Burbank 1899 Japanese plum; yellow skin and flesh, very heavy bearer, sweet mild flavor. About 500 chill hours; partially self-fertile, better with a pollinator. Ripens late July.
entries.source: USDA-ARS GRIN records; UC ANR home orchard cultivar list

entries.notes: Luther Burbank Japanese plum; very large heart-shaped fruit, mottled red skin with deep blood-red flesh. About 500 chill hours; needs pollinator (Santa Rosa works). Ripens late August.
entries.source: UC ANR home orchard guide; Burbank cultivar release records

entries.notes: Luther Burbank 1888 introduction from Japan; red-purple skin, yellow flesh, sweet. About 400 chill hours; needs pollinator. Ripens early August; a parent of many modern Japanese plums.
entries.source: USDA-ARS GRIN records; Burbank historical cultivar records

windows.notes: Needs an American or hybrid plum pollinator (Toka is ideal)

entries.notes: University of Minnesota 1933 hybrid; large red-skinned fruit, yellow flesh, very productive in cold climates. About 800 chill hours; needs pollinator. Ripens August.
entries.source: University of Minnesota Extension fruit cultivar trials; USDA-ARS GRIN records

entries.notes: Quebec heirloom European blue plum; very cold-hardy to zone 4. Freestone with sweet juicy flesh, excellent fresh or canned. About 800 chill hours; self-fertile, ripens September.
entries.source: University of Saskatchewan fruit program records; University of Minnesota Extension

windows.notes: Universal pollinator for hybrid and Japanese plums

entries.notes: South Dakota State 1911 Hansen hybrid; reddish-bronze fruit with spicy-sweet flavor. Cold-hardy to zone 3. Acts as a pollinator for nearly any Japanese or hybrid plum. About 800 chill hours.
entries.source: South Dakota State University cultivar records; University of Minnesota Extension

windows.notes: Pair with Toka or another hybrid for pollination

entries.notes: Niels Hansen 1913 SDSU hybrid; very large red-purple fruit on cold-hardy tree. About 800 chill hours; needs pollinator. Ripens August. Reliable cropper in the northern plains.
entries.source: South Dakota State University Hansen plum collection records

windows.notes: Pair with Toka or Superior for pollination

entries.notes: University of Minnesota 1942 hybrid; large red fruit with tough skin but sweet yellow flesh. Hardy to zone 3, ripens early August. Needs pollinator.
entries.source: University of Minnesota Extension cold-hardy fruit cultivars

entries.notes: English heirloom from 1760; very large fruit with orange skin and rich aromatic flesh, ripens unevenly on the tree. About 600 chill hours; self-fertile. Classic European jam variety.
entries.source: RHS Plant Finder; UC ANR home orchard cultivar list

windows.notes: Late-blooming and very cold-hardy; one of the best for the upper Midwest

entries.notes: Michigan State 1967 release; medium golden fruit with thick skin, productive and reliable in cold climates. About 800 chill hours; self-fertile. Ripens mid July.
entries.source: Michigan State University AgBioResearch cultivar release records

windows.notes: Very late bloom escapes most spring frosts

entries.notes: Canadian Harrow station release; compact tree, orange-red blushed fruit, resistant to brown rot and perennial canker. About 800 chill hours; self-fertile. Ripens late July.
entries.source: Agriculture and Agri-Food Canada Harrow cultivar records; OSU Extension stone fruit guide

windows.notes: One of few apricots that thrives in maritime Pacific Northwest

entries.notes: WSU Puyallup release bred for the Puget Sound region; tolerates cool wet springs that defeat most apricots. About 600 chill hours; self-fertile. Ripens late August.
entries.source: Washington State University Extension home orchard guide; Raintree Nursery release notes

windows.notes: Plant Tilton or Goldcot nearby for pollination

entries.notes: WSU 1937 release (also called Goldbeck); very large light yellow fruit, mild sweet flavor. About 700 chill hours; needs pollinator. Ripens early July.
entries.source: Washington State University Extension cultivar records

entries.notes: Rare Ontario heirloom selection; creamy white skin and flesh, sweet honey-pear flavor. About 700 chill hours; self-fertile. Ripens late July, low acid and best eaten fresh.
entries.source: Agriculture and Agri-Food Canada heritage cultivar records; Trees of Antiquity catalog

windows.notes: One of the most cold-hardy nectarines; good for northern sites

entries.notes: University of New Hampshire release; dark red skin, yellow freestone flesh, tangy rich flavor. About 800 chill hours; self-fertile. Ripens early August; brown rot tolerant.
entries.source: University of New Hampshire Cooperative Extension stone fruit cultivars; Cornell records

entries.notes: Zaiger Genetics white-fleshed nectarine; red and creamy skin, low-acid sweet flavor. About 500 chill hours; self-fertile. Ripens late July; popular farmers' market variety.
entries.source: Zaiger Genetics cultivar release records; Dave Wilson Nursery

entries.notes: New Jersey AES release; large red-skinned yellow freestone, firm flesh, ships well. About 850 chill hours; self-fertile. Ripens mid August; good crack and split resistance.
entries.source: Rutgers New Jersey Agricultural Experiment Station cultivar records

entries.notes: USDA Fresno 1969 release; large red-skinned yellow freestone, rich aromatic flavor. About 650 chill hours; self-fertile. Ripens early August; flavor benchmark for home growers.
entries.source: USDA-ARS Fresno cultivar records; UC ANR home orchard guide

entries.notes: Large red-blushed yellow freestone; firm, productive, popular commercial variety. About 750 chill hours; self-fertile. Ripens mid August.
entries.source: USDA-ARS GRIN records; UC ANR stone fruit cultivars

windows.notes: Among the most disease-resistant nectarines; bacterial spot and brown rot tolerant

entries.notes: Ontario Harrow station release; medium red-orange fruit with firm yellow freestone flesh. About 850 chill hours; self-fertile. Ripens late August; excellent humid-climate choice.
entries.source: Agriculture and Agri-Food Canada Harrow cultivar records

windows.notes: Showy double pink blossoms plus full-flavored fruit

entries.notes: Zaiger Genetics introduction; showy double pink ornamental flowers in spring followed by large dark red freestone fruit. About 300 chill hours; self-fertile. Ripens mid July.
entries.source: Zaiger Genetics cultivar release records; Dave Wilson Nursery

windows.notes: Plant after hard frosts pass; figs leaf out late

entries.notes: Southern US heirloom; small violet-brown fruit with rich sweet flavor, closed eye resists spoilage. Self-fertile; main crop only (no breba). Sometimes called Sugar Fig or Malta.
entries.source: LSU AgCenter fig cultivar guide; University of Georgia Extension

entries.notes: Spanish missionary fig brought to California in the 1700s; deep purple-black skin, pink flesh, sweet rich flavor. Self-fertile; two crops (breba and main). California commercial standard.
entries.source: UC ANR fig production guide; USDA-ARS GRIN records

mediterranean.notes: Premier CA Mission fig; long warm seasons produce dark sweet purple-black main crop in late August. The defining California commercial fig.

arid.notes: Productive in desert SW with drip irrigation; the Spanish mission fig of inland CA / AZ orchards. Heat-tolerant once established.

humid_subtropical.notes: Productive across the Southeast; chill hours not a limitation. Fig rust pressure heavy in late summer; copper spray at fruit set.

maritime.notes: Marginal in Pacific NW; cool wet falls do not develop full sweetness. Choose Desert King or Brown Turkey for maritime.

entries.notes: Italian Dottato selection; yellow-green skin with amber flesh, mild honey-sweet flavor. Self-fertile; main commercial canning fig. Two crops in long-season areas.
entries.source: UC ANR fig production guide; USDA-ARS GRIN records

windows.notes: Will die back to roots in cold winters but resprouts and fruits same year

entries.notes: Cold-hardy Mt Etna-type; survives to about minus 10 F as roots, regrows and bears on new wood. Medium purple-brown fruit with strawberry-red flesh. Self-fertile.
entries.source: Penn State Extension fig guide; Edible Landscaping Virginia cultivar notes

continental.notes: Cold-hardy fig (mulch heavily, can survive to Zone 5); the home-garden fig of choice for Northeast and Midwest. Roots survive 10F if crown is buried.

maritime.notes: Reliable producer in Pacific NW; wet winters not a problem for the dormant rootstock. Choose Desert King for the Pacific NW alternative.

humid_subtropical.notes: Productive across the Southeast; chill hours not a limitation. Fig rust pressure heavy in late summer; copper spray at fruit set.

mediterranean.notes: Ideal Mediterranean fig; long warm seasons produce two crops (breba in early summer, main in late summer). Drip-irrigate during fruit fill.

entries.notes: French heirloom; small dark purple-black fruit with strawberry-red flesh, intense berry flavor. Naturally compact tree, excellent for containers. Self-fertile; two crops.
entries.source: UC ANR cultivar list; Edible Landscaping Virginia fig collection notes

windows.notes: Best PNW fig; ripens breba crop reliably in cool summers

entries.notes: Pacific Northwest standard; large green-skinned figs with strawberry-pink flesh. Heavy breba crop on previous year's wood ripens July. Self-fertile; main crop often does not finish in cool climates.
entries.source: Oregon State University Extension fig guide; WSU Extension home orchard notes

windows.notes: Naturally dwarf; ideal patio container fig

entries.notes: Compact dwarf fig to 4 to 6 ft; dark purple-black fruit with deep red flesh, rich sweet flavor. Self-fertile; great for containers and small gardens. Two crops in mild climates.
entries.source: Raintree Nursery cultivar notes; UC ANR home fig guide

entries.notes: Thomas Jefferson grew this at Monticello; small pale green-yellow figs with white flesh, intense sweet flavor. Self-fertile; two crops. Sometimes called Lemon Fig or White Marseilles.
entries.source: Monticello historic plant records; USDA-ARS GRIN fig collection

entries.notes: Japanese non-astringent (PCNA) type; flat tomato-shaped orange fruit, eaten firm and crisp like an apple. Self-fertile; ripens October to November. The market standard kaki.
entries.source: UC ANR persimmon production guide; USDA-ARS GRIN Diospyros collection

windows.notes: Must be fully soft-ripe before eating to lose astringency

entries.notes: Japanese astringent (PVA) type; large acorn-shaped orange fruit, eaten only when jelly-soft. The classic hoshigaki drying variety. Self-fertile; ripens October to November.
entries.source: UC ANR persimmon production guide; USDA-ARS GRIN Diospyros collection

entries.notes: Japanese non-astringent kaki; squarer than Fuyu with deeper orange color and slightly sweeter flavor. Self-fertile; ripens October. Often sold as Maekawa Jiro selection.
entries.source: UC ANR persimmon cultivar list; USDA-ARS GRIN Diospyros collection

entries.notes: American persimmon (90-chromosome); large 1.5 to 2 inch orange fruit, very early ripening (September), seedless if no male nearby. Cold-hardy to zone 4. Productive, parthenocarpic.
entries.source: Northern Nut Growers Association persimmon cultivar reports; Edible Landscaping Virginia

entries.notes: Japanese astringent type; small elongated yellow-orange fruit, exceptionally sweet when fully ripe and soft. Cold-hardy for a kaki, productive. Self-fertile; ripens October.
entries.source: Raintree Nursery persimmon notes; California Rare Fruit Growers persimmon guide

entries.notes: University of New Hampshire Elwyn Meader selection; American persimmon, self-fertile (rare for the species), seedless fruit without a male pollinator. Cold-hardy to zone 4. Ripens early October.
entries.source: University of New Hampshire fruit breeding records; Northern Nut Growers Association

entries.notes: 1896 Florida selection; the US commercial standard. Large deep red fruit with crimson arils, tart-sweet flavor and high anthocyanins. Self-fertile; ripens October.
entries.source: UC ANR pomegranate production guide; USDA-ARS Davis Punica collection records

mediterranean.notes: Premier CA / Mediterranean climate pomegranate; long warm dry summers develop maximum arils. The defining commercial cultivar of the San Joaquin Valley.

arid.notes: Productive in inland AZ / NM / TX; drip-irrigate consistently during fruit fill. The defining xeriscape edible fruit tree.

humid_subtropical.notes: Productive in upper South Zone 7 plus; fruit cracking pressure in wet summers. Drip-irrigate consistently to maintain even moisture.

maritime.notes: Marginal in cool maritime; fruit does not ripen reliably in Pacific NW summers. Container culture overwintered in cool greenhouse for fruit.

continental.notes: Hardy to Zone 7 with mulch; choose Salavatski for Zone 6 cold-hardiness. Container culture overwintered indoors for colder zones.

entries.notes: Very low-acid sweet pomegranate; pink-red skin with nearly clear sweet arils, soft seeds. Self-fertile; ripens September. Good for cooler coastal sites where Wonderful struggles to ripen.
entries.source: Dave Wilson Nursery cultivar records; UC ANR pomegranate cultivar trials

entries.notes: Patented commercial variety; very large fruit with brilliant red arils and soft seeds, ripens 2 to 4 weeks earlier than Wonderful. Self-fertile; high juice yield.
entries.source: Dave Wilson Nursery cultivar records; UC ANR pomegranate cultivar trials

entries.notes: Turkmenistan heirloom from USDA-ARS Davis Levin collection; medium-large red fruit with deep red arils, soft seeds and balanced sweet-tart wine-like flavor. Self-fertile; ripens October.
entries.source: USDA-ARS Davis Punica collection records; UC ANR pomegranate cultivar trials

windows.notes: Late to break dormancy; do not give up on bare-root trees too early

entries.notes: Chinese cultivar; small round fruit with thin skin, sweet juicy crisp flesh tasting like sweet apple. Self-fertile but bigger crop with another jujube nearby. Cold-hardy to zone 6, ripens September.
entries.source: USDA-ARS GRIN Ziziphus records; University of Georgia Extension jujube guide

entries.notes: Medium pear-shaped fruit; very sweet crisp flesh, named for the sugar-cane sweetness. Heavy bearer, self-fertile, ripens early September. Mild thorns.
entries.source: USDA-ARS GRIN Ziziphus records; California Rare Fruit Growers jujube notes

entries.notes: Largest-fruited jujube cultivar; fruit can reach 2 inches long, sweet crisp flesh, excellent fresh. Self-fertile; ripens late September. From Shanxi province China.
entries.source: USDA-ARS GRIN Ziziphus records; California Rare Fruit Growers jujube notes

windows.notes: Needs a pollinator (any European pear except Seckel works)

entries.notes: Red-skinned sport of Bartlett; identical sweet juicy flesh in a deep red skin. About 800 chill hours; needs European pear pollinator. Ripens late August; pick firm and ripen off tree.
entries.source: Oregon State University Extension pear cultivar guide; USDA-ARS Corvallis Pyrus records

entries.notes: Japanese nashi pear; medium yellow-skinned round fruit, crisp juicy white flesh, mild sweet flavor. Self-fertile but better with another Asian pear. Ripens August.
entries.source: UC ANR Asian pear production guide; USDA-ARS Corvallis Pyrus records

windows.notes: Plant Shinseiki or Twentieth Century nearby for best crop

entries.notes: Japanese russet-skinned nashi; medium-large bronze fruit, very sweet juicy crisp flesh, top flavor among Asian pears. Needs pollinator; ripens August.
entries.source: UC ANR Asian pear production guide; OSU Extension pear cultivars

windows.notes: Do not plant with Bartlett; the two are cross-incompatible

entries.notes: American heirloom from 1700s Philadelphia; very small olive-bronze fruit, intensely sweet spicy flavor. The sugar pear. About 800 chill hours; needs non-Bartlett pollinator. Ripens September.
entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension pear cultivar guide; USDA-ARS Corvallis Pyrus records

entries.notes: Pennsylvania 1863 hybrid; large yellow-green pear, gritty crunchy flesh best for canning and preserves. Highly fire blight resistant; self-fertile. Ripens October; reliable cold-climate workhorse.
entries.source: Penn State Extension pear cultivar guide; USDA-ARS GRIN Pyrus records

entries.notes: Japanese 1898 introduction; small to medium yellow-green skinned fruit, crisp juicy white flesh, mild sweet flavor. The most widely planted Asian pear. Partially self-fertile; ripens August.
entries.source: UC ANR Asian pear production guide; USDA-ARS Corvallis Pyrus records

entries.notes: Japanese 1895 introduction; flat russet bronze fruit, crisp white flesh with distinctive butterscotch-rum flavor. Heavy bearer; cross-pollinator for other Asian pears. Ripens August.
entries.source: UC ANR Asian pear production guide; USDA-ARS Corvallis Pyrus records

windows.notes: Pick when greenish and eat crisp; do not let ripen on tree

entries.notes: University of Minnesota 1986 release; red-blushed green fruit eaten crisp like an apple, not ripened to softness. Hardy to zone 3 and fire blight tolerant. Needs pollinator (Parker, Luscious).
entries.source: University of Minnesota Extension pear cultivar trials

windows.notes: Plant with Summercrisp or Parker for pollination

entries.notes: South Dakota State release; small to medium Bartlett-quality fruit on a very cold-hardy tree (zone 3). Fire blight tolerant; ripens late August. Needs pollinator.
entries.source: South Dakota State University fruit cultivar records; University of Minnesota Extension

entries.notes: Canadian Summerland 1984 release (Van x Stella); large dark red sweet cherry, self-fertile, crack-resistant. About 800 chill hours; ripens late June. Pollinator for many sweet cherries.
entries.source: Agriculture and Agri-Food Canada Summerland cultivar records; OSU Extension cherry cultivars

windows.notes: Needs pollinator (Bing, Stella, Rainier all work)

entries.notes: Russian heirloom from 1700s; very dark purple-black sweet cherry with rich juicy flesh. About 700 chill hours; not self-fertile. Early-ripening (early June); a universal pollinizer.
entries.source: OSU Extension cherry cultivar guide; USDA-ARS GRIN Prunus records

windows.notes: Naturally dwarf to 6 to 10 ft; easy to net against birds

entries.notes: University of Minnesota 1950 release; genetic dwarf tart pie cherry, red skin and flesh. Cold-hardy to zone 4, self-fertile, very productive. Ripens late June.
entries.source: University of Minnesota Extension cherry cultivar records

entries.notes: Canadian Summerland 1994 release; large bright red sweet cherry, self-fertile, late-ripening (mid July). About 800 chill hours; extends the cherry season by 2 weeks past Bing.
entries.source: Agriculture and Agri-Food Canada Summerland cultivar records

entries.notes: Hungarian tart cherry; larger and darker than Montmorency with deep red flesh and richer flavor. Self-fertile; cold-hardy. Ripens 2 weeks after Montmorency, mid July.
entries.source: Michigan State University tart cherry cultivar trials; Cornell cherry records

entries.notes: University of Minnesota 1952 release; semi-dwarf tart pie cherry to 10 to 15 ft, bright red fruit with yellow flesh. Cold-hardy to zone 4; self-fertile, very productive.
entries.source: University of Minnesota Extension cherry cultivar records

windows.notes: Blooms late, which helps in frost-prone areas

entries.notes: Cornell NYSAES 2001 release; large dark red sweet cherry, self-fertile, late-blooming. About 700 chill hours; ripens mid July. Bred for the Northeast.
entries.source: Cornell NY Agricultural Experiment Station cultivar records

entries.notes: Cornell NYSAES 2001 release; yellow-skin sweet cherry with red blush, similar to Rainier but self-fertile and crack-resistant. About 700 chill hours; ripens early July.
entries.source: Cornell NY Agricultural Experiment Station cultivar records

windows.notes: Hardy enough for the Canadian prairies

entries.notes: Alberta Canada heirloom; bright red tart cherry on a very cold-hardy tree (zone 3). Self-fertile, suckers freely. Ripens mid July; excellent for pies, jam, and wine.
entries.source: Alberta Agriculture cultivar records; University of Saskatchewan fruit program

windows.notes: Plant a second genetically different cultivar within 30 ft for pollination

entries.notes: Peterson Pawpaws release from Neal Peterson breeding program; medium-large fruit with smooth custardy yellow flesh and mild banana-mango flavor, very few seeds. Needs cross-pollination.
entries.source: Peterson Pawpaws cultivar records; Kentucky State University pawpaw research

humid_subtropical.notes: Premier Eastern native fruit; ideal for upper South Zones 6 to 8. Long warm summers develop full mango-banana-custard flavor.

continental.notes: Cold-hardy to Zone 5; the defining Eastern continental native fruit tree. Plant two unrelated seedlings for cross-pollination.

maritime.notes: Marginal in Pacific NW; cool summers do not develop full flavor. Choose Sunflower or Susquehanna for cooler summers if attempting.

mediterranean.notes: Challenging in CA; pawpaw is an Eastern bottomland species needing summer humidity. Try mountain microclimates with consistent moisture.

windows.notes: Self-fertile but produces better with a second cultivar

entries.notes: Kansas heirloom; large yellow-fleshed fruit, sweet rich flavor, partially self-fertile (rare for pawpaws). Late-ripening (mid September to October). Productive once established.
entries.source: Kentucky State University pawpaw cultivar trials; California Rare Fruit Growers

windows.notes: Needs a second cultivar within 30 ft for pollination

entries.notes: Indiana 1950 heirloom; large round fruit with few seeds, smooth pale yellow custardy flesh, mild flavor. One of the historic standards; ripens September.
entries.source: Kentucky State University pawpaw germplasm records; Northern Nut Growers Association

entries.notes: Peterson Pawpaws release; one of the largest-fruited cultivars at 11 to 16 oz, very few seeds and firm yellow-orange flesh with rich complex flavor. Needs cross-pollinator.
entries.source: Peterson Pawpaws cultivar records; Kentucky State University pawpaw research

entries.notes: Illinois White Wabash 1958 hybrid selection; very sweet long blackberry-like fruit, ripens over 6 to 8 weeks rather than all at once. Self-fertile; nearly seedless and crack-resistant.
entries.source: USDA-ARS GRIN Morus records; Northern Nut Growers Association mulberry reports

entries.notes: Long-fruited Himalayan species; deep red-black fruit 3 to 4 inches long, very sweet with raspberry-like flavor. Self-fertile; not winter-hardy below about 15 F.
entries.source: California Rare Fruit Growers mulberry guide; UC ANR uncommon fruit notes

entries.notes: Old World black mulberry; deep purple-black fruit, intense sweet-tart wine-like flavor, considered the finest-flavored mulberry. Self-fertile; slow-growing and long-lived (centuries).
entries.source: RHS Plant Finder; California Rare Fruit Growers mulberry notes

windows.notes: Extremely cold-hardy; works in the upper Midwest

entries.notes: Russian-origin white mulberry; cream to lavender fruit, very sweet honey flavor. Cold-hardy to zone 4; self-fertile, fast-growing and drought-tolerant. Often planted as wildlife and chicken forage.
entries.source: USDA-ARS GRIN Morus records; Cornell Cooperative Extension mulberry notes

windows.notes: Plant a second cultivar within 60 ft for best fruit set

entries.notes: NY 1926 selection; American elderberry with large clusters of black fruit, vigorous and productive. Partially self-fertile, much better with a second cultivar (York is the classic pair). Ripens August.
entries.source: Cornell NY Agricultural Experiment Station elderberry records; University of Missouri elderberry research

continental.notes: Cold-hardy to Zone 3; productive native shrub through Northeast and Midwest. Plant two cultivars 6 to 10 ft apart for cross-pollination.

maritime.notes: Productive in Pacific NW with full sun; cool wet springs favor pollination. Choose Bob Gordon or Wyldewood for higher yield.

humid_subtropical.notes: Productive across the South; tolerant of heat and humidity. Choose Adams 1 + Adams 2 pollinator pair for best yield.

arid.notes: Marginal in dry desert SW without consistent drip irrigation. Plant in moister microclimate (north side of building).

entries.notes: NY 1964 selection; the largest-fruited cultivar with very large berries on heavy clusters. Partner with Adams or Nova for best pollination. Late-ripening (early September).
entries.source: Cornell NY Agricultural Experiment Station elderberry records; University of Missouri elderberry research

windows.notes: Pair with Adams or York

entries.notes: Nova Scotia Kentville 1959 release; sweet-flavored medium-sized berries on compact upright bushes. More self-fertile than most but cropping increases with a second cultivar. Ripens August.
entries.source: Agriculture and Agri-Food Canada Kentville cultivar records

entries.notes: Missouri 2007 selection; very large fruit clusters that bend over and drop facing the ground, reducing bird predation. High juice yield; self-fertile but better with a second cultivar.
entries.source: University of Missouri Center for Agroforestry elderberry cultivar records

windows.notes: Plant in part shade in hot-summer climates

entries.notes: University of Minnesota 1933 release; large bright red berries on long strigs, easy to pick. Self-fertile, cold-hardy to zone 3. The most widely planted red currant in North America.
entries.source: University of Minnesota Extension small fruit cultivar guide

entries.notes: Heirloom pink-fruited currant; translucent pale pink berries, milder and sweeter than red currants. Self-fertile; light cropping but exceptional dessert flavor. Cold-hardy to zone 3.
entries.source: RHS Plant Finder; Oregon State University Extension small fruit cultivar list

entries.notes: NY heirloom; translucent creamy yellow-white berries, sweetest of the currants and excellent fresh eating. Self-fertile; light crops but high quality. Hardy to zone 3.
entries.source: Cornell NY Agricultural Experiment Station Ribes records; OSU Extension

windows.notes: Naturally compact (3 to 4 ft); fits small gardens

entries.notes: Scottish Crops Research Institute release; compact bush with very large fruit. Self-fertile; mildew and big bud mite resistant. Late-blooming for frost avoidance.
entries.source: James Hutton Institute (formerly SCRI) Ribes breeding records; OSU Extension

entries.notes: Swedish-bred black currant; vigorous, very productive, immune to white pine blister rust. Large sweet berries on long strigs. Self-fertile and disease-resistant. Excellent for juice and jam.
entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension Ribes guide; Swedish University of Agricultural Sciences breeding records

windows.notes: Nearly thornless; fruit hangs free on long stems (picks well)

entries.notes: North Dakota 1932 release; American-type gooseberry, pink-blushed fruit, mild sweet flavor. Self-fertile; almost thornless and very cold-hardy (zone 3). Mildew tolerant.
entries.source: North Dakota State University fruit cultivar records; University of Minnesota Extension

entries.notes: Finnish-bred European gooseberry; medium-large red dessert berries, tart skin and sweet flesh. Self-fertile, mildew resistant, very productive. Cold-hardy and reliable.
entries.source: MTT Agrifood Research Finland Ribes records; OSU Extension small fruit guide

entries.notes: Finnish-bred European gooseberry; large yellow-green dessert berries with apricot-honey flavor. Self-fertile, mildew resistant. One of the best fresh-eating gooseberries.
entries.source: MTT Agrifood Research Finland Ribes records; OSU Extension small fruit guide

windows.notes: Very thorny; wear gloves to harvest

entries.notes: East Malling 1981 release; large pale green culinary gooseberry, very productive, mildew resistant. Self-fertile; the UK commercial standard for jam and pies.
entries.source: East Malling Research Ribes breeding records; OSU Extension small fruit guide

windows.notes: Female vine; needs a male arguta (typically 1 male per 6 to 8 females)

entries.notes: Hardy kiwi; small grape-sized smooth-skinned green fruit eaten whole, sweeter than fuzzy kiwi. Vigorous female vine, cold-hardy to about minus 25 F. Ripens September.
entries.source: Oregon State University Extension hardy kiwi guide; USDA-ARS Corvallis Actinidia records

windows.notes: Female; plant a male (Matua or Tomuri) within 30 ft

entries.notes: New Zealand 1924 selection; the world commercial fuzzy kiwi standard. Large brown fuzzy fruit with bright green flesh. Female vine; ripens late October to November. Not hardy below 10 F.
entries.source: UC ANR kiwifruit production guide; New Zealand Plant and Food Research records

windows.notes: Self-fertile but bigger crop with a male arguta nearby

entries.notes: Japanese hardy kiwi; rare self-fertile cultivar producing small smooth-skinned green fruit. Less vigorous and less cold-hardy than other arguta (zone 5). Begins bearing in 1 to 2 years.
entries.source: OSU Extension hardy kiwi guide; USDA-ARS Corvallis Actinidia records

windows.notes: Most cold-hardy kiwi species; needs a male kolomikta pollinator

entries.notes: Russian Far East species; the most cold-hardy kiwi (zone 3, to minus 40 F). Small sweet smooth-skinned green fruit; striking pink-and-white variegated foliage on male vines. Ripens August.
entries.source: University of Minnesota Extension hardy kiwi notes; OSU Extension Actinidia guide

entries.notes: Chinese-origin cultivar; large bright red elongated berries, sweeter and less bitter than wild types. Self-fertile, cold-hardy to zone 3 and drought-tolerant. Begins bearing in year 2.
entries.source: USDA-ARS GRIN Lycium records; California Rare Fruit Growers goji guide

entries.notes: Improved goji selection; very large red berries, milder flavor than wild types. Self-fertile, productive in containers and ground. Ripens from July through fall frost.
entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension goji production notes; OSU Extension uncommon fruit

entries.notes: North Dakota 1978 release; compact shrub (4 to 6 ft) with sweet blue-purple fruit, blueberry-almond flavor. Self-fertile, very cold-hardy (zone 3). Ripens late June to July.
entries.source: North Dakota State University fruit cultivar records; University of Saskatchewan fruit program

entries.notes: Saskatchewan heirloom saskatoon; very large dark blue-purple fruit, one of the largest cultivars. Tall shrub to 10 to 16 ft, self-fertile, cold-hardy to zone 2. Ripens early to mid July.
entries.source: University of Saskatchewan fruit program saskatoon cultivar records

entries.notes: Alberta Beaverlodge 1952 release; the Canadian commercial saskatoon standard. Medium-large sweet fruit, vigorous suckering shrub to 8 to 12 ft. Self-fertile; ripens early July.
entries.source: Agriculture and Agri-Food Canada Beaverlodge cultivar records

entries.notes: Scandinavian selection; black chokeberry shrub to 4 to 6 ft, large astringent black fruit very high in anthocyanins. Self-fertile, cold-hardy to zone 3. Ripens August to September.
entries.source: University of Minnesota Extension aronia production guide; Swedish University of Agricultural Sciences records

entries.notes: Eastern European selection; commercial black aronia standard, large clusters of high-anthocyanin fruit. Self-fertile, cold-hardy, productive. Used for juice, wine, and nutraceuticals.
entries.source: Iowa State University Extension aronia guide; Czech Republic horticulture breeding records

entries.notes: University of British Columbia selection; compact ornamental-and-edible aronia with brilliant red-purple fall color. Self-fertile; high-anthocyanin fruit good for juice and preserves.
entries.source: University of British Columbia botanical garden cultivar records; OSU Extension

windows.notes: Female; pair with a male such as Pollmix (1 male per 6 to 8 females)

entries.notes: German-bred female sea buckthorn; very large bright orange berries packed on stems, high vitamin C and omega-7. Cold-hardy to zone 3; nitrogen-fixing actinorhizal shrub.
entries.source: University of Saskatchewan fruit program seaberry records; German horticulture cultivar trials

windows.notes: Male pollinator; bears no fruit but is essential for female cultivars

entries.notes: German-bred male sea buckthorn; extended bloom period covers most named female cultivars. Nitrogen-fixing, cold-hardy to zone 3. Plant 1 male per 6 to 8 females within 30 ft.
entries.source: University of Saskatchewan fruit program seaberry records; German horticulture cultivar trials

windows.notes: Female; needs a male sea buckthorn within 30 ft

entries.notes: Russian-bred female seaberry; one of the largest-fruited cultivars, long stems and reduced thorniness make harvest easier. Cold-hardy to zone 3; high vitamin C. Ripens August.
entries.source: University of Saskatchewan fruit program seaberry records; Russian Lisavenko Institute breeding notes

entries.notes: Hybrid (P. edulis x P. edulis flavicarpa); large purple-skinned fruit with yellow aromatic pulp, sweet-tart flavor. Self-fertile; fast-growing vine, bears in 12 to 18 months from planting.
entries.source: UC ANR passionfruit production guide; University of Florida IFAS passion fruit notes

entries.notes: Florida selection; medium-large dark purple fruit with sweet aromatic golden pulp, considered the best fresh-eating purple passionfruit. Self-fertile; vigorous vine needs strong trellis.
entries.source: University of Florida IFAS passion fruit cultivar notes; California Rare Fruit Growers

windows.notes: Self-incompatible; needs cross-pollination or a second seedling within reach of carpenter bees

entries.notes: Yellow form of edible passion fruit; larger and more vigorous than purple, more acidic but very productive. Used for commercial juice. Heat- and humidity-tolerant; common in tropical commercial plantings.
entries.source: University of Florida IFAS passion fruit production guide; UC ANR passionfruit notes

windows.notes: Cross-pollinator (Apollo or Coolidge) increases yield substantially

entries.notes: New Zealand selection; very large oval green fruit with sweet aromatic granular flesh, mint-pineapple-guava flavor. Partially self-fertile but much better with a second cultivar. Ripens fall.
entries.source: California Rare Fruit Growers feijoa guide; New Zealand Plant and Food Research cultivar records

entries.notes: Pear-shaped medium green fruit with sweet aromatic flesh; the most reliably self-fertile feijoa, making it a good single-plant choice. California heirloom; ripens late October to November.
entries.source: UC ANR feijoa notes; California Rare Fruit Growers feijoa guide

entries.notes: Florida tropical guava; medium round fruit with pink fragrant flesh and small soft seeds, sweet rich flavor. Self-fertile; ripens summer to fall. Damaged below 28 F.
entries.source: University of Florida IFAS guava production guide; California Rare Fruit Growers guava notes

entries.notes: Yellow-skinned tropical guava with pale yellow flesh and pronounced lemon-citrus aroma. Self-fertile; productive and precocious, often bearing in year 2. Damaged below 28 F.
entries.source: University of Florida IFAS guava production guide; UC ANR guava cultivar notes

windows.notes: Hardier than tropical guava; tolerates light frost once established

entries.notes: Brazilian species; small dark red round fruit with white aromatic flesh, strawberry-guava flavor. Self-fertile; hardier than tropical guava (light frost tolerant). Invasive in Hawaii, avoid there.
entries.source: California Rare Fruit Growers strawberry guava guide; UC ANR Psidium notes

windows.notes: Wait for night temps consistently above 50 F

entries.notes: The traditional Italian pesto basil; large flat green leaves with a sweet anise-clove aroma. Pinch flower spikes weekly to keep leaf production going. Susceptible to downy mildew.
entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension; UF/IFAS basil variety trial

maritime.notes: Cool maritime summers slow basil; downy mildew pressure heavy. Choose Eleonora or Prospera DM-resistant cultivars for outdoor growing.

humid_subtropical.notes: Premier basil climate; productive through summer heat with consistent moisture. Pinch flower spikes weekly to extend leaf production.

continental.notes: Direct sun and warm summer nights produce maximum basil; succession-sow every 3 weeks for continuous harvest. Harvest before first cool fall night.

arid.notes: Productive in arid heat with drip irrigation; mulch heavily to retain moisture. 30% shade cloth above 100F preserves leaf quality.

mediterranean.notes: The defining basil climate; long warm summers and limited foliar disease. Drip-irrigate consistently; pinch flowers to extend harvest.

entries.notes: Purple stems, narrow leaves, strong anise-licorice aroma. The defining basil for pho, larb, and Thai stir-fry. Less prone to bolting in heat than Genovese.
entries.source: UF/IFAS basil variety trial; Cornell vegetable variety database

humid_subtropical.notes: Heat-tolerant Thai basil thrives in Florida summers; the defining Southeast herb-garden basil. More bolt-resistant than Genovese in humid heat.

mediterranean.notes: Productive in long warm Mediterranean summers; bolt-resistant once established. Drip-irrigate consistently.

maritime.notes: Marginal in cool maritime; greenhouse-only for reliable production. Choose Genovese basil instead for outdoor culture.

continental.notes: Direct sun and warm nights produce maximum Thai basil; harvest before first cool fall night. Stronger anise-licorice aroma in warm climates.

arid.notes: Productive in arid heat with consistent drip irrigation; tolerates desert heat better than Genovese. 30% shade cloth above 100F preserves leaf quality.

entries.notes: Native Seeds/SEARCH heirloom lemon basil; large leaves, bright citrus-lemon flavor. The strongest of the lemon basils for cooking and tea.
entries.source: Native Seeds/SEARCH catalog; New Mexico State University basil notes

entries.notes: AAS 1962 winner; deep purple leaves, classic Genovese-style flavor. Use fresh leaves to make pink basil vinegar.
Mexican cultivar with high methyl cinnamate; sweet warm cinnamon-clove aroma. Beautiful pink flower spikes attract pollinators.
entries.source: UF/IFAS basil variety trial; ATTRA herb production guide

entries.notes: Compact 10 to 12 inch globe-shaped basil; small leaves, spicy traditional flavor. Excellent ornamental hedge in herb gardens; perfect for containers.
entries.source: Cornell vegetable variety database; UF/IFAS basil variety trial

windows.notes: Cutting-propagated; sterile hybrid does not seed

entries.notes: Sterile interspecific hybrid; large 3 ft mounding plant with camphor-purple-basil aroma. The pollinator-favorite basil; flowers nonstop through frost. Cutting-grown only.
entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension perennial basil notes; ATTRA herb production guide

entries.notes: Bright green broader-leaf tulsi strain; clove-like aroma, milder than Krishna. Sacred plant of Hindu households; daily medicinal tea use. Annual outside zone 10.
entries.source: United Plant Savers; Strictly Medicinal Seeds catalog

windows.notes: Plant in a bottomless container or buried bed-edging to limit spread

entries.notes: The Derby-day mint julep cultivar; large soft crinkly leaves, sweet pure spearmint aroma. Vigorous spreader, mulch heavily.
entries.source: University of Kentucky Cooperative Extension; ATTRA herb production guide

entries.notes: Cuban hierbabuena cultivar; mild sweet musky mint flavor without sharp menthol bite. The traditional Cuban mojito mint. Cutting-propagated.
entries.source: Strictly Medicinal Seeds catalog; ATTRA herb production guide

entries.notes: Bergamot-type peppermint cultivar with bright grapefruit-citrus aroma; rounded slightly fuzzy leaves. Excellent for iced tea and infused water.
entries.source: Mountain Valley Growers catalog; ATTRA herb production guide

windows.notes: Likes part shade and moist soil; struggles in dry heat

entries.notes: Tiny prostrate ground cover mint; releases peppermint aroma when stepped on. Used between flagstones in herb paths. The traditional creme de menthe flavoring.
entries.source: Royal Horticultural Society ground-cover plant database; Cornell Cooperative Extension

mediterranean.notes: Premier Mediterranean climate ground cover; thrives in dappled shade and consistent moisture. The defining peppermint-aroma path mint.

maritime.notes: Productive in cool wet Pacific NW; choose part shade and moist soil. Spreads aggressively; contain to designated path or wall edges.

continental.notes: Cold-tender in Zone 5 and colder; container culture overwintered indoors. Reliable perennial in Zone 6 plus with mulch.

arid.notes: Marginal in dry desert SW; drip-irrigate consistently and provide afternoon shade. Choose a moister microclimate (north side of building).

entries.notes: Variegated apple-mint cultivar; cream-edged green leaves with mild fruity pineapple-mint aroma. Less aggressive than spearmint, good ornamental container herb.
entries.source: Royal Horticultural Society plant database; ATTRA herb production guide

entries.notes: The true culinary oregano (high carvacrol); intense pungent aroma, white flowers, fuzzy gray-green leaves. Drought tolerant; the Mediterranean cooking herb.
entries.source: Royal Horticultural Society plant database; UF/IFAS oregano cultivar trial

entries.notes: Selected high-carvacrol oregano cultivar; strong spicy bite. Excellent for pizza, marinara, dried herb blends.
entries.source: UF/IFAS oregano cultivar trial; Mountain Valley Growers catalog

entries.notes: Not a true oregano; succulent Plectranthus from the Old World tropics. Thick fuzzy leaves with strong oregano-thyme-camphor flavor. Annual outside zone 10 plus.
entries.source: UF/IFAS tropical herbs guide; Strictly Medicinal Seeds catalog

entries.notes: The traditional culinary thyme; narrow gray-green leaves, sweet pure thyme aroma. The defining herb of bouquet garni and Provencal cooking.
entries.source: Royal Horticultural Society plant database; UF/IFAS thyme cultivar trial

entries.notes: Variegated culinary thyme; silver-edged green leaves on woody stems. Same flavor as French thyme with ornamental value.
Upright thyme cultivar with distinctive orange-pine aroma; the orange-flavored thyme for marmalades and Middle Eastern cooking.
entries.source: Royal Horticultural Society plant database; Mountain Valley Growers catalog

entries.notes: Corsican prostrate ground-cover thyme; high carvone gives caraway aroma. The traditional rubbing herb for Corsican baron-of-beef.
entries.source: Royal Horticultural Society plant database; ATTRA herb production guide

entries.notes: German selection; broader rounded leaves, rarely flowers (more leaf production). The premier culinary sage cultivar.
entries.source: Royal Horticultural Society plant database; Mountain Valley Growers catalog

mediterranean.notes: Premier Mediterranean climate sage; the defining culinary cultivar. Thrives in long warm dry summers with minimal irrigation.

arid.notes: Drought-tolerant; productive in desert SW xeriscape gardens. Minimal irrigation after establishment.

continental.notes: Cold-hardy to Zone 4; the defining hardy culinary sage. Mulch lightly in winter; well-drained soil mandatory.

humid_subtropical.notes: Marginal in deep South humidity; root rot pressure on heavy clay soils. Choose raised bed or container culture for reliable longevity.

entries.notes: Variegated gold-and-green leaved culinary sage; full sage flavor with ornamental value. Slightly less hardy than common sage.
Variegated culinary sage with cream, purple, and green leaves. Full culinary use; striking ornamental.
Biennial Mediterranean sage grown for essential-oil and traditional medicinal use. Large showy bracted flower spikes in year two; harvest fresh herb just before flower bracts open.
entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension herb production guide; United Plant Savers

entries.notes: Mexican tender perennial; fragrant pineapple-scented leaves, brilliant red tubular flowers in fall. Hummingbird magnet; annual outside zone 8.
entries.source: UF/IFAS Salvia variety notes; Native Seeds/SEARCH

entries.notes: Upright vigorous Italian rosemary; broad leaves, deep blue flowers, robust culinary flavor. Excellent for Mediterranean kitchens. Not cold-hardy below zone 8.
entries.source: UC ANR; Royal Horticultural Society plant database

entries.notes: Hardy Madeline Hill TX selection; upright habit, classic flavor, reliable to zone 6. The mid-South cold-hardy rosemary.
entries.source: Texas A&M AgriLife Extension; Mountain Valley Growers catalog

entries.notes: Upright cultivar bred for long straight stems; perfect for stripping into rosemary skewers. Excellent flavor.
entries.source: Mountain Valley Growers catalog; UC ANR

windows.notes: Cutting or division propagated only; does not come true from seed

entries.notes: True French culinary tarragon; sweet anise flavor. The defining herb of bearnaise sauce. Sterile sport, propagated only from cuttings or division. Distinct from Russian tarragon (seed-grown, flavorless).
entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension; Mountain Valley Growers catalog

entries.notes: Mexican marigold-tarragon; not a true tarragon but offers identical anise flavor with heat tolerance French tarragon lacks. Yellow flowers attract pollinators.

windows.notes: Slow germinator; presoak seed 24h

entries.notes: Flat-leaf parsley with extra-large dark green leaves; stronger flavor than curly. The defining parsley of tabbouleh and gremolata.
entries.source: Cornell vegetable variety database; Johnny's Selected Seeds notes

windows.notes: Needs deep loose soil for straight roots

entries.notes: Central European root parsley; parsnip-shaped white root with celery-parsley flavor. Used in soups and stews; the leaves edible but coarse.
entries.source: Seed Savers Exchange; Cornell vegetable variety database

windows.notes: Bolts above 75 F; succession-sow every 2 weeks

entries.notes: The slow-bolt commercial cilantro standard; flat parsley-like leaves, classic cilantro flavor. Longer leaf-harvest window than older strains.
entries.source: Johnny's Selected Seeds variety notes; Cornell vegetable variety database

maritime.notes: Premier cool maritime herb; productive April through October. Bolt-slow long-cool springs extend harvest 8 to 10 weeks.

humid_subtropical.notes: Cool-season only; sow Sep through Mar for fall through spring harvest. Bolts within 3 weeks past 75F.

continental.notes: Succession-sow every 2 weeks until heat hits 75F. Resume in August for fall harvest through first hard freeze.

arid.notes: Sow in late winter and again in fall; skip summer. 30% shade cloth above 85F extends harvest by 2 weeks.

mediterranean.notes: Sow Sep through Mar for cool-season harvest; the traditional Mediterranean herb-garden window. Skip the heat-bolt summer.

entries.notes: Slowest-bolting cilantro available; 3 to 4 weeks longer cut window than Santo. Dense low rosette habit.
entries.source: Johnny's Selected Seeds variety notes; OSU Extension herb variety trial

entries.notes: Mexican summer-cilantro substitute; not in the parsley family, completely heat-tolerant. Stronger soapy-cilantro flavor; one leaf flavors a whole pot of beans.
entries.source: Native Seeds/SEARCH; UF/IFAS tropical herbs notes

humid_subtropical.notes: Heat-tolerant Mexican cilantro substitute; productive through Florida summers where Santo bolts. The defining summer cilantro for the deep South.

arid.notes: Drought-tolerant Sonoran herb; productive in desert SW heat. The traditional Sonoran summer cilantro.

continental.notes: Direct sow after last frost; harvest leaves June through frost. Stronger cilantro flavor than Santo; one leaf flavors a pot of beans.

maritime.notes: Marginal in cool maritime; needs warm summer to produce full leaf flavor. Choose Santo for outdoor cilantro in cool zones.

mediterranean.notes: Productive in long warm Mediterranean summers; bolt-resistant. Drip-irrigate consistently.

entries.notes: Caribbean and Southeast Asian perennial herb; spiny-edged dark green rosette leaves, intensely cilantro-flavored. The recao of Puerto Rican sofrito.
entries.source: UF/IFAS Tropical Research and Education Center; UPR Mayaguez specialty crops

entries.notes: Selected garlic chives cultivar with broader flatter leaves; cleaner mild garlic-onion flavor. Slower to seed and spread than wild type.
entries.source: Bejo Seeds variety notes; Cornell vegetable variety database

entries.notes: Compact English lavender bred by Gertrude Jekyll; pale lavender flowers, classic culinary lavender flavor. The lavender for shortbread and bee balm tea.
entries.source: Royal Horticultural Society plant database; OSU Extension lavender variety trial

entries.notes: Deep violet-blue compact English lavender; the darkest culinary lavender in common circulation. Excellent dried flower color.
entries.source: Royal Horticultural Society plant database; OSU Extension lavender variety trial

entries.notes: French Provence-type lavandin; long stems, large flower heads, high essential-oil yield. The defining commercial cut and dried lavender.
entries.source: OSU Extension lavender variety trial; Royal Horticultural Society plant database

entries.notes: The standard commercial lavandin worldwide; highest essential-oil yield per acre. Large vigorous plants, deep purple-blue flowers.
entries.source: OSU Extension lavender variety trial; USDA lavender cultivar notes

mediterranean.notes: Premier commercial lavandin worldwide; the defining French Provence cultivar. Thrives in long warm dry summers.

arid.notes: Drought-tolerant; productive in desert SW with minimal irrigation. Excellent xeriscape herb for sunny inland gardens.

continental.notes: Cold-hardy to Zone 5; mulch lightly in winter. Choose well-drained soil; wet feet kill lavandin faster than cold.

maritime.notes: Productive in Pacific NW with well-drained raised beds; wet winter soils cause root rot. Excellent on slope or with gravel mulch.

humid_subtropical.notes: Marginal in deep South humidity; Phytophthora root rot pressure heavy. Choose Provence or Grosso in raised bed; expect 3 to 5 year lifespan.

entries.notes: Swiss bolt-resistant bulbing fennel; tight white bulbs, mild anise flavor. The standard market Florence fennel.
entries.source: Cornell vegetable variety database; Johnny's Selected Seeds notes

maritime.notes: Cool maritime summers reliably produce tight Florence bulbs; transplant May through Aug. Slug pressure heavy on young rosettes; iron phosphate baits.

mediterranean.notes: Sow Sep through Nov for winter and spring harvest; the traditional Italian fennel window. Skip the heat-bolt summer crop.

continental.notes: Transplant after last frost; harvest before Aug heat triggers premature bolting. Day-neutral cultivars (Zefa Fino, Orion) bolt-resist into July.

humid_subtropical.notes: Cool-season only; sow Sep through Nov for winter and spring harvest. Spring summer crop fails reliably to heat-bolt past 80F.

arid.notes: Sow in fall and overwinter under row cover; harvest Feb through April. Bolts past 80F; skip late spring and summer.

entries.notes: Ornamental herb fennel; bronze-purple feathery foliage, yellow umbels in late summer. Black swallowtail butterfly host plant. Leaves and seed harvestable.
entries.source: Royal Horticultural Society plant database; Cornell ornamental herb notes

entries.notes: Japanese / Korean culinary mint-family herb; ruffled red-purple leaves with cinnamon-basil-mint aroma. The defining herb of umeboshi pickling and Korean kkaennip.
entries.source: Kitazawa Seed Company variety notes; Strictly Medicinal Seeds catalog

entries.notes: Bright green serrated-leaf shiso; mint-basil-anise aroma. The defining herb of sashimi presentation and tempura aojiso.
entries.source: Kitazawa Seed Company variety notes; Cornell vegetable variety database

windows.notes: Division-propagated; one stalk roots in a glass of water

entries.notes: Cochin lemongrass; finer stems, stronger citral concentration than West Indian. The traditional Ayurvedic lemongrass for tea and essential oil. Annual outside zone 9.
entries.source: UF/IFAS Tropical Research and Education Center; ATTRA herb production guide

entries.notes: Peruvian black mint; tall (4 ft) marigold-family herb with strong basil-mint-tarragon aroma. The defining herb of huancaina sauce and pachamanca. Also nematode-suppressive in the soil.
entries.source: International Potato Center (CIP) Andean herb collection; Native Seeds/SEARCH

entries.notes: Paraguayan candy-sweet leaf herb; steviol glycosides 200x sweeter than sugar. Tender perennial outside zone 9. Pinch flowers to keep leaf production going.
entries.source: UF/IFAS specialty herbs notes; Strictly Medicinal Seeds catalog

windows.notes: Cutting-propagated; roots in water

entries.notes: Tropical perennial herb of the knotweed family; spicy cilantro-lemon-peppery flavor. The defining herb of pho and Vietnamese spring rolls. Tender outside zone 9.
entries.source: UF/IFAS Tropical Research and Education Center; Kitazawa Seed Company

entries.notes: Perennial Mediterranean savory; needle-like leaves with strong thyme-peppermint aroma. Stronger flavor than summer savory; the traditional bean-cooking herb.
entries.source: Royal Horticultural Society plant database; Cornell Cooperative Extension

entries.notes: Annual savory; bushy 12 to 18 inch plant, milder than winter savory. The traditional German green-bean herb (Bohnenkraut).
entries.source: Cornell vegetable variety database; Mountain Valley Growers catalog

entries.notes: Sterile interspecific Agastache hybrid; lavender-blue flower spikes June through frost, anise-licorice aroma. Premier perennial pollinator plant.
entries.source: Chicago Botanic Garden Agastache evaluation; OSU pollinator plant trials

entries.notes: Deep red full-length stalks; sweeter than Victoria, premier color for pie and compote. Vigorous, productive.
entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension; OSU rhubarb variety notes

entries.notes: Tropical rhizome; sister to ginger. Bright orange interior, peppery-earthy flavor, the defining Indian curry spice. 9 to 10 month growing season in warm zones.
entries.source: UF/IFAS Extension specialty crops; UH Manoa Cooperative Extension turmeric notes

entries.notes: Southeast Asian Zingiberaceae rhizome; pungent piney-citrus aroma, the defining root of Thai tom kha and Indonesian gado-gado. Grow in containers north of zone 9.
entries.source: UF/IFAS Tropical Research and Education Center; UH Manoa Cooperative Extension

entries.notes: South Indian small tree; fresh-tasting aromatic leaves are the defining tempering herb of South Indian and Sri Lankan cuisine. Container-grown north of zone 10.
entries.source: UF/IFAS Tropical Research and Education Center; UH Manoa Cooperative Extension

entries.notes: Eastern North American sumac; tart red berry clusters dried and ground for the Middle Eastern spice. Confusable with poison sumac (white berries); fruit color distinguishes them.
entries.source: USDA-NRCS Plant Materials Program; United Plant Savers

entries.notes: Cold-hardy native sumac; dried red drupes ground for spice or steeped into sumac-ade. The North American spice that substitutes for Middle Eastern Rhus coriaria.
entries.source: USDA-NRCS Plant Materials Program; Cornell Cooperative Extension native foods

windows.notes: Requires shaded, cool, running-water stream-bed conditions for true sawa-style production; field production possible in cool maritime climates

entries.notes: Japanese stream herb; pungent rhizome takes 18 to 24 months. Notoriously finicky: needs cool 50 to 60 F shade and constant moisture. Field production possible in coastal PNW, Vancouver Island, parts of NC mountains.
entries.source: NC State University wasabi production research; University of British Columbia Pacific Field Production studies

windows.notes: Frost-tender top; harvest tubers after first frost knocks back foliage

entries.notes: Andean tuber with sweet pear-water-chestnut flavor; inulin-sweetened, low glycemic. 6 to 10 lb yield per plant in long warm seasons. Treat as tender perennial.
entries.source: International Potato Center (CIP) Andean roots and tubers collection; OSU Extension specialty crops

windows.notes: Short-day tuber initiation: tubers form only as fall day length shortens below 12 hours

entries.notes: Andean oxalis tuber; tangy-sweet lemon-flavored tubers, harvest after first frost. Day-length sensitive: needs frost-free fall after equinox to bulk up. Pink, yellow, white strains exist.
entries.source: International Potato Center (CIP) Andean roots and tubers collection; Cornell Tropical Crops

entries.notes: Chinese stem-lettuce; bolting variety grown for the thick juicy stem (peeled, sliced raw or stir-fried). Crisp celery-asparagus-cucumber flavor. The defining vegetable of woasun.
entries.source: Kitazawa Seed Company variety notes; Cornell vegetable variety database

windows.notes: Wait for warm soil; succession sow every 2 weeks.

entries.notes: Classic late-maturing white (su) sweet corn introduced 1955. Large 8 to 9 inch ears, 14 to 16 rows of tender kernels. Heat tolerant Southern favorite, strong stalks, good husk coverage.
entries.source: USDA-ARS GRIN-Global accession data; Clemson Cooperative Extension HGIC 1306 sweet corn cultivars.

windows.notes: Isolate from other corn types to keep bicolor true.

entries.notes: Bicolor sugary (su) hybrid with yellow and white kernels in equal mix. 7 to 8 inch ears, balanced sweetness, fresh-eating standard for mid-season harvest.
entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension Vegetable Varieties for Gardeners; Johnny's Selected Seeds cultivar descriptions.

windows.notes: Sugary-enhanced (se) type; sweetness holds 3 to 4 days after picking.

entries.notes: Yellow sugary-enhanced (se) hybrid known for reliable performance and rich corn flavor. 8 inch ears with 16 to 18 rows; tolerant of cool spring soils relative to other se types.
entries.source: Penn State Extension Vegetable Variety Guide; Crookham Company breeder notes.

entries.notes: Bicolor sugary-enhanced (se) hybrid, 8 inch ears, very tender pericarp. Widely planted for farm stand and home garden mid-season slot.
entries.source: University of Massachusetts Extension Vegetable Program; Harris Seeds variety database.

entries.notes: Yellow sugary-enhanced (se) hybrid with large 9 inch ears and excellent tip fill. Strong stalks resist lodging; popular for late summer fresh market.
entries.source: Ohio State University Extension Bulletin 672 Sweet Corn Production; Seminis variety profile.

windows.notes: Earlier su/se transition; good first planting in short-season gardens.

entries.notes: Bicolor sugary-enhanced (se) early hybrid bred for short seasons. 7 inch ears, productive on 5 to 6 foot stalks. Often the first sweet corn ready in Northern markets.
entries.source: University of Minnesota Extension Yard and Garden News; Johnny's Selected Seeds cultivar database.

windows.notes: Synergistic type; isolate 25 feet or 14 days from other sweet corn.

entries.notes: Japanese-bred synergistic (sy) series combining su, se, and sh2 kernels for exceptional tenderness and sweetness. Available in bicolor and yellow strains; premium fresh-market type.
entries.source: University of Illinois Extension Hort Answers; Mirai brand cultivar literature (Sakata Seed America).

entries.notes: Yellow sugary (su) processing and fresh-market standard. 8 to 9 inch ears with 16 to 20 rows of golden kernels; widely planted across canning belt since 1960s.
entries.source: Wisconsin Vegetable Crop Updates UW-Madison Extension; Rogers (Syngenta) variety archive.

windows.notes: sh2 supersweet needs warm soil (70F) and strict isolation from other corn.

entries.notes: University of Illinois supersweet (sh2) yellow corn, released 1970s. High sugar, slow conversion to starch giving long shelf life. Requires warm soils; do not plant near su or se types.
entries.source: University of Illinois Department of Crop Sciences sweet corn breeding history; Ag Alumni Seed variety notes.

windows.notes: Long season; start at first reliable warm soil for Northern gardens.

entries.notes: Heirloom white sugary (su) introduced 1848 by Nathan Stowell of New Jersey. 8 to 9 inch ears, holds milk stage unusually long, hence the Evergreen name. 8 foot stalks.
entries.source: Seed Savers Exchange heirloom collection notes; USDA-ARS GRIN PI accession records.

windows.notes: Tolerates cooler soils than modern hybrids.

entries.notes: Heirloom yellow sugary (su) introduced 1902 by Burpee. 6 to 7 inch ears on 5 to 6 foot stalks; the variety that made yellow corn popular in America. Open-pollinated, seed-saving friendly.
entries.source: Seed Savers Exchange Garden Seed Inventory; W. Atlee Burpee Company catalog archives.

entries.notes: Yellow synergistic (sy) hybrid, 2001 All-America Selections winner. 8 to 9 inch ears with 16 to 18 rows; balanced sweetness and creamy texture, vigorous emergence.
entries.source: All-America Selections 2001 vegetable winners; Crookham Company variety descriptions.

windows.notes: Cold-tolerant; can be planted earlier than sweet corn.

entries.notes: Open-pollinated short-season flint developed by Dave Christensen in Montana over 30 years, combining 70+ Native American and homesteader strains. 5 to 6 foot stalks, multicolored kernels for flour and meal.
entries.source: Seeds Trust catalog and Dave Christensen breeding documentation; Organic Seed Alliance grower trials.

entries.notes: Translucent jewel-toned flint developed by Carl Barnes (Cherokee) from Pawnee and Osage parent lines. Ornamental and grindable; 3 to 4 inch ears with iridescent kernels in every color.
entries.source: Native Seeds/SEARCH variety notes; Greg Schoen and Carl Barnes breeding records.

entries.notes: Heirloom flint from the Mandan people of the upper Missouri River. Multicolored 7 inch ears on 5 foot stalks; cold tolerant and drought tolerant, prized for cornmeal and parching.
entries.source: Seed Savers Exchange and USDA-ARS GRIN North Dakota landrace records.

entries.notes: Vermont heirloom flint from Roy Fair of Calais, descended from Abenaki seed. 8 inch ears with maroon and yellow kernels; excellent for jonnycake meal and polenta in cold climates.
entries.source: Vermont Land Trust seed library; High Mowing Organic Seeds variety notes.

windows.notes: Drought tolerant; deep plant 2 to 4 inches in dry conditions.

entries.notes: Ancient Hopi flour corn from the American Southwest. Deep blue kernels on 8 to 10 inch ears; soft starch grinds into traditional piki bread flour. Drought adapted, tolerates poor soils.
entries.source: Native Seeds/SEARCH Hopi traditional varieties collection; USDA-ARS GRIN southwestern landrace records.

entries.notes: Rio Grande Pueblo blue flour corn, slightly larger and later than Hopi Blue. Used for atole, chaquegue, and blue cornbread. Adapted to high desert and intermountain conditions.
entries.source: Native Seeds/SEARCH variety database; New Mexico State University Extension publication H-232.

entries.notes: Northern Plains flour corn from the Mandan people, rose-pink kernels on 6 to 7 inch ears. Short season and cold tolerant; soft starch ideal for traditional cornmeal preparations.
entries.source: Seed Savers Exchange; North Dakota State University historical landrace inventory.

windows.notes: Tolerates cooler soil than most snap beans; succession every 2 weeks.

entries.notes: 1965 USDA release, the most reliable cool-soil germinator among snap beans. 5 to 6 inch round green pods, heavy yields, disease resistance to BCMV and powdery mildew.
entries.source: USDA-ARS variety release notes; Cornell Cooperative Extension Vegetable Varieties for Gardeners.

entries.notes: 1949 introduction, flat-round 6 to 7 inch medium-green pods. Tolerant of heat and cool soils; resistant to common bean mosaic and powdery mildew. Reliable Southern shipper.
entries.source: Clemson Cooperative Extension HGIC 1313 snap bean varieties; USDA-ARS GRIN accession data.

entries.notes: Heat tolerant bush snap bean with dark green 6 to 7 inch slender pods held high on the plant. Resistant to brown spot, mosaic, and rust; long picking window with continued set.
entries.source: North Carolina State University Extension AG-758 bean varieties; Seedway commercial seed catalog descriptions.

entries.notes: 1925 USDA-released stringless round-podded bean, 6 inch dark green pods. Heavy concentrated set, vigorous in cool conditions; classic home-garden and freezer bean.
entries.source: USDA-ARS variety release records; University of Maine Cooperative Extension bulletin 2057.

entries.notes: Dutch heirloom dual-purpose snap and shell bean. Cream-yellow 7 inch pods streaked purple (color fades on cooking); buttery flavor fresh or as a horticultural bean.
entries.source: Seed Savers Exchange Garden Seed Inventory; Organic Seed Alliance variety notes.

windows.notes: Germinates in cooler soil than green beans; purple pods easy to spot.

entries.notes: Purple-podded bush bean with 5 to 6 inch slightly curved pods that turn green when cooked. Excellent germination in cool soil, attracts fewer bean beetles than green types.
entries.source: University of Vermont Extension vegetable variety trials; Johnny's Selected Seeds variety database.

entries.commonName: Pencil Pod Yellow Wax Bush Bean
entries.notes: 1900 heirloom wax bean from Oscar Will and Sons. Stringless 6 inch buttery-yellow pencil-thin pods, productive over a long window. Classic canning and pickling bean.
entries.source: Seed Savers Exchange heirloom collection notes; USDA-ARS GRIN historical varieties.

entries.notes: Improved purple bush bean with 5 inch glossy purple pods that turn green when cooked. Vigorous emergence in cool soil, compact upright habit, BCMV resistant.
entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension Vegetable Varieties for Gardeners; Stokes Seeds variety descriptions.

entries.notes: Bush form of Italian flat Romano-type bean, 4 to 5 inch wide flat green pods, meaty texture, rich flavor. AAS-recommended for home gardens; freezes very well.
entries.source: All-America Selections variety records; Cornell Cooperative Extension trial notes.

windows.notes: Pick small, 3 to 4 inches, for true filet quality; daily harvest at peak.

entries.notes: French filet bean producing very slender, straight 4 inch dark green pods. Concentrated set ideal for high-end fresh market; resistant to anthracnose and halo blight.
entries.source: Johnny's Selected Seeds variety database; Vilmorin breeder cultivar notes.

entries.notes: French heirloom filet bean producing exceptionally long 7 to 8 inch straight slender pods held high on the plant for easy picking. Stays tender at large size if picked promptly.
entries.source: Organic Seed Alliance variety trials; Vilmorin and Andrieux historical seed records.

entries.notes: 1948 AAS winner, golden-yellow 6 inch oval pods on sturdy 18 inch plants. Heat tolerant, BCMV resistant, stringless even at maturity; reliable canning bean across the South.
entries.source: All-America Selections 1948 winner records; USDA-ARS variety release documentation.

windows.notes: Provide 6 to 8 foot trellis or pole; pick young for snap, mature for shell.

entries.notes: Pre-1864 American heirloom, 7 to 9 inch silvery-green sickle-shaped pods, rich beany flavor, productive for many weeks. Excellent dual-purpose snap and shell bean.
entries.source: Seed Savers Exchange heirloom records; USDA-ARS GRIN accession history.

entries.notes: Oregon-bred (FM-1) pole strain of the famous canning bean. 6 to 7 inch round dark green pods, white seeds, tender flavor; vigorous to 7 feet with steady production.
entries.source: Oregon State University Extension vegetable variety guide; USDA-ARS GRIN cultivar records.

windows.notes: Drought and heat tolerant; well suited to Southern summers.

entries.notes: Southern heirloom with 7 to 8 inch green pods streaked purple, twisting vines resembling snakes. Drought tolerant, sets pods even in hot weather; buff seeds are a fine dry bean.
entries.source: North Carolina State University Extension AG-758; Sand Hill Preservation Center catalog notes.

entries.commonName: Cherokee Trail of Tears Pole Bean
entries.notes: Cherokee heirloom carried over the Trail of Tears in 1838. Purple-streaked snap pods or shiny black dry seeds; vigorous 8 foot vines, tolerates heat and drought.
entries.source: Seed Savers Exchange Cherokee Nation collaborative seed bank records; SSE Garden Seed Inventory.

windows.notes: Needs sustained heat; not a true Phaseolus bean, related to cowpeas.

entries.notes: Asian yardlong cowpea producing 18 to 36 inch slender pods on 10 foot vines. Heat loving, drought tolerant, pest resistant; pick young for tender stir-fry quality.
entries.source: University of Florida IFAS Extension HS-616 Asian vegetables; AVRDC World Vegetable Center cultivar notes.

entries.commonName: Grandma Nellie's Yellow Mushroom Pole Bean
entries.notes: Russian-American heirloom from Nellie Klein, golden 6 inch flat pods with distinctive mushroom-like flavor when shelled. Productive over a long season; rare in commerce.
entries.source: Seed Savers Exchange member-listed heirloom records; Sand Hill Preservation Center bean catalog.

entries.notes: French filet pole bean producing 11 inch straight dark green pods that stay stringless and tender at large size. Heavy yields, BCMV resistant, top market-garden choice.
entries.source: Johnny's Selected Seeds variety database; High Mowing Organic Seeds variety trials.

entries.commonName: Mountaineer White Half Runner Pole Bean

windows.notes: Half-runner; 3 to 4 foot short trellis or brush is sufficient.

entries.notes: Appalachian half-runner heirloom with 4 inch round green pods on 3 to 4 foot vines. BCMV resistant strain of the traditional Southern home-canning bean; old-fashioned beany taste.
entries.source: West Virginia University Extension publication 21-A; USDA-ARS GRIN heirloom bean records.

entries.notes: Triple-purpose bean: tender snap pods at 60 days, fresh shell at 70 days, dry brown-and-white pinto-like seeds at 80 days. Vigorous, productive, holds pattern in dry seed.
entries.source: Seed Savers Exchange variety notes; Fedco Seeds cultivar descriptions.

windows.notes: Germinates well in cool soil; pods turn green on cooking.

entries.notes: Henry Fields 1930s introduction, 6 to 7 inch glossy deep-purple stringless pods on vigorous 8 foot vines. Productive in cool wet summers; lavender flowers attractive in the garden.
entries.source: Seed Savers Exchange heirloom collection; USDA-ARS GRIN cultivar history.

entries.notes: French pole filet bean, 7 to 9 inch slender straight pods, stringless and tender. Very early for a pole type; reliable in cool maritime climates and Northern gardens.
entries.source: Johnny's Selected Seeds variety database; Vilmorin breeder notes.

entries.notes: Traditional Italian flat Romano with 7 inch wide green pods, meaty texture, very full bean flavor when cooked. Productive over many weeks; tolerates summer heat well.
entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension Vegetable Varieties for Gardeners; Franchi Seeds of Italy catalog notes.

windows.notes: Drought-adapted; do not overwater. Best in arid climates.

entries.notes: Sonoran Desert domesticated bean of the Tohono Oodham, distinct species from common bean. Small seeds in white, brown, or black; extreme drought and heat tolerance, low water needs.
entries.source: Native Seeds/SEARCH collection records; University of Arizona Cooperative Extension AZ1572.

windows.notes: Cure on the vine until pods rattle; thresh in dry weather.

entries.notes: Classic Southwestern dry bean with mottled tan-and-brown seeds. Bush habit, productive in semi-arid conditions; the staple bean of Mexican and Tex-Mex cuisine.
entries.source: Colorado State University Extension fact sheet 0.305; USDA-ARS dry bean breeding records.

entries.notes: Italian-American heirloom (Borlotti type) with cream seeds splashed cranberry-red. Bush habit, rich chestnut flavor when cooked; excellent fresh shell or dried.
