entries.source: Seed Savers Exchange variety notes; Cornell Cooperative Extension dry bean trials.

entries.notes: New England heirloom with bold white-and-maroon spotted kidney seeds. Bush habit, holds shape on cooking, traditional baked bean of Maine and the Maritimes.
entries.source: Seed Savers Exchange heirloom records; University of Maine Cooperative Extension dry bean variety notes.

entries.notes: Small glossy black-seeded dry bean of Latin American origin. Bush habit, productive, holds shape after long cooking; staple of Caribbean, Cuban, and Mexican cuisines.
entries.source: Michigan State University Extension dry bean variety trials; USDA-ARS GRIN cultivar records.

entries.notes: Pre-1700s New England heirloom, larger and rounder than common cranberry beans. Adapted to short Northern seasons; soft skin and creamy texture when baked.
entries.source: Seed Savers Exchange heirloom collection; Vermont Bean Seed Co. historical records.

windows.notes: Excellent fresh shell stage; harvest before pods fully dry for shelling.

entries.notes: Tierra del Fuego heirloom shell-and-dry bean. Cream pods flamed with red and round cream-red mottled seeds; nutty flavor fresh, holds shape dried.
entries.source: Seed Savers Exchange Garden Seed Inventory; Fedco Seeds variety descriptions.

entries.notes: Hidatsa Nation heirloom from the upper Missouri River, small dark red seeds, short-season half-runner habit. Cold-tolerant Northern Plains adaptation; rich earthy flavor.
entries.source: Native Seeds/SEARCH and Seed Savers Exchange Hidatsa collection notes; USDA-ARS GRIN landrace records.

entries.notes: Striking bicolor seeds, half white and half black with contrasting eye, resembling the yin-yang symbol. Bush habit, decent producer, mild flavor good for soups and chili.
entries.source: Seed Savers Exchange variety notes; Fedco Seeds and Johnny's Selected Seeds catalog descriptions.

entries.notes: North Carolina coastal heirloom said to have arrived on the Mayflower. Tan with maroon eye, half-runner habit, good keeper; mellow flavor, holds shape when cooked.
entries.source: Seed Savers Exchange heirloom collection; North Carolina State University Extension AG-758 heritage beans.

windows.notes: Limas need warm soil; do not plant early or seeds rot.

entries.notes: 1945 AAS winner, bush habit, large pale-green seeds in 4 inch pods. Heat tolerant, sets pods in hot weather where other limas fail; the standard home-garden bush lima.
entries.source: All-America Selections 1945 winner records; W. Atlee Burpee Company variety history.

entries.commonName: King of the Garden Lima Bean

windows.notes: Pole type; needs 8 to 10 foot trellis and a long warm season.

entries.notes: 1883 American heirloom pole lima with large 6 inch pods containing 4 to 6 large flat white seeds. Vigorous climber, productive for many weeks in long warm summers.
entries.source: Seed Savers Exchange heirloom records; USDA-ARS GRIN cultivar history.

entries.notes: 1889 baby lima introduced by Peter Henderson, the original American bush baby lima. Small white seeds in 3 inch pods, productive, the parent of most modern bush limas.
entries.source: USDA-ARS GRIN historical cultivar records; Seed Savers Exchange heirloom collection.

entries.notes: Heirloom pole lima with large flat white seeds splashed maroon-red. Chestnut-like flavor, productive in hot humid summers, beautiful in the bowl; needs a long season.
entries.source: Seed Savers Exchange heirloom records; Southern Exposure Seed Exchange catalog notes.

windows.notes: Tolerates cool soil better than common beans; brilliant red flowers attract hummingbirds.

entries.notes: Classic English-garden Phaseolus coccineus with brilliant scarlet flowers. 8 to 12 inch fuzzy green pods are edible young, mature seeds purple-and-black mottled; perennial in zone 7+.
entries.source: Royal Horticultural Society plant database; Cornell Cooperative Extension Vegetable Varieties for Gardeners.

entries.notes: 1855 English heirloom runner bean with bicolor red-and-white flowers. Edible green pods at 6 to 8 inches; ornamental on a tall trellis or arbor.
entries.source: Royal Horticultural Society heritage variety records; Seed Savers Exchange catalog.

entries.notes: Soft peachy-pink flowered runner bean, less common than scarlet types. Vigorous climber to 10 feet, edible green pods, attractive seeds in lavender and black.
entries.source: Thompson and Morgan variety database; Organic Seed Alliance grower trials.

windows.notes: Plant as soon as soil is workable; tolerates frost as seedlings.
Fall crop in mild-summer regions.

entries.notes: Compact 30 inch vines from Oregon State breeder Jim Baggett. Thick crisp 3 inch pods, powdery mildew resistant, sweet flavor; needs no trellis in home gardens.
entries.source: Oregon State University vegetable breeding program records; Johnny's Selected Seeds variety database.

entries.notes: Improved Sugar Snap descendant on 5 to 6 foot vines. 3 inch crisp sweet pods with strong powdery mildew resistance; heavy yields and slightly earlier than the original.
entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension Vegetable Varieties for Gardeners; Stokes Seeds variety notes.

entries.notes: Hyper-tendril snap pea from breeder Alan Kapuler, bicolor purple-and-pink flowers, green pods edible at flat or filled stages. 6 foot vines self-support via heavy tendrils.
entries.source: Peace Seeds and Peace Seedlings (Alan Kapuler) cultivar documentation; Adaptive Seeds catalog notes.

entries.notes: Pennsylvania Amish heirloom preserved before modern snap peas existed. 6 foot vines, small 2 inch crunchy stringless pods, very sweet; one of the oldest documented snap types.
entries.source: Seed Savers Exchange Amish heirloom collection; Southern Exposure Seed Exchange variety notes.

entries.notes: First stringless snap pea, 24 inch dwarf vines need no trellis. Thick 3 inch crisp pods; productive but slightly less sweet than tall types. Good for container culture.
entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension Vegetable Varieties for Gardeners; Park Seed variety database.

entries.notes: Oregon State University release from Jim Baggett, very large 4 to 5 inch flat pods, 3 foot vines. Resistant to powdery mildew, pea enation virus, and bean yellow mosaic.
entries.source: Oregon State University vegetable breeding program; USDA-ARS GRIN cultivar records.

windows.notes: Trellis 4 to 5 foot vines; pick young while pods are still flat.

entries.notes: 1900 American heirloom snow pea, vigorous 5 foot vines, 4 to 5 inch tender flat pods. Heat tolerant for a snow pea; the long-time market-garden standard.
entries.source: Seed Savers Exchange heirloom records; USDA-ARS GRIN historical cultivar accession.

entries.notes: Indian heirloom with lemon-yellow 3 inch flat pods and bicolor purple flowers. 6 foot vines, productive, ornamental and edible; very rare yellow-podded snow pea.
entries.source: Baker Creek Heirloom Seeds variety records; Seed Savers Exchange Garden Seed Inventory.

entries.notes: Provençal French heirloom with very large 5 to 6 inch flat green pods and bicolor purple flowers. 6 foot vines, slightly sweeter than typical snow peas, lovely in the garden.
entries.source: Vilmorin and Andrieux historical seed catalog; Seed Savers Exchange variety notes.

entries.notes: British shelling pea on 28 inch vines, slim 4 inch pods packed with 8 to 11 sweet peas. Concentrated set, resistant to downy mildew and Fusarium wilt; freezes well.
entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension Vegetable Varieties for Gardeners; Stokes Seeds variety database.

entries.notes: 1908 English heirloom shelling pea, 30 inch vines, 3 to 4 inch curved pods with 7 to 9 sweet peas. Slightly heat tolerant; an old favorite for fresh eating and freezing.
entries.source: Seed Savers Exchange heirloom records; USDA-ARS GRIN cultivar history.

windows.notes: Most heat-tolerant shelling pea; useful for late spring sowings in the South.

entries.notes: 1943 USDA release with notable heat tolerance for a shelling pea. 30 inch vines, 3 inch pods with 7 to 8 peas; extends the pea season into warmer weather.
entries.source: USDA-ARS variety release records; Clemson Cooperative Extension HGIC 1315 pea cultivars.

entries.notes: European-bred large-podded shelling pea, 36 inch vines, 4 to 5 inch pods packed with 10 to 11 deep-green sweet peas. Excellent fresh eating quality and freezing.
entries.source: Johnny's Selected Seeds variety database; Bejo Seeds breeder cultivar notes.

windows.notes: Vines reach 6 to 8 feet; provide sturdy trellis.

entries.notes: 1881 English heirloom shelling pea on towering 6 to 8 foot vines. Large 4 to 5 inch pods with 8 to 10 large sweet peas; productive over a long picking window.
entries.source: Seed Savers Exchange heirloom records; USDA-ARS GRIN historical accession data.

windows.notes: Heat loving; sow after corn.

entries.notes: Classic Southern crowder cowpea with purple pods and cream seeds with pink eye. Bush habit, 6 inch pods held above foliage for easy picking; tolerates heat and poor soils.
entries.source: USDA-ARS Southern Regional Plant Introduction Station records; Clemson Cooperative Extension HGIC 1314 cowpea varieties.

entries.notes: 1965 Mississippi Agricultural Experiment Station release. Silvery-green pods with reddish overlay, buff seeds with brown eye; productive bush habit, easy to shell.
entries.source: Mississippi State University Extension publication 1091; USDA-ARS GRIN release records.

entries.notes: University of California-Riverside release, the standard blackeye pea grown for dry beans and traditional Southern cuisine. White seed with black eye, productive bush habit.
entries.source: University of California ANR Publication 8235 cowpea production; USDA-ARS GRIN variety records.

windows.notes: Start in peat pots; melons resent root disturbance.

entries.notes: French heirloom true cantaloupe (no netting). 2 pound smooth gray-green fruit with deep orange aromatic flesh; harvest at full slip when stem detaches cleanly.
entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension Vegetable Varieties for Gardeners; Vilmorin and Andrieux historical seed catalog.

maritime.notes: Marginal in cool maritime; choose only in long warm summers and use black plastic, row cover, and a south wall. Petit Gris de Rennes is a more reliable alternative.

mediterranean.notes: The defining French cultivar for Mediterranean climates; sets fruit reliably through long dry summers. Train on a low trellis for cleaner fruit.

continental.notes: Direct-sow only after soil hits 70F; full Charentais melon needs 85 days of consistent heat. Black plastic mulch and row cover speed early growth.

humid_subtropical.notes: Plant early to beat July bacterial wilt; train on trellis for airflow. Powdery mildew pressure heavy in late season; switch to PM-resistant cultivar where available.

arid.notes: Excellent producer in arid heat; harvest by morning to retain sugar levels. Mulch heavily and drip-irrigate twice weekly.

entries.notes: Israeli-bred hybrid melon (Galia x reticulatus), heavily netted with pale green aromatic flesh. 3 pound fruit, distinctive perfumed aroma when ripe; harvest at full slip.
entries.source: Volcani Center Israeli melon breeding records; Johnny's Selected Seeds variety database.

windows.notes: Long-season; needs hot dry summer to develop full sweetness.

entries.notes: Spanish toad-skin melon with green mottled rind and crisp pale flesh. 4 to 6 pound oval fruit, stores 1 to 2 months at room temperature, sweet honeydew-style flavor.
entries.source: Spanish seed cooperative variety records; University of California ANR Publication 7218 melon production.

entries.notes: Bright yellow oval winter melon (inodorus type) with crisp pale flesh and mild tangy-sweet flavor. 4 to 5 pound fruit; harvest when fully yellow, no slip needed.
entries.source: University of California ANR Publication 7218; Johnny's Selected Seeds variety notes.

entries.notes: Modern Charentais-type hybrid with deep orange flesh and lightly netted rind. 2 to 3 pound fruit, very aromatic, improved disease resistance over open-pollinated Charentais.
entries.source: Johnny's Selected Seeds variety database; Cornell Cooperative Extension melon trial reports.

entries.notes: 1924 California heirloom muskmelon from Japanese-American grower I. D. Hale. Heavy netting, deep orange flesh, 3 to 4 pound oval fruit; the classic American cantaloupe.
entries.source: Seed Savers Exchange heirloom records; USDA-ARS GRIN cultivar history.

entries.notes: Listed in 1885 Burpee catalog. Elongated banana-shaped fruit 16 to 24 inches long, yellow rind, salmon-pink flesh with banana-like aroma; eat ripe or as a kitchen curiosity.
entries.source: Seed Savers Exchange heirloom collection; W. Atlee Burpee Company catalog archives.

entries.notes: 1846 American heirloom named for the Swedish opera singer. 1 to 2 pound turban-shaped fruit with a button on the blossom end, lightly netted, green flesh with delicate sweetness.
entries.source: Seed Savers Exchange heirloom records; USDA-ARS GRIN historical cultivar accession.

entries.notes: Pre-1850 French cantaloupe with deeply ribbed warty 6 to 9 pound fruit. Pale gray-green at maturity, deep orange aromatic flesh; ornamental and delicious heritage variety.
entries.source: Vilmorin and Andrieux Les Plantes Potagères 1885; Seed Savers Exchange heirloom collection.

windows.notes: Short-season melon for Northern gardens; tolerates cooler nights.

entries.notes: University of New Hampshire short-season release. 2 to 3 pound lightly netted muskmelon with orange flesh; one of the earliest melons available, suited to cool summers.
entries.source: University of New Hampshire Cooperative Extension vegetable variety guide; USDA-ARS GRIN release records.

entries.notes: Ukrainian heirloom honeydew-style melon. Smooth yellow rind, very sweet pale flesh, 2 to 3 pound oval fruit; productive even in cool short-season climates.
entries.source: Seed Savers Exchange heirloom collection; Baker Creek Heirloom Seeds variety notes.

entries.notes: Armenian small heirloom with striking vivid yellow rind striped fire-red. 1 pound personal-size fruit, white flesh, intense floral fragrance; productive and ornamental.
entries.source: Seed Savers Exchange Armenian heirloom records; Baker Creek Heirloom Seeds catalog notes.

entries.notes: Modern canary-type hybrid with bright yellow rind and crisp white flesh. 4 to 5 pound oval fruit, sweet mild flavor, excellent shelf life; good resistance to powdery mildew.
entries.source: Johnny's Selected Seeds variety database; Hollar Seeds breeder cultivar notes.

windows.notes: Compact 3 to 4 foot vines; suitable for small gardens and containers.

entries.notes: 1948 University of Minnesota release for short seasons. Personal-size 4 inch netted cantaloupe with sweet orange flesh edible to the rind; compact bush-like vines.
entries.source: University of Minnesota Extension Yard and Garden News; USDA-ARS GRIN release records.

entries.notes: French Brittany heirloom said to date to 1612 in the gardens of the Bishop of Rennes. 2 pound smooth gray-green Charentais-type fruit, deep orange flesh, intense aroma.
entries.source: Vilmorin and Andrieux French heritage seed records; Seed Savers Exchange Garden Seed Inventory.

entries.notes: 1956 icebox watermelon, 8 to 12 pound round fruit, dark green rind, sweet red flesh. Compact 6 foot vines suit small gardens; reliable producer across most US regions.
entries.source: USDA-ARS GRIN cultivar records; Cornell Cooperative Extension Vegetable Varieties for Gardeners.

maritime.notes: Short-season icebox cultivar suited to cool maritime; one of the few watermelons that ripens before September. Black plastic mulch and row cover for 4 weeks post-transplant.

continental.notes: First-ripe in 75 to 80 days; perfect for Zones 4 to 6 where full-season Crimson Sweet would not finish. Direct sow after soil hits 70F.

humid_subtropical.notes: Plant earlier than full-season types to dodge July bacterial wilt pressure. Single-stem trellis with sling support also reduces ground rot.

arid.notes: Plant in early spring; harvest by July before mid-summer fruit-set fails in heat above 100F. Drip-irrigate twice weekly.

entries.notes: 1954 USDA Charleston, South Carolina release. Long oblong 25 to 30 pound fruit with thin gray-green rind and sweet red flesh; resistant to anthracnose and fusarium.
entries.source: USDA-ARS variety release records; Clemson Cooperative Extension HGIC 1310 watermelon cultivars.

entries.notes: 1964 Kansas State University release, the modern American picnic watermelon standard. 20 to 25 pound oval fruit, striped rind, very sweet red flesh; fusarium and anthracnose resistant.
entries.source: Kansas State University variety release records; USDA-ARS GRIN cultivar history.

entries.notes: Pre-1926 American heirloom rediscovered by Kent Whealy. Dark green rind spotted with yellow stars and a large moon; 15 to 40 pound fruit, pink flesh, classic flavor.
entries.source: Seed Savers Exchange heirloom records; USDA-ARS GRIN historical cultivar accession.

entries.notes: 1963 University of Florida release. 25 to 40 pound oblong striped fruit, sweet red flesh; widely planted commercial watermelon in the Southeast for decades.
entries.source: University of Florida IFAS Extension HS-103 watermelon variety guide; USDA-ARS GRIN release records.

entries.notes: Early icebox watermelon hybrid, 5 to 8 pound striped round fruit with crisp sweet yellow flesh. Compact vines, productive in Northern gardens, novel color in mixed displays.
entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension Vegetable Varieties for Gardeners; Johnny's Selected Seeds variety database.

entries.notes: Willhite Seed heirloom, 18 to 25 pound oblong striped fruit with brilliant orange flesh and exceptionally sweet flavor. Often rated top heirloom in melon-tasting competitions.
entries.source: Seed Savers Exchange heirloom records; Willhite Seed Inc. variety catalog.

windows.notes: Adapted to cool nights and short seasons.

entries.notes: Glenn Drowns selection from northern Idaho, the earliest watermelon for cool-summer climates. 6 to 12 pound round fruit, dark green rind, red flesh; reliable in Zone 3 to 4.
entries.source: Seed Savers Exchange Glenn Drowns cultivar records; Sand Hill Preservation Center catalog notes.

entries.notes: Russian-Mennonite heirloom brought to Canadian prairies. 4 to 10 pound round fruit, thin pale green striped rind, crisp creamy white sweet flesh; suited to short cool seasons.
entries.source: Seed Savers Exchange Mennonite heritage seed records; Heritage Seed Program of Canada (Seeds of Diversity) variety notes.

entries.notes: Indiana heirloom dating to the late 1800s. Large 30 to 50 pound oblong dark green fruit, sweet red flesh; vigorous vines, good keeper, classic late-summer slicing melon.
entries.source: Seed Savers Exchange heirloom records; Sand Hill Preservation Center variety catalog.

entries.notes: 1830s Southern heirloom, 25 to 35 pound oblong fruit with light green wavy stripes resembling snake markings. Pink-red sweet flesh, drought tolerant, long Southern season.
entries.source: Seed Savers Exchange Southern heirloom records; USDA-ARS GRIN historical cultivar accession.

entries.notes: Iraqi heirloom collected by Aziz Nail of Baghdad. 12 to 30 pound oblong light green fruit, crisp very sweet red flesh; drought and heat tolerant, productive in hot summers.
entries.source: Seed Savers Exchange Aziz Nail collection records; Baker Creek Heirloom Seeds catalog notes.

windows.notes: Needs long warm season (110+ frost-free days); hill soil around plants at flowering.

entries.notes: Virginia market type with very large two-seed pods, the standard for in-shell roasted peanuts and ballpark peanuts. Erect bunch habit; cures best in dry warm fall weather.
entries.source: Virginia Cooperative Extension Publication 2912-1429 peanut production; USDA-ARS Tidewater Agricultural Research Station notes.

entries.notes: Spanish market type with small reddish-brown seeds high in oil. Earlier maturity than Virginia types, upright habit, well suited to the Southern Plains; primary use is peanut butter and oil.
entries.source: Oklahoma State University Extension PSS-2243 peanut production; USDA-ARS GRIN cultivar records.

entries.notes: Valencia type heirloom with bright red seed coats and 3 to 5 seeds per pod. Sweet flavor, lower in oil than Spanish types; the classic boiled-peanut and home-garden variety.
entries.source: Seed Savers Exchange heirloom records; New Mexico State University Extension publication H-235 peanut production.

entries.notes: Runner market type from University of Georgia, the dominant commercial peanut for peanut butter in the US. Prostrate habit, uniform medium-sized pods, tomato spotted wilt tolerance.
entries.source: University of Georgia Extension Bulletin 1146 peanut production; USDA-ARS GRIN cultivar records.

windows.notes: Cool-season legume; sow early, harvest before peak summer heat.

entries.notes: Large cream-colored chickpea type grown across the Mediterranean and Northern India. 18 to 24 inch bushy plants, 1 to 2 seeds per pod; the standard garbanzo for hummus and salads.
entries.source: USDA-ARS Pullman Washington pulse crops research; ICARDA chickpea variety database.

entries.notes: Smaller dark-seeded chickpea type, traditional across South Asia and Ethiopia. More drought tolerant than Kabuli, higher fiber; ground for besan flour, split for chana dal.
entries.source: ICRISAT chickpea improvement program records; USDA-ARS GRIN cultivar accession data.

entries.notes: Rare Italian heirloom (ceci neri) with small glossy black seeds. Earthy nutty flavor stronger than common chickpeas; productive bush habit, prized in Puglia regional cuisine.
entries.source: Seed Savers Exchange heirloom records; Slow Food Italian Ark of Taste catalog.

windows.notes: Cold tolerant to 15F as seedlings; sow very early spring or fall in mild winter zones.

entries.notes: Classic English broad bean dating to 1799. 3 foot upright plants, 4 to 5 inch pods with 4 to 5 large flat green seeds; rich buttery flavor when young, starchy when mature.
entries.source: Royal Horticultural Society heritage variety records; Seed Savers Exchange Garden Seed Inventory.

windows.notes: Traditional overwintering fava in mild winter regions; sows in fall, harvests in spring.

entries.notes: Spanish overwintering fava preferred across the Mediterranean. Very cold tolerant, 3 to 4 foot plants, long 7 to 9 inch pods with 5 to 7 white seeds; the classic Iberian variety.
entries.source: Vilmorin and Andrieux historical seed records; West Coast Seeds variety database.

entries.notes: English heirloom with very long 8 to 10 inch pods containing 6 to 7 oblong green seeds. 3 foot upright plants, heavy yielder; classic for fresh shelling and freezing.
entries.source: Royal Horticultural Society plant records; Heritage Seed Library variety notes.

entries.notes: Dutch-bred quick-maturing fava, 30 inch sturdy plants, 6 to 7 inch pods with 4 to 5 large pale green seeds. Holds up better in heat than older types; popular with market growers.
entries.source: Dutch seed cooperative variety records; Johnny's Selected Seeds variety database.

windows.notes: Cool-season pulse; sow early. Drought tolerant once established.

entries.notes: Spanish brown lentil grown in Castile and Tierra de Campos. Small mottled brown seeds, holds shape in soups and stews; widely grown in the US Palouse region for export.
entries.source: USDA-ARS Pullman Washington pulse crops research; ICARDA lentil variety database.

entries.notes: Canadian-bred small green lentil from the Crop Development Centre at University of Saskatchewan, 1980 release. The dominant commercial green lentil across the Northern Plains.
entries.source: University of Saskatchewan Crop Development Centre release records; USDA-ARS pulse crop variety notes.

entries.notes: Medium-sized brown lentil grown for hearty soup-style dishes. Earthy flavor, slightly softer cooking texture than Pardina, ideal for traditional Iberian lentejas estofadas.
entries.source: USDA-ARS Pullman pulse crops research; Castilian regional pulse cultivar records.

windows.notes: Bareroot transplant after last frost

entries.notes: Canopy layer nitrogen fixer. Edible flowers (raw or fritters), rot resistant timber, premium bee forage. Pods and bark toxic. Coppices well for fuelwood.
entries.source: USDA-NRCS Plant Materials Program; Mark Shepard Restoration Agriculture

windows.notes: Plant after soil thaws

entries.notes: Canopy legume (mycorrhizal not rhizobial, light N benefit). Edible sweet pod pulp, livestock fodder. Dappled shade for silvopasture. Pods 30 percent sugar.
entries.source: USDA-NRCS; J. Russell Smith Tree Crops

entries.notes: Canopy legume, non N-fixing despite family. Roasted seeds make coffee substitute (raw seeds toxic). Drought tolerant. Slow growing prairie native. Note: 10+ years to first fruit; daysToHarvest capped at schema max 3650 days.
Canopy nitrogen fixer (rhizobial). Edible young leaves and flowers (he huan hua in TCM). Fast growing pioneer, can be invasive in SE US. Bee forage.
entries.source: USDA-NRCS; Dave Jacke Edible Forest Gardens vol 2

entries.notes: Canopy nitrogen fixer via Frankia actinobacteria. Pacific NW pioneer. Smoked salmon wood. Riparian buffer. Builds soil fast for succession plantings.
Canopy nitrogen fixer (Frankia). Tolerates dry soils better than other alders. Windbreak species. Coppices for biomass. Heart shaped leaves.
entries.source: Bill Mollison Permaculture A Designers Manual

entries.notes: Shrub-form nitrogen fixer (Frankia). Cold hardy, wetland pioneer. Stream bank stabilization. Native to northern NA. Builds soil in 5 to 10 yr.
Canopy nitrogen fixer (Frankia). Wet soil tolerant, fixes 50 to 200 lb N per acre per yr. Coppice species. Listed invasive in some Pacific NW areas.
Shrub layer nitrogen fixer. Edible peas (raw young, cooked mature) up to 36 percent protein. Chicken forage. Windbreak hedge. Extreme cold hardy.
entries.source: Eric Toensmeier Perennial Vegetables; Permaculture Research Institute

entries.notes: Understory nitrogen fixer (ornamental, ALL PARTS TOXIC if ingested). Dramatic yellow racemes. Bee plant. Use as N donor only, no edible parts.
entries.source: RHS Plantfinder; Dave Jacke Edible Forest Gardens

entries.notes: Drylands canopy nitrogen fixer. Edible pods ground into sweet flour (11 to 17 percent protein). Premium honey. Native staple, drought legend.
entries.source: USDA-NRCS Tucson Plant Materials; Gary Paul Nabhan

entries.notes: Sonoran desert canopy N-fixer. Edible pod flour, less astringent than P. glandulosa. Drought adapted. Pollinator powerhouse. Coppices vigorously.
Mediterranean canopy non-nodulating legume. Edible sweet pods (chocolate substitute), seeds yield locust bean gum. Drought tolerant. Dioecious.
Shrub-layer nitrogen fixer, premier fodder tree (20 percent protein leaves). Fast growing pioneer in poor soils. Bee plant. Canary Islands native.
Shrub layer nitrogen fixer (Frankia). Sweet red berries high in lycopene, ripens June. Self fertile. Productive nurse plant for fruit guilds.
entries.source: One Green World catalog; USDA-ARS Corvallis

entries.notes: Shrub layer N-fixer. Larger fruit than Sweet Scarlet, slightly later. Cross pollinate for max yield. Edible red berries, jam quality.
entries.source: One Green World; Restoration Agriculture Mark Shepard

entries.notes: Shrub layer nitrogen fixer (Frankia). Red tart berries (high lycopene), traditional plains native food. Dioecious, need both sexes. Drought hardy.
Shrub layer N-fixer (Frankia). Edible mealy silver fruits, traditional First Nations food. Wildlife forage. Cold prairie native. Suckers.
entries.source: USDA-NRCS Northern Plains; Plants For A Future

entries.notes: Shrub layer N-fixer (Frankia). Dried leaves brew caffeine-free tea used during Revolution. Bee plant. Drought tolerant prairie native.
Shrub layer nitrogen fixer (Frankia). Cobalt blue flower clusters, exceptional bee forage. Drought, evergreen. Mediterranean climate guild.
Herb layer nitrogen fixer. Deep taproot, drought proof prairie native. Native bumblebee specialist host. Not edible. Long lived perennial.
Shrub layer N-fixer (Frankia). Aromatic leaves for bay seasoning. Waxy berries yield bayberry wax for candles. Salt tolerant, coastal hedge.
Shrub layer nitrogen fixer (Frankia). Aromatic leaves (bay substitute), waxy fruit for candles. Salt and drought tolerant evergreen hedge.
Shrub layer N-fixer (Frankia). Wetland species. Aromatic leaves used historically in gruit ale brewing. Insect repellent. Wet boggy soils.
entries.source: Plants For A Future; Scottish Beer Heritage

windows.notes: Root crowns

entries.notes: Herb layer dynamic accumulator (K, Ca, P, deep taproot 8 ft). Sterile cultivar will not seed in. Chop and drop mulch, compost activator, biomass.
entries.source: Lawrence D. Hills Russian Comfrey; Permaculture Research Institute

entries.notes: Herb layer accumulator (taproot mines subsoil minerals). Seeds freely (unlike Bocking 14). Traditional medicinal (topical only, contains PAs).
entries.source: Dave Jacke Edible Forest Gardens vol 2; Plants For A Future

entries.notes: Herb layer accumulator, deep taproot for coffee substitute (roasted). Mines compacted subsoil. Edible greens, bee forage in 2nd year bloom.
Herb layer dynamic accumulator (Ca, K, P, taproot 3 ft). Edible greens, root coffee, flower wine. Larger leaves than wild type, less bitter.
Herb layer accumulator (Fe, K, P, deep taproot). Edible young leaves (cooked, oxalic acid). Medicinal root tonic. Naturalizes readily.
entries.source: Plants For A Future; Susun Weed Healing Wise

entries.notes: Herb accumulator. Broad leaves cool nettle stings, edible young (oxalic acid, cook well). Deep taproot mines subsoil. Considered weed.
entries.source: Plants For A Future; UK Herbal Tradition

entries.notes: Herb layer nitrogen fixer, deep accumulator. Showy flower spikes, native bumblebee host. Seeds toxic raw. Acid soil tolerant, cool climate.

windows.notes: Fall sow for spring bloom

entries.notes: Herb layer N-fixer, annual self-seeding. Texas state flower, prairie reseed cover. Honey bee forage. Seeds toxic raw. Drought adapted.
entries.source: Lady Bird Johnson Wildflower Center; USDA-NRCS Texas

entries.notes: Herb layer silica accumulator (up to 25 percent dry weight). Decoction is antifungal foliar spray. Young shoots edible (cooked). Aggressive, contain it.
entries.source: Plants For A Future; Biodynamic 508 spray tradition

entries.notes: Herb layer silica accumulator (scouring rush). Wetland margin native, contained pond plant. Not edible. Stems used historically as pot scrubbers.
entries.source: USDA Wetland Plants; Plants For A Future

entries.notes: Herb layer accumulator (K, Ca, Mg). Edible young leaves, medicinal poultice for bites and burns. Pasture component, deep fibrous root system.
Herb ground cover, accumulator. Tangy edible leaves (cooked or salads, oxalic acid). Acid soil indicator. Component of Essiac herbal blend.
entries.source: Plants For A Future; Eric Toensmeier

entries.notes: Herb layer culinary perennial. Lemon flavored shield shaped leaves, milder than garden sorrel. Classic French sauce ingredient. Cut and come again.
entries.source: Eric Toensmeier Perennial Vegetables; Real Seeds

entries.notes: Ground layer winter annual, accumulator. Edible mild salad green, traditional spring tonic. Reseeds, living mulch under brassicas, chicken forage.
entries.source: Plants For A Future; Susun Weed

entries.notes: Herb layer accumulator, edible spinach substitute (oxalic acid). Highest protein wild green at 4 percent. Self seeds. Garden volunteer.
Herb layer leaf amaranth, Mediterranean traditional. C4 heat lover. Cut and come again greens, mild flavor. Self seeds, becomes garden volunteer.
entries.source: Eric Toensmeier Perennial Vegetables; Real Seeds

entries.notes: Herb layer Mediterranean annual nettle, accumulator (Fe, Ca, N). Edible cooked greens (high protein 25 percent dry). Globular seed pods.
entries.source: Plants For A Future; Italian Heritage Seeds

windows.notes: Cold stratify seeds

entries.notes: Herb layer medicinal (topical only, never internal). Alpine native, acid soil. Yellow daisy flowers harvested for tincture and salves.
Herb layer wetland accumulator. Salicylate source (gave aspirin its name). Almond-scented flowers for tea, mead flavoring. Damp meadow native.
entries.source: Plants For A Future; Strictly Medicinal

entries.notes: Herb layer accumulator. Bitter culinary (goose seasoning), moxibustion plant, dream herb. Aggressive rhizomes, contain in bed. Pollinator host.
entries.source: Plants For A Future; Bill Mollison

entries.notes: Ground layer N-fixer, miniature white clover for lawn mix. Tolerates close mowing. Adds N to turf at 50 to 100 lb per acre. Bee free if mown.
Ground layer winter annual N-fixer. Self-burying seed, regenerates yearly. Mediterranean climate orchard cover. Australian sheep pasture standard.
entries.source: USDA-NRCS Lockeford Plant Materials; Australian PIRSA

entries.notes: Ground layer shade tolerant. Coumarin-scented dried leaves flavor May wine. Forms dense mat under fruit trees. Suppresses weeds in dappled shade.
entries.source: Dave Jacke Edible Forest Gardens; Strictly Medicinal

entries.notes: Ground layer pollinator magnet. Magenta flowers, walkable mat. Drought tolerant. Culinary (mild thyme flavor). Bee forage extraordinaire.
Ground layer culinary creeper. Pink flowers, bee plant. Tolerates light foot traffic. Stronger flavor than T. serpyllum. Drought tolerant.
Ground layer mat. Yellow flowers, golden cultivar 'Aurea' popular. Moist soil pond edge. Not edible. Can be aggressive, contain in beds.
Ground layer mint, traditional flea repellent. Essential oil TOXIC internally, never use during pregnancy. Walkable mat in moist shade.
entries.source: Plants For A Future; Strictly Medicinal

entries.notes: Ground layer evergreen mat (not true moss). Tiny white flowers. Walkable between pavers. Moist part shade. Not edible.
Ground layer chartreuse-gold mat form. White spring flowers. Walkable in light traffic. Not edible. Pair with dark stone for contrast.
Ground layer tiny-leaved mint. Releases creme de menthe scent when crushed underfoot. Edible. Crema de menthe flavoring. Moist part shade.
Ground layer micro-cultivar of creeping thyme. 1 inch tall mat, pink flowers. Tightest carpet variety. Drought tolerant. Edible culinary.
Ground layer non-flowering Roman chamomile, classic chamomile lawn cultivar. Apple scent when walked on. Propagated by division only.
Ground layer trailing culinary rosemary. Spills over walls and terraces. Mediterranean drought tolerant. Edible needles, bee forage spring bloom.
Understory tree, blueberry-like sweet fruit. Earliest-ripening saskatoon cultivar, large 5/8 inch berries. Hardy to -50F. Edible Plant Manitoba selection.

continental.notes: Cold-hardy to Zone 2; the defining Northern Plains and Canadian Prairie native fruit shrub. Drought-tolerant once established.

maritime.notes: Productive in Pacific NW with full sun; fewer pollinators visit in cool wet springs. Less productive than zone-2 native habitat.

semi_arid.notes: Excellent semi-arid native fruit shrub; the saskatoon berry of choice for the Great Plains. Drought-tolerant with low irrigation.

humid_subtropical.notes: Marginal in deep South; entomosporium leaf spot pressure heavy in humid summers. Choose serviceberry-regent for upper South alternative.

entries.notes: Understory native fruit shrub. Industry standard saskatoon for fresh eating, mild sweet flavor. Heavy producer. Cold hardy zone 2.
Understory single-trunk tree form, dark purple sweet berries July. White spring blossoms. Better fruit set than wild laevis. Bird hedge.
Understory tree, non-nodulating legume. Edible magenta flowers (salads, fritters), young pods. Native ornamental, bee plant. Dappled shade.
entries.source: USDA-NRCS; Samuel Thayer The Forager's Harvest

entries.notes: Understory non-nodulating legume. Glossy leaves, wine-red flowers. Heat and drought tolerant SW cultivar. Edible flowers and young pods.
entries.source: Oklahoma State Hort Extension; Native Plants Journal

entries.notes: Canopy nut tree. Largely lost to blight, restoration cultivars available. Edible starchy nuts, premium rot resistant timber. Plant in groups for pollination.
Canopy nut tree, blight resistant. Edible sweet nuts (Asian roasting style). Bears in 4 to 6 yr. Plant 2 cultivars for cross pollination.
Canopy hybrid nut tree, blight resistant. Large sweet nuts, American type form. Heavy producer in 3 to 5 yr. Industry standard for new orchards.
entries.source: Chestnut Hill Tree Farm; Restoration Agriculture Mark Shepard

entries.notes: Shrub layer nut, EFB resistant Oregon State release. Large nuts, blanches well. Cross pollinate with 'Theta' or 'Eta'. Catkins late winter.
entries.source: Oregon State University Nut Breeding Program

entries.notes: Shrub layer EFB resistant, kernel cultivar. Heavy producer, early ripening. Cross pollinate with 'York' or 'Felix'. Wind pollinated.
Canopy nut tree. Heart-shaped nuts crack cleanly into two halves. Less juglone than black walnut. Tropical-looking foliage. Late leaf-out.
Canopy nut tree, native NE NA. Oily edible nuts, syrup-tappable sap. Threatened by canker disease, plant resistant selections. Allelopathic juglone.
entries.source: USDA Forest Service; Northern Nut Growers

entries.notes: Canopy nut tree, largest hickory nuts. Sweet kernels, premium smoking wood. Slow to bear (8 to 12 yr). Bottomland species, moist soils. Note: 10+ years to first fruit; daysToHarvest capped at schema max 3650 days.
Canopy nut, USDA northern adapted release. Early ripening (Sep). Type I pollen shed, pair with type II ('Kanza'). Scab resistant.
Canopy nut, USDA northern release. Type II pollen, pair with 'Pawnee'. Excellent kernel quality, very scab resistant. Cold hardy to zone 5.
Canopy nut, heavy-bearing thin-shelled cultivar. Strongly allelopathic (juglone, avoid in vegetable proximity). Premium timber, edible nuts.
Shrub layer cherry. Sweet-tart red fruit, jam quality. Pink early spring blossoms. Extreme cold hardy. Two plants for cross pollination.
entries.source: USDA-NRCS Bismarck; Northern Hardy Fruit Trials

entries.notes: Shrub layer native Atlantic coast plum. Tart purple fruit excellent jam. Salt and sand tolerant. White spring bloom. Cross pollinate seedlings.
Shrub layer dwarf cherry, U Saskatchewan Romance series. Dark sweet-tart fruit, self fertile but better with cross. Hardy to zone 2, 6 to 8 ft.
Shrub layer dwarf cherry, Romance series. Sweetest of the line, fresh eating quality. Self fertile. Cold hardy to -50F. Disease resistant.
Shrub layer blue honeysuckle, U Saskatchewan release. Large sweet-tart elongated berries late June. Cross pollinate with 'Honey Bee' or 'Borealis'.
Shrub layer haskap, U Saskatchewan. Standard yield cultivar. Earliest ripening berry, beats strawberries. Hardy to zone 2. Needs cross pollinator.
Shrub layer haskap, U Saskatchewan. Firm berry suited for mechanical harvest. Anthocyanin-rich. Pair with 'Aurora' or 'Honey Bee' pollinator.
Shrub layer Russian-type honeyberry. Mild blueberry flavor, midseason ripening. Pair with 'Blue Velvet' for pollination. Hardy to zone 3.
entries.source: One Green World; Russian Berry Research Institute

entries.notes: Shrub layer honeyberry. Compact 3 to 4 ft bush, sweet-tart fruit. Powdery blue bloom on skin. Pollinate with 'Blue Moon'. Bird hedge possible.
Shrub N-fixer (Frankia). High-lycopene red fruit. INVASIVE in much of E US, check local rules before planting. Use 'Cardinal' wildlife cultivar.
Shrub layer native viburnum, not a true cranberry. Tart red fruit (cook with sweetener for sauce). Beautiful fall color. Bird forage in winter.
Shrub layer native viburnum. Sweet blue-black fruit September, raisin-like. White flat-topped spring flowers. Tall thicket-forming shrub.
Vine layer hardy kiwi. Grape-sized smooth skinned fruit, eaten skin and all. Female, needs male pollinator (1 male per 6 females).
Vine layer self-fertile hardy kiwi (rare in genus). Earliest bearing, smaller fruit. Good for small spaces. Better yields with male nearby.
entries.source: One Green World; Japanese Kiwi Growers

entries.notes: Vine layer hardy kiwi, U New Hampshire selection. Cold hardy (-25F), heavy producer. Female, requires male pollinator. Pergola-suitable.
entries.source: University of New Hampshire; Edible Landscaping

entries.notes: Vine layer climber. Edible purple pods (gelatinous mild pulp), edible young leaves and shoots. Vanilla-scented flowers. Can be aggressive.
entries.source: Plants For A Future; Eric Toensmeier

windows.notes: Plant rhizomes 2 to 4 inches deep

entries.notes: Vine layer dual-purpose hop, classic Pacific NW citrus floral profile. 4 to 7 percent alpha acid. Vigorous bine, 15 to 20 ft. Cone harvest Aug to Sep.
entries.source: USDA-ARS Hop Cultivar Database; Yakima Chief Hops

entries.notes: Vine layer dual-purpose hop, supercharged Cascade with 9 to 12 percent alpha acid. American IPA staple. Floral citrus aroma. 15 ft bine.
Vine layer bittering hop, 12 to 14 percent alpha acid. Pine, grapefruit, spice profile. Heavy yielding bine. Cold hardy and disease resistant.
entries.source: USDA-ARS Hop Cultivar Database; Hop Growers of America

entries.notes: Vine layer tropical fruit. Aromatic purple fruit, juice signature flavor. Self-fertile but better with two. Trellis or fence. Bee pollinated.
entries.source: University of Florida IFAS; FAO Passion Fruit Manual

entries.notes: Vine layer native US passionflower. Cold hardy to zone 5 (dies back). Yellow fruit with tart pulp. Medicinal calmative herb. Suckers.

windows.notes: Plant entire sprouted fruit

entries.notes: Vine layer cucurbit perennial in warm zones. Edible fruit (mild summer squash), shoots, tubers, leaves. Single plant 30 to 100 fruit. Mexican staple.
entries.source: Eric Toensmeier Perennial Vegetables; UC Davis Cucurbit

entries.notes: Vine layer heat-loving climber. Red-stemmed cultivar, edible glossy leaves (mucilaginous spinach substitute). Tropical perennial, US annual. Trellis.
Vine layer green-stemmed Basella. Cut and come again spinach substitute. Tolerates 90F+ when true spinach bolts. South Asian staple. Trellis.
Vine layer epiphytic orchid. True vanilla spice (cured pods). Requires hand pollination outside Mexico. Tropical or greenhouse only. 70F minimum.
entries.source: FAO Vanilla Manual; USDA Tropical Plants

windows.notes: Plant tubers 3 inches deep

entries.notes: Root layer vining nitrogen fixer (rhizobial legume). Edible protein-rich tubers (16 percent). Eastern native staple, Pilgrim survival food. Slow start.
entries.source: Eric Toensmeier; LSU AgCenter breeding program

entries.notes: Herb layer wet-soil perennial. Edible celery-like leaves (Asian staple, Korean minari). Cut and come again. Pond margin or boggy spot.
entries.source: Kitazawa Seed; Eric Toensmeier Perennial Vegetables

windows.notes: Needs cold stratification

entries.notes: Root layer woodland forage. Edible chestnut-flavored tuber, raw or cooked. British and French hedgerow native. Slow to establish 2 to 3 yr.
entries.source: Plants For A Future; Roger Phillips Wild Food

windows.notes: Plant tubercles or root sections

entries.notes: Vine and root layer. Edible white tubers (3 to 4 ft long), edible aerial bulbils. Cold hardy yam. INVASIVE in SE US, harvest bulbils to control.
entries.source: Eric Toensmeier; USDA Invasive Plant Atlas

windows.notes: Plant rhizome divisions

entries.notes: Root layer Andean starch staple. Edible large rhizomes (mild potato flavor), edible young shoots. Ornamental tropical foliage. Dig in fall, replant.
entries.source: CIP Lima Andean Roots and Tubers; Eric Toensmeier

entries.notes: Root layer Medieval European staple, lost vegetable. Sweet finger-thin roots in clusters, taste like parsnip-carrot. Reseeds, divide crowns.
entries.source: Eric Toensmeier Perennial Vegetables; Real Seeds

entries.notes: Root and herb layer scrambling perennial legume. Edible chestnut-flavored tubers, magenta pea flowers. Nitrogen fixing. Old European peasant food.
entries.source: Plants For A Future; Eric Toensmeier

windows.notes: Fall plant bulbs

entries.notes: Root layer Pacific NW native first food. Edible blue-flowered bulbs (slow pit roast). DO NOT confuse with toxic death camas (white flowers).
entries.source: Nancy Turner Plants of the Pacific NW; USDA-NRCS

entries.notes: Root and ground layer spring ephemeral. Edible chestnut-flavored corms, edible flowers and leaves. Eastern woodland indicator. Naturalizes in lawns.
entries.source: Samuel Thayer Nature's Garden; Eric Toensmeier

entries.notes: Windbreak canopy tree. Edible samaras (young winged seeds, salad green). Fast growth, drought hardy. INVASIVE in much of US, prefer native elms.
Windbreak canopy tree, DED resistant. Edible young samaras (fall ripening, opposite Ulmus pumila). Mottled bark ornamental. Not aggressive.
Windbreak and hedge tree, original Great Plains living fence (pre-barbed wire). Fruits not human edible. Premium rot resistant fence post wood.
entries.source: USDA-NRCS Great Plains; J. Russell Smith Tree Crops

entries.notes: Canopy nut tree, Mediterranean classic. Edible pine nuts (pignoli, premium for pesto). 15 to 25 yr to commercial nut production. Umbrella canopy. Note: 10+ years to first fruit; daysToHarvest capped at schema max 3650 days.
entries.source: FAO Pinus pinea Manual; Spanish Pine Nut Industry

entries.notes: Canopy SW US nut pine. Edible rich oily pine nuts, indigenous staple. 25 yr to first crop, masts every 4 to 7 yr. Drought hardy. Note: 10+ years to first fruit; daysToHarvest capped at schema max 3650 days.
entries.source: USDA Forest Service Southwest; Gary Paul Nabhan

entries.notes: Canopy Great Basin nut pine. Largest pinyon seeds, prized Paiute and Shoshone food. Single needle (unique in genus). Extreme drought tolerant. Note: 10+ years to first fruit; daysToHarvest capped at schema max 3650 days.
entries.source: USDA Forest Service; Nevada Native Plant Society

entries.notes: Canopy nut pine, southern Sierra Madre. Edible thin-shelled large pinyon nuts. Drought hardy. Suited to Texas, Arizona, Mexico foothills. Note: 10+ years to first fruit; daysToHarvest capped at schema max 3650 days.
entries.source: USDA Forest Service; Mexican Pine Nut Industry

entries.notes: Windbreak Great Basin native. Edible berries used by Hopi and Navajo (cook with meat). Drought-extreme. Slow growth. Allergenic pollen.
Windbreak conifer, cold hardy western native. Edible berries (gin flavoring, limited human use). Wildlife winter forage. Drought tolerant.
Understory shrub-tree nut, native SE US. Single small sweet nuts per bur (vs 3 in true chestnut). Blight tolerant. Cross pollinate seedlings.
Aquatic edge layer. Edible peppery leaves, vitamin C and K dense. Cool flowing water ideal, but moist garden bed also works. Hardy perennial.
entries.source: Kitazawa Seed; Rocky Mountain Watercress Co

entries.notes: Aquatic floating layer. Edible cooked horned seeds (Asian water chestnut, different from Eleocharis). INVASIVE in US, prohibited in many states.
entries.source: USDA Invasive Aquatics; FAO Asian Water Plants

entries.notes: Aquatic and herb layer. Edible hollow stems and leaves, SE Asian stir fry staple. Wet bed or flooded paddy. INVASIVE noxious in many US states.
entries.source: Kitazawa Seed; USDA Federal Noxious Weed List

windows.notes: Plant tubers in pond mud

entries.notes: Aquatic layer. Edible crisp rhizomes, edible seeds (raw or roasted), edible young leaves. Asian staple. Cold hardy when rhizomes below frost line.
Aquatic layer. Edible starchy tubers (cooked, Chinese ci gu). European cousin of native wapato. Pond edge wetland. White arrow-shaped leaves.
entries.source: Plants For A Future; Kitazawa Seed

windows.notes: Inoculate sawdust blocks or logs year-round indoors; outdoor log inoculation Mar to May.

entries.notes: Wild Italian is a wide-temperature-range shiitake strain (50 to 80F fruiting) producing dense brown caps with white veiling. Reliable for both indoor sawdust and outdoor oak/maple logs. First flush 6 to 12 weeks after spawn run.
entries.source: Field & Forest Products and North Spore strain catalogs (2023)

windows.notes: Cool-weather strain; outdoor log fruiting peaks 50 to 65F in spring and fall.

entries.notes: Snow Cap is a cool-weather shiitake selected for thick white-flecked caps and slow growth that yields dense flesh. Best fruited at 50 to 65F. Excellent for fall log production in temperate climates.

windows.notes: Warm-weather shiitake; fruits readily 60 to 80F on logs or blocks.

entries.notes: West Wind is a warm-weather shiitake strain reliable in summer outdoor log production in zones 6 to 9. Medium-thick chestnut-brown caps with moderate yields. Less prone to soak-shocking than cool strains.

windows.notes: Indoor sawdust block strain; fruit year-round at 55 to 70F.

entries.notes: Night Velvet is a dark-capped indoor sawdust shiitake strain prized for near-black mature caps and high yields on supplemented hardwood blocks. Short spawn run for commercial indoor production.
entries.source: North Spore commercial shiitake strain notes (2023)

windows.notes: Cool-fruiting oyster; pin and fruit at 50 to 65F.

entries.notes: Blue Oyster fruits cool (50 to 65F) with steel-blue young caps that mature to gray-brown. Vigorous on straw, supplemented sawdust, and master mix. Fast colonizer, popular for cold-season indoor production.
entries.source: Stamets, Growing Gourmet and Medicinal Mushrooms (2000); North Spore strain guide

windows.notes: Tropical oyster; fruit at 70 to 85F. Will not fruit below 65F.

entries.notes: Pink Oyster (P. djamor) is the fastest oyster, fruiting in under 14 days from pin set. Bright salmon-pink clusters fade to tan when cooked. Short shelf life (1 to 2 days). Warm-only species.
entries.source: Stamets, Growing Gourmet and Medicinal Mushrooms (2000)

windows.notes: Warm-fruiting oyster; 65 to 80F ideal.

entries.notes: Golden Oyster (P. citrinopileatus) produces bright yellow clusters with cashew-like flavor. Warm fruiter (65 to 80F), short shelf life. Naturalizes in eastern North America from escaped cultivation.

windows.notes: Bottle or bag culture on supplemented sawdust; 55 to 65F fruiting.

entries.notes: King Oyster (P. eryngii) produces thick fleshy stems prized for scallop-like cooking texture. Requires supplemented sawdust (10 to 20% bran). Fruits at 55 to 65F with high CO2 tolerance during pin formation.
entries.source: Stamets (2000); commercial growers' notes Hokto Kinoko

windows.notes: Cool to moderate fruiter, 55 to 70F.

entries.notes: Tarragon Oyster (P. euosmus) emits a distinct tarragon-anise aroma. Rare in commerce. Gray-brown caps on supplemented straw or sawdust. Moderate colonizer best at 55 to 70F.
entries.source: Stamets (2000); Mycological Society of America species notes

windows.notes: Warm-tolerant oyster; fruit 60 to 80F.

entries.notes: Phoenix Oyster (P. pulmonarius) tolerates warmer fruiting temps than P. ostreatus, ideal for summer production. Pale tan caps on thin laterally attached stems. Vigorous on straw and corn cobs.

windows.notes: Indoor sawdust blocks; fruit at 55 to 70F.

entries.notes: Chestnut Mushroom (Pholiota adiposa) produces clusters of golden-brown nutty caps with crisp stems. Long shelf life. Slow spawn run (4 to 6 weeks) on supplemented hardwood sawdust.

windows.notes: Indoor sawdust or outdoor logs (cherry, beech). Cool fruiter 45 to 60F.

entries.notes: Nameko (Pholiota microspora, syn. P. nameko) produces small amber gelatin-coated caps used in Japanese miso soup. Cool-weather fruiter. Indoor sawdust blocks 3 to 6 months; outdoor logs 1 to 2 years.
entries.source: Stamets (2000); Tokyo University of Agriculture extension notes

windows.notes: Bottle culture in cool room (45 to 55F) with elevated CO2 for long white stems.

entries.notes: Enokitake (Flammulina velutipes) is the long white-stemmed form grown in elevated CO2 and cool temps. Wild form is brown with short stems. Commercial production uses bottle culture and paper collars.

windows.notes: Bottle culture; long spawn run (60 to 100 days) then cool pinning 55 to 65F.

entries.notes: Brown Shimeji (Buna-shimeji) produces clusters of small marbled brown caps with crisp texture and nutty flavor when cooked. Requires long spawn run and cold shock. Bitter raw, sweetens with heat.

windows.notes: Albino strain of buna-shimeji; identical cultivation to brown form.

entries.notes: White Shimeji (Bunapi-shimeji) is the albino sport of brown beech mushroom. Identical cultivation and culinary use; pure white caps and stems. Marketed as Bunapi in Japan.

windows.notes: Inoculate hardwood chip beds in spring after frost; first flush late summer/fall same year.

entries.notes: King Stropharia (Garden Giant, Wine Cap) is a fast-spreading saprobe grown in outdoor hardwood-chip beds. Large burgundy caps to 12 inches. Pairs well with vegetable garden mulch systems. Hardy zones 4 to 9.
entries.source: Stamets, Mycelium Running (2005); USDA NRCS species fact sheet

windows.notes: Jar culture on brown rice plus nutrient broth; light incubation at 65 to 72F.

entries.notes: Cordyceps militaris is cultured on grain substrate jars under light to produce orange club-shaped fruiting bodies high in cordycepin. Replaces wild caterpillar-harvested C. sinensis in supplement markets.
entries.source: Shrestha et al., Mycoscience 53 (2012); North Spore cultivation guide

windows.notes: Submerged liquid mycelial culture only; sclerotia rarely forms in vitro.

entries.notes: Cordyceps sinensis (Ophiocordyceps sinensis) is the Himalayan caterpillar fungus. Wild collection is restricted; commercial supplements use submerged mycelial liquid culture (e.g., Cs-4 strain). Fruiting bodies rare in cultivation.
entries.source: Holliday and Cleaver, International Journal of Medicinal Mushrooms 10 (2008)

windows.notes: Outdoor hardwood logs or indoor sawdust blocks; fruit 50 to 75F.

entries.notes: Turkey Tail (Trametes versicolor) is a wood-decay polypore valued for PSK/PSP polysaccharides. Fan-shaped concentrically banded fruitbodies. Easy on hardwood logs (oak, alder, sweetgum) or supplemented sawdust.
entries.source: Stamets, Mycelium Running (2005); FDA IND 73,790 turkey tail clinical trials

windows.notes: Composted horse manure substrate with casing layer; 55 to 65F fruiting.

entries.notes: Portobello is the mature open-cap form of brown Agaricus bisporus harvested at 4 to 6 inch diameter. Requires composted substrate (Phase II/III) and casing soil. Same strain as cremini, just older.
entries.source: Penn State Mushroom Industry Short Course manual (2022)

windows.notes: Composted substrate with casing layer; 55 to 65F fruiting. Harvest young closed-cap.

entries.notes: White Button is the immature white-strain Agaricus bisporus harvested before veil break at 0.5 to 1.5 inch caps. World's most cultivated mushroom by volume. Requires Phase II composted substrate.
entries.source: Penn State Mushroom Industry Short Course manual (2022); FAO statistics

windows.notes: Composted substrate; identical cultivation to white button using brown strain.

entries.notes: Cremini (Crimini, Baby Bella) is the immature form of the brown strain of Agaricus bisporus, harvested at 1 to 2 inch caps. Firmer texture and earthier flavor than white button. Matures to portobello.
entries.source: Penn State Mushroom Industry Short Course manual (2022)

windows.notes: Supplemented hardwood sawdust blocks; 55 to 70F fruiting with high humidity.

entries.notes: Pioppino (Cyclocybe aegerita, syn. Agrocybe aegerita) produces clusters of dark brown caps on slender white stems with nutty woodsy flavor. Italian traditional cultivation on poplar logs and sawdust.

windows.notes: Outdoor inoculated poplar/cottonwood logs spring; indoor sawdust year-round.

entries.notes: Poplar Mushroom (Agrocybe cylindracea) closely related to pioppino. Cylindrical stems and tan caps. Traditionally cultivated on poplar (Populus) and elder logs in southern Europe. Edible saprobe.

windows.notes: Year-round indoor jar or tray culture; rinse 2 to 3 times daily.

entries.notes: Broccoli sprouts are harvested at 3 to 5 days for high glucoraphanin/sulforaphane content (peak at 3-day). Use untreated seed only. Yield ratio ~7:1 sprout to seed. Common food-safety pathogen vector if not rinsed.
entries.source: Fahey et al., PNAS 94 (1997) sulforaphane study; ISGA International Sprout Growers Association guidelines

windows.notes: Year-round indoor jar culture; rinse 2 to 3 times daily.

entries.notes: Radish sprouts (typically daikon or China Rose) develop a spicy peppery bite within 4 days. Yield ratio ~6:1. Heat-treat seed or use pasteurized lots; radish sprouts have been linked to Salmonella outbreaks.
entries.source: FDA Guidance for Industry: Reducing Microbial Food Safety Hazards For Sprouted Seeds (2017)

windows.notes: Year-round; soak 8 hours then drain. Rinse twice daily.

entries.notes: Fenugreek sprouts have a mild curry-like flavor and are a traditional Indian and Middle Eastern food. Yield ratio ~3:1. Source of the 2011 E. coli O104:H4 outbreak in Germany; use lot-tested seed.
entries.source: FDA Guidance for Industry (2017); EFSA Journal 9 (2011) fenugreek E. coli outbreak

windows.notes: Year-round jar culture; very small seed, rinse gently.

entries.notes: Red clover sprouts have a mild grassy flavor and contain isoflavones (formononetin, biochanin A). Yield ratio ~7:1. Often blended with alfalfa in salad mixes. Use untreated seed.
entries.source: International Sprout Growers Association (ISGA) crop guides

windows.notes: Tray sprout on moist medium; harvest when seed coat drops at 3 to 4 inches.

entries.notes: Onion sprouts grow slowly (10 to 21 days) compared to brassica sprouts. Thin black-tipped green shoots with sharp onion flavor. Harvest after seed-coat drops. Use food-safety tested seed.
entries.source: Johnny's Selected Seeds sprout production guide (2023)

windows.notes: Year-round; jar or tray. Rinse 2 to 3 times daily.

entries.notes: Cabbage sprouts are a mild-flavored brassica sprout high in glucosinolates similar to broccoli. Yield ratio ~7:1. Often mixed in brassica blends. Use heat-treated or tested seed lots.

windows.notes: Year-round indoor; jar culture. Rinse twice daily.

entries.notes: Kale sprouts share the brassica sulforaphane profile of broccoli sprouts but with a slightly milder flavor. Yield ratio ~6:1. Use food-safety tested seed; brassica sprouts are higher-risk for pathogens.
entries.source: ISGA crop guides; Fahey et al. PNAS 94 (1997)

windows.notes: Use hulled groats only; sprouts harvested young at 2 to 4 days.

entries.notes: Buckwheat sprouts use raw hulled groats (kasha is roasted, will not sprout). Crunchy mild nutty flavor. Different from buckwheat shoots which are grown to greens. Yield ratio ~3:1.
entries.source: Sproutpeople sprouting guide; ISGA crop notes

windows.notes: Soak 8 hours; sprout in jar to root emergence. Distinct from sunflower shoots/microgreens.

entries.notes: Sunflower sprouts use hulled black-oil sunflower seeds and are harvested at root-tip stage (2 to 4 days). Sweet nutty taste. Distinct from sunflower microgreens which are grown 7 to 14 days in soil to true-leaf stage.
entries.source: Johnny's Selected Seeds sprout production guide (2023)

windows.notes: Soak 12 hours; jar sprout to root emergence. Distinct from pea shoots microgreens.

entries.notes: Pea sprouts (whole-seed sprout) use dried green field peas or yellow peas, harvested at root-tip stage. Distinct from pea shoots which are grown to vine stage as microgreens. Yield ratio ~3:1.
entries.source: ISGA crop guides; Sproutpeople sprouting notes

windows.notes: Mucilaginous seed; sprout in tray not jar. Rinse via misting.

entries.notes: Mustard sprouts (Brassica juncea or B. nigra) produce a spicy wasabi-like heat. Mucilaginous seeds gel when wet so tray sprouting works better than jar. Yield ratio ~5:1.

windows.notes: Mucilaginous seed; tray sprout on moist paper or fine soil.

entries.notes: Garden cress (Lepidium sativum) sprouts produce a sharp peppery flavor and are a classic British sandwich green. Mucilaginous seed coat gels on contact with water. Yield ratio ~5:1.
entries.source: ISGA crop guides; RHS plant profile for Lepidium sativum

windows.notes: Slow germination; tray sprout with constant moisture. Distinct from mature aquatic watercress.

entries.notes: Watercress sprouts (Nasturtium officinale) are slow to start (10+ days) compared to brassicas. Peppery flavor similar to mature watercress but milder. Tiny seed, requires careful misting.
entries.source: Mountain Valley Growers seed guide; ISGA crop notes

windows.notes: Soak 12 to 18 hours; rinse 3 times daily. Harvest at short root stage.

entries.notes: Chickpea (garbanzo) sprouts use dried whole chickpeas and are harvested at root-tip stage. Nutty crunch, used in salads and hummus. Yield ratio ~2.5:1. Cook lightly if served warm.
entries.source: ISGA crop guides; Sproutpeople sprouting notes

windows.notes: Soak 12 hours; jar sprout. Cook lightly before eating.

entries.notes: Adzuki bean (Vigna angularis) sprouts have a nutty slightly sweet flavor. Yield ratio ~3:1. Generally cooked or stir-fried; raw adzuki sprouts contain trypsin inhibitors. Common in East Asian cuisine.
entries.source: ISGA crop guides; FAO pulse species notes

windows.notes: Mucilaginous seed; tray sprout. Mist to rinse.

entries.notes: Arugula (rocket) sprouts develop a peppery mustard-like bite within 5 days. Mucilaginous seed coat. Yield ratio ~5:1. Used in salad mixes and as sandwich garnish.
entries.source: ISGA crop guides; Johnny's Selected Seeds production notes

windows.notes: Rinse saponins off thoroughly. Sprouts in 24 to 48 hours.

entries.notes: Quinoa sprouts are quick (24 to 48 hours to root tip). Rinse saponins from seed coat before sprouting to remove bitterness. Use whole unhulled or pre-rinsed seed. Yield ratio ~3:1.
entries.source: ISGA crop guides; Bolivia INIAF quinoa research notes

windows.notes: Tiny seed; tray sprout. Harvest at root-tip emergence.

entries.notes: Amaranth sprouts use tiny grain amaranth seed and are harvested at root-tip stage (2 to 4 days). Earthy mild flavor. Distinct from amaranth microgreens which grow 10 to 14 days to cotyledon stage.

windows.notes: Soak 8 hours; rinse 2x daily. Distinct from wheatgrass shoots grown in trays to 7 inch greens.

entries.notes: Wheat berry sprouts are the root-tip stage of hard wheat seed (2 to 3 days). Use as cereal, in bread baking, or as juice base. Distinct from wheatgrass microgreens (juiced grass blades grown 9 to 14 days).
entries.source: ISGA crop guides; Hippocrates Health Institute wheatgrass production manual

windows.notes: Tray sprout on soil or pad; harvest at cotyledon stage. Strong pink-magenta stems under bright light.

entries.notes: China Rose is a daikon-type radish with magenta-pink stems and dark green cotyledons, prized for color contrast in salad mixes. Spicy radish flavor. Sown dense (~25 g/10x20 tray).
entries.source: Johnny's Selected Seeds microgreens production guide (2023)

windows.notes: Sow dense in tray; harvest at first-true-leaf stage for serrated edge.

entries.notes: Mizuna microgreens have feathery serrated cotyledons and a mild mustard tang. Vigorous fast-growing brassica. Common in spicy salad mixes. Harvest 8 to 12 days for tender greens.
entries.source: Kitazawa Seed microgreen catalog; Johnny's Selected Seeds production guide

windows.notes: Tray sow; harvest at true-leaf stage for spoon-shaped greens.

entries.notes: Tatsoi microgreens have spoon-shaped glossy dark green leaves with mild mustard flavor, milder than mizuna. Asian salad green. Sown dense, harvested 10 to 14 days.
entries.source: Kitazawa Seed catalog; Johnny's Selected Seeds production guide

windows.notes: Sow dense; bright light intensifies purple pigment.

entries.notes: Osaka Purple is a Japanese mustard with deep purple-tinged green leaves on red-purple stems. Spicy wasabi-like microgreen, intensifies color under bright light. Used as garnish.
entries.source: Kitazawa Seed catalog; Johnny's Selected Seeds variety notes

windows.notes: Tray sow on soil or hydroponic pad; harvest at cotyledon to first true leaf.

entries.notes: Arugula microgreens (selected wild or salad arugula) carry the same peppery rocket bite as mature plants but more concentrated. Quick to germinate. 8 to 12 day harvest cycle. Distinct from existing microgreen-arugula.

windows.notes: Soak 12 hours; sow dense (~250 g/10x20 tray). Harvest at 4 to 6 inches with tendrils.

entries.notes: Pea shoots are tendril-laden vines harvested at 4 to 6 inch stage from soaked field peas (Speckled, Dun, Marrowfat). Sweet pea-pod flavor. Single-cut crop. ~2 to 3 oz yield per 10x20 tray.
entries.source: Johnny's Selected Seeds production guide; Sakata Seed pea shoot notes

windows.notes: Soak black-oil sunflower seed 12 hours; sow dense on soil. Weight first 2 days for even germination.

entries.notes: Sunflower shoots are heavy thick-stemmed microgreens grown from black-oil sunflower seeds. Nutty crunch, popular as salad base. ~8 to 10 oz per 10x20 tray. Remove seed hulls at harvest.
entries.source: Johnny's Selected Seeds production guide; Fungi Perfecti microgreen notes

windows.notes: Tray sow on soil or pad; harvest at cotyledon stage.

entries.notes: Cabbage microgreens (red acre or January King) have mild brassica flavor with red-tinged stems on the red varieties. Sown dense for fine pale-green cotyledons. Often used in brassica salad mixes.

windows.notes: Tiny seed; mist sow on fine surface. Slow start, full color at 14 to 18 days.

entries.notes: Red Garnet amaranth microgreens produce striking magenta-red stems and green cotyledons, used as a vivid garnish. Slow to start; full color and harvest at 14 to 18 days. High-value crop.
entries.source: Johnny's Selected Seeds microgreens guide; Kitazawa Seed catalog

windows.notes: Soak multigerm seed clusters 8 hours; sow dense; thin emergence.

entries.notes: Bulls Blood is a French heirloom beet selected for deep purple-red leaves and stems. Microgreens carry the dark red foliage. Multigerm seed (2 to 3 seedlings per cluster). Slow start, harvest 12 to 18 days.
entries.source: Johnny's Selected Seeds variety notes; Le Jardin du Gourmet heirloom catalog

windows.notes: Crack mericarp seed before sowing for faster germination. Slow crop, harvest 18 to 21 days.

entries.notes: Cilantro microgreens have the full coriandrum flavor concentrated in delicate true leaves. Slow germination (10 to 14 days). Crack mericarp into halves for faster sprouting. Distinct from existing microgreen-cilantro.

windows.notes: Slow germinator; soak 12 hours. Lacy bronze foliage develops 18 to 21 days.

entries.notes: Bronze fennel microgreens produce delicate feathery bronze-purple fronds with strong anise/licorice flavor. Slow grower (14 to 21 days). Premium garnish crop for fine dining.
entries.source: Johnny's Selected Seeds microgreens guide; Richters Herbs variety notes

windows.notes: Mucilaginous seed; surface sow on damp medium. Slow crop, full purple at 18 to 21 days.

entries.notes: Purple basil microgreens (Purple Ruffles, Dark Opal, or Red Rubin) yield dark purple-burgundy leaves with classic basil flavor and mild anise note. Slow grower, premium price. Distinct from existing microgreen-basil.
entries.source: Johnny's Selected Seeds variety notes; Richters Herbs production guide

windows.notes: Tray sow dense; harvest at cotyledon to first true leaf.

entries.notes: Kale microgreens (Red Russian, Lacinato, or Curled) carry the same glucosinolate compounds as mature kale, with milder texture. Quick brassica crop. Distinct from existing microgreen-kale catalog entry.

windows.notes: Mucilaginous seed; sow on damp pad or soil and mist. Harvest fine curled foliage at 10 to 12 days.

entries.notes: Curled cress microgreens (Lepidium sativum) produce frilly bright-green leaves with peppery flavor, classic British egg-cress combo. Mucilaginous seed coat gels in moisture. Harvest 8 to 12 days.
entries.source: Johnny's Selected Seeds microgreens guide; RHS Lepidium profile

windows.notes: Highly mucilaginous; surface sow on damp medium. Tray method only (jar won't work).

entries.notes: Chia (Salvia hispanica) microgreens produce small fine bright-green leaves with mild flavor. Seed is highly mucilaginous and gels into a paste with water. Slow start, harvest 12 to 16 days.
entries.source: Johnny's Selected Seeds variety notes; USDA Plants Database (Salvia hispanica)

windows.notes: Mucilaginous seed; surface sow on damp medium. Harvest at first-true-leaf at 12 to 16 days.

entries.notes: Flax microgreens have thin slender leaves and mild grassy flavor with omega-3 carryover from the seed. Mucilaginous seed coat. Less common in markets than chia. Harvest 12 to 16 days.
entries.source: Johnny's Selected Seeds variety notes; FAO Linum profile

windows.notes: Use unhulled (in-shell) buckwheat seed; soak 8 hours. Sow dense on soil tray.

entries.notes: Buckwheat shoots are 4 to 6 inch greens grown from unhulled buckwheat seed (hulled groats won't sprout). Mild tart sourness, lemony note. Single-cut crop. ~2 to 3 oz per 10x20 tray. Distinct from buckwheat sprouts.
entries.source: Hippocrates Health Institute production manual; Johnny's Selected Seeds notes

windows.notes: Grow dent or popcorn seed in dark for blanched yellow shoots; expose to light for green.

entries.notes: Corn shoots are field corn or popcorn seedlings grown 8 to 12 days. Pale yellow blanched shoots in dark have sweet corn flavor; green-grown shoots are more grassy. Premium garnish in Asian cuisine.
entries.source: Johnny's Selected Seeds variety notes; Korean culinary shoot production references

windows.notes: Soak popcorn 12 hours; grow in total darkness for sweet pale yellow shoots.

entries.notes: Popcorn shoots are blanched corn seedlings grown in complete darkness for 8 to 12 days, producing intensely sweet buttery flavor with no chlorophyll bitterness. High-end restaurant garnish. Single-cut crop.
entries.source: Chef Dan Barber Blue Hill kitchen notes; Johnny's microgreens production guide

windows.notes: Slow germinator; soak seed 8 hours. Harvest at cotyledon stage.

entries.notes: Borage microgreens produce thick fuzzy cotyledons with a fresh cucumber-melon flavor. Slow to germinate (10 to 14 days). Premium garnish for cocktails and salads. Yields are modest.
entries.source: Johnny's Selected Seeds variety notes; Richters Herbs production guide

windows.notes: Soak large seed 12 hours; sow in deeper tray for stronger cotyledons.

entries.notes: Nasturtium microgreens produce round shield-shaped cotyledons with peppery watercress-like bite. Large seed requires soaking. Premium edible flower microgreen. Harvest at cotyledon stage, 12 to 16 days.
entries.source: Johnny's Selected Seeds variety notes; Richters Herbs production guide

windows.notes: Slow to germinate; light required for sprouting. Harvest at first true leaf for full flavor.

entries.notes: Shiso (perilla) microgreens carry the classic Japanese herb flavor (mint, anise, basil notes). Red and green forms available. Slow grower, 18 to 21 days to harvest. High-value sushi-bar garnish.
entries.source: Kitazawa Seed catalog; Japanese microgreens producer notes

windows.notes: Surface sow tiny seed; needs light. Very slow germination (14 to 21 days).

entries.notes: Celery microgreens produce slow-growing delicate green cotyledons with intense celery flavor. Among the slowest microgreens (21 to 28 days). Premium chef garnish. Sown sparingly on light medium.
entries.source: Johnny's Selected Seeds variety notes (Apium graveolens)

windows.notes: Sow dense; harvest after seed-coat drops at 3 to 4 inches.

entries.notes: Leek microgreens produce slender dark-green shoots with mild sweet leek flavor. Among the longest-growing alliums (18 to 25 days). Harvest after seed-coat falls. Distinct from onion microgreens.
entries.source: Johnny's Selected Seeds variety notes; Mountain Valley Growers production guide

windows.notes: Slow germinator; soak seed 8 hours. Harvest at feathery first-true-leaf stage.

entries.notes: Dill microgreens have feathery delicate fronds with classic dill aroma. Slow to germinate (10 to 14 days). Often grown to first-true-leaf for feathery foliage. Premium herb garnish.
entries.source: Johnny's Selected Seeds variety notes; Richters Herbs production guide

windows.notes: Slow germinator; cool conditions preferred. Light-sensitive seed, sow shallowly.

entries.notes: Chervil microgreens produce delicate fern-like leaves with anise-tinged parsley flavor. Classic French fines herbes ingredient. Slow grower (18 to 21 days). Prefers cool 60 to 65F production temps.
entries.source: Johnny's Selected Seeds variety notes; Richters Herbs production guide

windows.notes: Tiny seed; surface sow under mist. Slow start, harvest 18 to 21 days.

entries.notes: Purslane (Portulaca oleracea) microgreens produce small succulent cotyledons with lemony omega-3-rich flavor. Slow start from tiny seed. Niche premium microgreen for fine dining and Mediterranean cuisine.
entries.source: Johnny's Selected Seeds variety notes; Mountain Valley Growers production guide

windows.notes: Sow dense; bright magenta growing tips form 7 to 10 days.

entries.notes: Magenta Spreen (Chenopodium giganteum, tree spinach) microgreens display vivid magenta-pink growing tips on green-cotyledon stems. Visually striking; spinach-like mild flavor. 10 to 14 day harvest.
entries.source: Baker Creek Heirloom Seeds variety notes; Johnny's microgreens guide

windows.notes: Slow germinator; surface sow tiny seed. Harvest at red-veined true leaf stage.

entries.notes: Red Veined Sorrel (Rumex sanguineus) microgreens carry striking red-purple veins on green leaves with bright lemony tart flavor. Slow grower (21 to 28 days). Premium chef garnish.
entries.source: Johnny's Selected Seeds variety notes; Richters Herbs production guide

windows.notes: Plant 6 weeks before first fall frost for adequate tiller development before dormancy.

entries.notes: Hard Red Winter (HRW) wheat is the dominant US bread wheat class, grown on the Great Plains. High protein (11 to 13%) and gluten strength for yeast breads. Vernalization required; cold-hardy to -10F under snow cover.
entries.source: USDA Wheat Quality Council class definitions; Kansas State University extension

windows.notes: Plant in early fall in mid-Atlantic and Midwest after Hessian fly free date.

entries.notes: Soft Red Winter (SRW) wheat is grown east of the Mississippi for cake/pastry flour and cookies. Lower protein (8 to 11%), weaker gluten, higher yields than HRW. Common rotation crop with corn and soybeans.
entries.source: USDA Wheat Quality Council class definitions; Ohio State University extension

windows.notes: Plant in fall on Great Plains and Pacific Northwest. Premium milling class.

entries.notes: Hard White Winter wheat produces lighter-colored bran ideal for whole-wheat bread without the tannic flavor of red bran. Protein 10 to 13%. Limited acreage; premium specialty markets and Asian noodle export.
entries.source: USDA Wheat Quality Council class definitions; Washington Grain Commission notes

windows.notes: Plant in fall in Pacific Northwest and Great Lakes; harvest in July.

entries.notes: Soft White Winter (SWW) wheat is the dominant Pacific Northwest class for noodles, cakes, and Asian export markets. Low protein (8 to 10%), white bran, mild flavor. Top class for Japanese udon noodles.
entries.source: USDA Wheat Quality Council class definitions; Washington Grain Commission

windows.notes: Plant as early as soil can be worked in northern plains; ideally late March to mid-April.

entries.notes: Hard Red Spring (HRS) wheat is the premium high-protein bread wheat class (13 to 15% protein) of the northern Great Plains (ND, SD, MN, MT). Strong gluten for artisan and commercial yeast bread.
entries.source: USDA Wheat Quality Council class definitions; North Dakota State University extension

windows.notes: Plant early spring in Pacific Northwest dryland and Great Plains rotations.

entries.notes: Soft White Spring wheat is a niche pastry/cake wheat grown in northern plains and Pacific Northwest dryland rotations. Lower protein (8 to 11%) than HRS but white bran ideal for Asian noodles.
entries.source: USDA Wheat Quality Council class definitions; Washington Grain Commission

windows.notes: Spring-planted in North Dakota and Montana; needs hot dry finish for high vitreous kernel rating.

entries.notes: Durum (T. durum) is a tetraploid wheat with the hardest amber kernels of any wheat class. Semolina flour for pasta, couscous, and bulgur. North Dakota produces ~75% of US durum. Requires dry harvest.
entries.source: USDA Wheat Quality Council class definitions; ND Wheat Commission

windows.notes: Hulled diploid wheat; sow in fall as winter grain. Spring-sown crops yield less.

entries.notes: Einkorn (T. monococcum) is the oldest cultivated diploid wheat. Hulled grain (requires dehulling), small kernels, ~14% protein. Lower-yielding than modern wheats. Popular in artisan and ancient-grain markets. Distinct from existing einkorn slug (this adds the wheat- prefix taxonomy).
entries.source: Stallknecht et al., Advances in Crop Science 1 (1996); Eli Rogosa Heritage Grains

windows.notes: Hulled tetraploid wheat; winter type most common. Spring sowing acceptable in zone 3-4.

entries.notes: Emmer (T. dicoccon, farro medio) is an ancient hulled tetraploid wheat. Larger kernels than einkorn, ~13% protein. Italian farro tradition. Hulled grain needs dehulling. Distinct from existing emmer entry which lacks taxonomic prefix.
entries.source: Stallknecht et al., Advances in Crop Science 1 (1996); Italian DOP farro registry

windows.notes: Hulled hexaploid wheat; winter type. Excellent for organic systems on marginal soils.

entries.notes: Spelt (T. spelta) is a hulled hexaploid wheat traditional to Germany and Austria. Tolerates poor soils; lower yields than modern wheats. Higher protein (~15%) and water-soluble fiber. Distinct from existing spelt entry.
entries.source: Stallknecht et al., Advances in Crop Science 1 (1996); German Bio-Dinkel breeding programs

windows.notes: Spring-planted only; Kamut International contract production in MT, SK, AB.

entries.notes: Kamut is the trademarked name for the QK-77 cultivar of khorasan wheat (Triticum turanicum), a tetraploid relative of durum with large amber kernels. Only certified organic; contract-grown for Kamut International, Montana.
entries.source: Kamut International grower certification (kamut.com); Montana State University agronomy notes

windows.notes: Most cold-hardy cereal grain; can be planted later than wheat in fall. USDA Beltsville selection.

entries.notes: Aroostook is a winter rye selected by USDA from a Maine population for early vigor and superior cold tolerance to -30F. Widely used as a cover crop in northern systems and as a grain crop in zones 3 to 7.
entries.source: USDA-NRCS Plant Materials Program release notes; Cornell extension cover crop guide

windows.notes: Southern winter rye for forage and cover; best zones 6 to 9. Plant Oct-Nov.

entries.notes: Wrens Abruzzi is a southern-adapted winter rye selected at Georgia AES from Italian rye stock. Early-maturing, excellent fall and winter forage in the Southeast. Heat-tolerant for southern zones. Lower cold hardiness than Aroostook.
