entries.source: Georgia Agricultural Experiment Station release notes; USDA-NRCS Plant Materials Program

windows.notes: Southern Plains winter rye; popular cover crop in Oklahoma and Texas wheat rotations.

entries.notes: Elbon is a winter rye selected by Oklahoma State University in 1956 for southern Great Plains adaptation. Excellent fall forage; tolerates root-knot nematode (a benefit for cotton rotation). Often used as a cover crop ahead of summer crops.
entries.source: Oklahoma State University agronomy release notes; SARE cover crop database

windows.notes: Spring-sown in northern plains; standard milling oat in MN, ND.

entries.notes: Jerry is a tall white-hulled milling oat released by North Dakota State University (1990s) with strong straw and high test weight. Reliable for groats, rolled oats, and feed. Widely grown in northern Plains.
entries.source: North Dakota State University NDO release notes; USDA GRIN registry

windows.notes: Spring-sown; hulless type requires gentle threshing to preserve groat integrity.

entries.notes: Streaker is a hulless (naked) oat that threshes free of its hull, leaving clean groats. Higher protein than hulled types (~16%) but slightly lower yield. Popular for direct human consumption and organic markets.
entries.source: University of Saskatchewan Crop Development Centre release notes; Canadian Grain Commission

windows.notes: Spring-sown; northern Minnesota and Wisconsin adaptation.

entries.notes: Northstar is a yellow-hulled milling oat released by Minnesota AES with crown rust resistance and stiff straw. Mid-season maturity. Excellent test weight for milling and feed markets in upper Midwest.
entries.source: Minnesota Agricultural Experiment Station release notes; USDA GRIN

windows.notes: Spring-sown two-row malting barley; AMBA-approved variety in northern Plains.

entries.notes: Conlon is a two-row spring malting barley released by North Dakota State University (1996). Approved by AMBA for craft brewing. Medium-tall plant, good lodging resistance. Replaced Robust in some northern Plains rotations.
entries.source: North Dakota State University NDO release notes; American Malting Barley Association recommended list

windows.notes: Spring-sown six-row barley; classic Minnesota malting variety.

entries.notes: Robust is a six-row spring malting barley released by University of Minnesota in 1983. Dominated US malt production through the 1990s. High yield, good enzyme profile for adjunct brewing. Replaced by Tradition/Lacey in many areas.
entries.source: University of Minnesota AES release notes; AMBA recommended list (historical)

windows.notes: Spring-sown hulless purple-grained heirloom; food-grade specialty crop.

entries.notes: Purple Tibet is a hulless (naked) heirloom barley with deep purple-black grain from the Tibetan plateau. High anthocyanin content. Specialty food-grade crop for tsampa, breads, and craft beer. Lower yields than modern barley.
entries.source: Eli Rogosa Heritage Grains catalog; ICARDA Hordeum landrace collection notes

windows.notes: Fall-sown winter forage triticale; widely used in California and PNW dairy forage systems.

entries.notes: Trical 718 is a winter forage triticale (wheat x rye hybrid) released by Resource Seeds Inc. Vigorous early biomass for silage. Disease resistant. Workhorse forage variety in California dairy rotations.
entries.source: Resource Seeds Inc. (RSI) variety release; UC Davis forage trials

windows.notes: Plant after last frost; frost-sensitive. Quick 70 to 90 day cycle allows summer or late-summer planting.

entries.notes: Koban is a Japanese soba-noodle buckwheat selection with plump kernels and high test weight. Short-season grain ideal for summer cover or grain rotation. Frost-sensitive; needs 70F+ for vigorous flowering.
entries.source: Johnny's Selected Seeds variety notes; National Agricultural Research Center for Hokkaido Region

windows.notes: Canadian-bred large-seeded buckwheat; standard variety in northern grain rotations.

entries.notes: Mancan is a Canadian-bred buckwheat (Manitoba Agriculture, 1974) with large dark seeds and high yields. Widely used in North American buckwheat groat and flour production. Tolerant of cool seasons in northern Plains.
entries.source: Manitoba Agriculture variety release notes; Canadian Grain Commission

windows.notes: Cool-loving; sow in early spring. Daylength-neutral coastal Chilean selection.

entries.notes: Temuco is a coastal Chilean quinoa selection that is daylength-neutral (sets seed regardless of photoperiod), making it adapted to mid-latitude North American production. Mid-tall plant, golden seed, moderate saponin.
entries.source: Wild Garden Seed / Frank Morton breeding notes; Washington State University quinoa trials

windows.notes: Cool-season grain; sow early spring. Wild Garden Seed multicolor open-pollinated mix.

entries.notes: Brightest Brilliant Rainbow is a Wild Garden Seed open-pollinated quinoa mix selected by Frank Morton for vivid yellow, orange, red, pink, and purple seed heads. Ornamental and edible; daylength-neutral.
entries.source: Wild Garden Seed (Frank Morton) catalog; Oregon State University quinoa trial notes

windows.notes: Warm-season grain amaranth; sow after soil warms to 65F. Plant 6 to 8 ft tall.

entries.notes: Burgundy is a Rodale-introduced grain amaranth selection (1970s) with deep burgundy foliage, dark red seed heads, and pale tan grain. Striking ornamental dual-purpose plant. 6 to 8 ft tall, sets seed in mid-Atlantic and Midwest summers.
entries.source: Rodale Research Center introduction (1980s); Seed Savers Exchange listing

windows.notes: High-yielding grain type; large pale gold seed heads. Sow after frost.

entries.notes: Golden Giant is a high-yielding grain amaranth with massive pale gold seed heads producing up to 1 lb of cream-colored grain per plant. Top yields among grain amaranths in trials. Used for popping, flour, and porridge.
entries.source: Rodale Research Center evaluation trials (1980s); Johnny's Selected Seeds catalog

windows.notes: Hopi heritage selection; sow after frost. Dye and grain dual purpose.

entries.notes: Hopi Red Dye is a traditional Hopi amaranth used to color piki bread red and as a grain crop. Deep red-purple flowers and bracts contain natural betalain dyes. Black seed. 5 to 7 ft tall.
entries.source: Native Seeds/SEARCH catalog; Soleri and Cleveland, Economic Botany 47 (1993)

windows.notes: Fast-growing summer millet; tolerates wet feet. Excellent waterfowl forage.

entries.notes: Japanese millet (Echinochloa esculenta) is a fast-growing warm-season annual tolerant of wet soils and shallow flooding. Used for emergency forage, waterfowl food plots, and grain in Asia. 60 to 75 day crop.
entries.source: USDA-NRCS Plant Materials Program; Cornell forage extension notes

windows.notes: Sow after soil warms; dual-purpose forage and grain. Distinct from millet-forage-foxtail catalog entry.

entries.notes: Foxtail millet (Setaria italica) is an ancient grain millet from China with short bristly seed heads. Drought-tolerant. Grain used for porridge and bird seed; hay for cattle. 60 to 85 day crop. Distinct from forage-only foxtail.
entries.source: USDA-NRCS Plant Materials Program; FAO grain millet production notes

windows.notes: Tropical grain; needs warm season. Distinctive finger-like seed heads.

entries.notes: Finger millet (Eleusine coracana, ragi) is a staple grain of East Africa and South India with finger-shaped seed heads. Drought-tolerant, high in calcium (~344 mg/100g). 110 to 140 day crop; needs long warm season.
entries.source: ICRISAT genebank descriptors; FAO Millet Year (2023) reports

windows.notes: Heritage food sorghum from Sierra Madre; sow after soil warms.

entries.notes: Tarahumara is a heritage food sorghum from the Sierra Madre Occidental of Mexico, used by the Raramuri people for porridge and tortillas. Cream-colored grain with low tannin. Drought-tolerant; 100 to 120 days to maturity.
entries.source: Native Seeds/SEARCH catalog; Soleri and Cleveland, Economic Botany 47 (1993)

windows.notes: Sorghum cane for sorghum syrup production; sow after frost.

entries.notes: Black Amber is a sweet cane sorghum traditional in southern Appalachia for sorghum-molasses syrup production. Short maturity (90 to 100 days), heavy juice content, black-purple seed. Crushed and boiled to make syrup.
entries.source: Sand Mountain Research and Extension Center (Alabama); Sorghum Producers Association heritage notes

windows.notes: Tiny seed; surface sow then roll. Heat-loving warm-season grain.

entries.notes: Brown teff is the more common Ethiopian teff color (Eragrostis tef), used for everyday injera flatbread. Higher protein and iron than white teff. Tiny seed (~3000 seeds/g). Warm-season grass; needs 100+ frost-free days.
entries.source: Ethiopian Institute of Agricultural Research teff variety guides; FAO Eragrostis profile

windows.notes: Premium pale-grain teff variety; surface sow tiny seed.

entries.notes: Ivory teff (Magna or Quncho varieties) is the premium pale-grain Ethiopian teff used for high-status white injera. Slightly milder flavor than brown teff. Higher market price in Ethiopia and US export. Warm-season annual.
entries.source: Ethiopian Institute of Agricultural Research teff cultivar registry; The Teff Company Idaho production notes

windows.notes: Tiny tropical seed; surface sow on warm soil. West African heritage grain.

entries.notes: Acha (fonio, Digitaria exilis) is a tiny West African grain cultivated in Sahelian Africa. Drought-tolerant on poor sandy soils. ~70 to 90 day crop. Nutty flavor; nutritionally rich in amino acids (esp. methionine). Gluten-free.
entries.source: Cruz, Ethnobotany Research and Applications 3 (2005); FAO Fonio profile

windows.notes: Photoperiod-sensitive; flowers in short days. Sets seed only in zones 8+ or under cover.

entries.notes: Salba is a white-seeded chia (Salvia hispanica) variety trademarked by Salba Corp, selected for higher oil and protein than wild chia. Short-day flowering means North American grain production limited to southern zones.
entries.source: Salba Corp variety registration; Coates and Ayerza, Industrial Crops and Products 11 (2009)

windows.notes: Spring-sown oilseed flax; very early planting. Standard variety in upper Midwest.

entries.notes: Omega is a Canadian-bred high-omega-3 oilseed flax (Linum usitatissimum) released by Crop Development Centre, University of Saskatchewan. Brown seed, high ALA (alpha-linolenic acid) content for nutraceutical markets.
entries.source: University of Saskatchewan Crop Development Centre variety release notes

windows.notes: Spring-sown golden-seeded flax for milling and direct food use.

entries.notes: Golden flax cultivars (e.g., AC Linora, CDC Bethune) produce pale yellow-gold seed preferred for milling into ground flaxseed and direct food applications. Comparable omega-3 to brown flax but milder flavor and better visual.
entries.source: Agriculture and Agri-Food Canada variety registration (AC Linora); Flax Council of Canada

windows.notes: Cool-season perennial bunch grass; spring or late-summer seeding. Hay crop established year 2.

entries.notes: Alta is a tall late-maturing timothy (Phleum pratense) bred for dairy hay quality in northern US and Canada. Slow to establish. Premium horse and dairy hay; export markets in Japan and Korea. Distinct from existing timothy-grass entry.
entries.source: USDA-NRCS Plant Materials Program; Cornell Cooperative Extension forage notes

windows.notes: Cool-season perennial bunch grass; spring or late-summer seeding. Productive on hayfields and pastures.

entries.notes: Amba is a late-maturing orchardgrass (Dactylis glomerata) variety from Barenbrug, selected for rust resistance and forage quality. Excellent for cool-season hay and pasture in zones 4 to 8. Distinct from existing orchard-grass entry.
entries.source: Barenbrug USA variety release notes; University of Wisconsin forage trials

windows.notes: Sod-forming perennial grass; best spring or late-summer seeding. Slow establishment.

entries.notes: Smooth Brome (Bromus inermis) is a sod-forming cool-season perennial grass widely used for hay and pasture in northern Plains and Canada. Very cold-hardy (zone 2). Spreads by rhizomes. Distinct from existing smooth-bromegrass entry.
entries.source: USDA-NRCS Plant Materials Program; University of Nebraska forage extension

windows.notes: Cool-season perennial; best late-summer seeding for fall establishment. Endophyte-infected (E+) on most lots.

entries.notes: Kentucky 31 is the original tall fescue (Festuca arundinacea) cultivar, dominant on US Transition Zone pastures. Endophyte-infected (E+) lots cause fescue toxicosis in livestock; novel-endophyte alternatives available. Hardy persistent grass.
entries.source: Auburn University release notes (1942); University of Kentucky forage extension

windows.notes: Cool-season fine-bladed turf grass; shade-tolerant. Late-summer seeding ideal.

entries.notes: Creeping Red Fescue (Festuca rubra) is a fine-bladed cool-season turf grass with rhizomatous habit. Most shade-tolerant cool-season turf grass. Major component of northern shade-mix lawn seed and golf course rough.
entries.source: USDA-NRCS Plant Materials Program; USGA Green Section turfgrass notes

windows.notes: Cool-season turf and forage grass; quick establishment. Late-summer or spring seeding.

entries.notes: Perennial ryegrass (Lolium perenne) is the fastest-establishing cool-season grass and a major component of athletic fields, lawns, and intensive dairy pastures. Less heat-tolerant than tall fescue. Bunch grass habit.
entries.source: USDA-NRCS Plant Materials Program; Oregon State University grass seed extension

windows.notes: Annual or short-lived biennial; fall sown for winter forage and cover crop.

entries.notes: Italian ryegrass (Lolium multiflorum) is a fast-growing annual or short-lived biennial grass used for winter cover, overseed of warm-season pastures, and high-quality forage. Quick spring growth. Distinct from existing ryegrass-annual entry.
entries.source: Oregon State University grass seed extension; SARE cover crop database

windows.notes: Slow-establishing cool-season turf grass; late-summer seeding for fall germination.

entries.notes: Kentucky bluegrass (Poa pratensis) is the premium cool-season turfgrass for northern lawns, sports fields, and golf course fairways. Rhizomatous, self-repairing. Slow to establish (3 to 4 weeks germination). Many cultivars available.
entries.source: USDA-NRCS Plant Materials Program; National Turfgrass Evaluation Program (NTEP) trials

windows.notes: Warm-season native bunch grass; late-spring seeding after soil warms. Seed needs cold stratification.

entries.notes: Cave-in-Rock is an upland switchgrass (Panicum virgatum) ecotype from southern Illinois, widely used for biomass, prairie restoration, and warm-season pasture. Vigorous, drought-tolerant; key biofuel feedstock variety. Distinct from existing switchgrass entry.
entries.source: USDA-NRCS Plant Materials Program release notes; Oak Ridge National Laboratory bioenergy trials

windows.notes: Warm-season native; late-spring seeding. Cold-stratified seed germinates better.

entries.notes: Cheyenne Indiangrass (Sorghastrum nutans) is a USDA-NRCS-released ecotype from the southern Great Plains, used for prairie restoration, range seeding, and forage. Bronze plume seed heads in fall. Distinct from existing indiangrass entry.
entries.source: USDA-NRCS Plant Materials Center release notes; Oklahoma State University prairie extension

windows.notes: Warm-season tallgrass prairie species; late-spring seeding into firm seedbed.

entries.notes: Pawnee is a Nebraska USDA-NRCS release of big bluestem (Andropogon gerardii) selected for forage productivity and adaptation to the central Great Plains. Tallgrass prairie keystone species. Distinct from existing big-bluestem entry.
entries.source: USDA-NRCS Plant Materials Center release notes; University of Nebraska extension

windows.notes: Warm-season prairie bunch grass; late-spring drill seeding. Tolerates poor soils.

entries.notes: Aldous is a Kansas USDA-NRCS release of little bluestem (Schizachyrium scoparium) from Flint Hills tallgrass prairie. Drought-tolerant, copper-red fall color. Used for range and conservation seeding. Distinct from existing little-bluestem entry.
entries.source: USDA-NRCS Plant Materials Center release notes; Kansas State University range extension

windows.notes: Warm-season native; drill seed in late spring. Tolerates drought and poor soils.

entries.notes: Haskell is a Texas USDA-NRCS release of sideoats grama (Bouteloua curtipendula) selected from a southern Plains ecotype. Used for range, prairie restoration, and erosion control. Distinctive hanging spikelets along one side of stem.
entries.source: USDA-NRCS Plant Materials Center release notes; Texas A&M AgriLife Extension

windows.notes: Warm-season shortgrass prairie native; drill seed late spring into firm seedbed.

entries.notes: Hachita is a New Mexico USDA-NRCS release of blue grama (Bouteloua gracilis) selected from southern Plains shortgrass prairie. Drought-tolerant, low-growing (12 to 18 inches), characteristic eyelash-shaped seed head. Erosion control and range.
entries.source: USDA-NRCS Plant Materials Center release notes; New Mexico State University extension

windows.notes: Warm-season turf and range native; drill or sod plug in late spring.

entries.notes: Cody is a Nebraska USDA-NRCS release of buffalograss (Bouteloua dactyloides, syn. Buchloe dactyloides) selected for turf use. Drought-tolerant low-water shortgrass prairie native. Female-only plants in cultivar; spread by stolons.
entries.source: USDA-NRCS Plant Materials Center release notes; University of Nebraska Buffalograss Breeding Program

windows.notes: Annual summer forage; sow after soil reaches 65F. Fast biomass for grazing and silage.

entries.notes: Piper is a USDA-released sudangrass (Sorghum sudanense, syn. Sorghum bicolor var. sudanense) selected for low prussic acid content, reducing livestock poisoning risk during regrowth. Fast-growing warm-season annual forage. Distinct from existing sorghum-sudan entry.
entries.source: USDA Beltsville release notes; Cornell Cooperative Extension forage selection

windows.notes: Plant dormant bulbs in autumn for spring emergence; needs wet winter, dry summer.

entries.notes: PNW prairie staple; bulbs slow-roasted for sweet inulin sugars. Caution: avoid Zigadenus (death camas) which has white flowers; only harvest blue-flowered Camassia quamash.
entries.source: USDA NRCS Plant Guide Camassia quamash

windows.notes: Bare-root or container in spring; tolerates wet sites including tidal estuary margins.

entries.notes: Native PNW crab apple; small tart 10-15mm yellow to red fruits used by Coastal First Nations for preserves and traditional storage in water-filled cedar boxes. Cold hardy and shade tolerant.

windows.notes: Acidic soil pH 4.5 to 5.5; shade tolerant; mulch heavily with conifer needles.

entries.notes: PNW understory shrub with glossy evergreen leaves and small dark berries ripening August to November. Slow growing but long-lived; excellent for shaded coastal gardens.
entries.source: Oregon State Univ Landscape Plants Database Vaccinium ovatum

windows.notes: Naturally grows from decaying conifer stumps; needs rich woody organic medium and dappled shade.

entries.notes: Deciduous PNW understory huckleberry; bright translucent red berries with tart-sweet flavor; favorite of varied thrush and Swainson thrush.
entries.source: USDA NRCS Plant Guide Vaccinium parvifolium

windows.notes: Spreads by rhizomes; tolerates dense shade and acidic forest soils.

entries.notes: Dark blue-black berries with hairy texture; traditional First Nations dried-cake food and jam ingredient. Foliage industry harvests leaves for floral greens.
entries.source: USDA NRCS Plant Guide Gaultheria shallon

windows.notes: Drought tolerant once established; sticky resinous leaves used for traditional respiratory tea.

entries.notes: Aromatic resinous shrub; leaves traditionally used as expectorant tea by Indigenous peoples of Oregon and California chaparral.

windows.notes: Notoriously difficult to transplant; direct seed or use very young container stock; mycorrhizal partner required.

entries.notes: PNW evergreen tree with cinnamon peeling bark; small mealy red-orange berries edible raw or processed; traditionally used by Coast Salish peoples.

windows.notes: Riparian shrub; primarily used as native hedgerow and pollinator support; bark traditionally medicinal.

entries.notes: Note: fruits are not consumed; bark and inner cambium used historically in trace medicinal preparations by Coast Salish peoples. Plant primarily for habitat and bee forage.
entries.source: USDA NRCS Plant Guide Physocarpus capitatus

windows.notes: Trailing groundcover in dappled shade; cuttings root easily; aromatic minty leaves.

entries.notes: PNW native mint relative; mild peppermint flavor; traditional Ohlone and Coast Salish tea herb. Original namesake of Yerba Buena, San Francisco.

windows.notes: Plant bulbs in fall or spring; flowers nod downward; harvest bulbs after second year.

entries.notes: Native onion of PNW prairies; mild garlicky-onion flavor; pink umbels are pollinator favorites and edible. Distinct from camas by its onion smell.
entries.source: USDA NRCS Plant Guide Allium cernuum

windows.notes: Plant bulbs in fall on serpentine or gravelly soils; summer dormant.

entries.notes: Pacific coast native onion of dry meadows and serpentine; small white umbels; bulbs traditionally roasted by Indigenous peoples.

windows.notes: Aquatic emergent; plant tubers in 4 to 12 inches of standing water along pond margins.

entries.notes: Starchy underwater tubers; staple food of Lower Columbia tribes; harvested by treading mud to release floating tubers. Plant in still ponds, not running creeks.
entries.source: USDA NRCS Plant Guide Sagittaria latifolia

windows.notes: Slow growing understory conifer; shade tolerant; needs decades to fruit.

entries.notes: CAUTION: only the fleshy red aril is non-toxic; seeds, foliage, and bark are highly toxic (taxine alkaloids). Source of paclitaxel (Taxol). Not for casual home harvest. Note: 10+ years to first fruit; daysToHarvest capped at schema max 3650 days.
entries.source: USDA NRCS Plant Guide Taxus brevifolia

windows.notes: Nitrogen-fixing actinorhizal shrub; needs male and female plants; tolerates saline and alkaline soils.

entries.notes: Northern Plains and intermountain native; tart red berries traditionally whipped into Indian ice cream by Lakota and Blackfoot peoples. Drought and salt tolerant.
entries.source: USDA NRCS Plant Guide Shepherdia argentea

windows.notes: Cultivar Spartan; soil pH 4.5 to 5.2; cross-pollinate with another northern highbush.

entries.notes: Early-season cultivar from USDA-Beltsville; very large light blue berries with excellent flavor and aroma; vigorous upright habit. Bred 1977.

windows.notes: Cultivar Darrow; very large fruit; late mid-season harvest; tart-sweet.

entries.notes: Late-season Darrow cultivar named for Dr. George Darrow USDA; produces unusually large light-blue berries with characteristic firm texture and tart-sweet balance.

windows.notes: Native trailing dewberry; parent of marionberry and many trailing blackberry cultivars.

entries.notes: PNW native; small intensely flavored dark berries; foundational parent of West Coast trailing blackberry industry. Dioecious so plant several for fruit set.
entries.source: USDA NRCS Plant Guide Rubus ursinus

windows.notes: Trellis trailing canes; bears on second-year floricanes.

entries.notes: Thornless selection of classic Boysen cross (raspberry by blackberry by loganberry); large deep-purple fruit with rich winey flavor. Popular for jam and pies.

windows.notes: Scottish raspberry by blackberry hybrid; trellis trailing canes; bears on floricanes.

entries.notes: Bred at Scottish Crop Research Institute 1979 from Aurora blackberry by tetraploid raspberry; long bright red conical fruit with sweet aromatic flavor.
entries.source: James Hutton Institute Tayberry release notes

windows.notes: Trailing canes; trellis at 6 feet; bears on floricanes second season.

entries.notes: Accidental 1881 hybrid by Judge James Logan of Santa Cruz between Aughinbaugh blackberry and Red Antwerp raspberry; tart elongated deep-red fruit ideal for pies and wine.

windows.notes: Bare-root in early spring or fall; mulch heavily; thrives in part shade.

entries.notes: American gooseberry cultivar from 1888 Utah; nearly thornless; large dark red sweet fruit; mildew resistant. Best fresh-eating gooseberry for the American Midwest.

windows.notes: Surface sow with light; needs cold stratification 2 weeks for good germination.
Self-seeds readily in PNW gardens; tolerates cool nights better than basil.

entries.notes: Cool-tolerant alternative to basil for PNW summers; purple-red leaved selections highly aromatic; used in Korean and Japanese cuisine for wraps and tsukemono.
entries.source: Univ of Washington Master Gardener Perilla

windows.notes: Aquaculture only; seed gametophytes onto longlines in 30 to 100 ft cold ocean water.

entries.notes: Marine aquaculture species; fastest-growing organism on earth at up to 2 ft per day. Wild-harvest or seaweed-farm only; not for home gardens. PNW pilot farms in California and Alaska.

windows.notes: Aquaculture only; seed onto lines October to November in cold PNW coastal waters.

entries.notes: Annual PNW bull kelp; long hollow stipe with bulbous float; traditional pickling food of Pacific NW Indigenous peoples; emerging crop for Alaska mariculture.

windows.notes: Tank or longline mariculture; cold North Atlantic and N Pacific species.

entries.notes: Red seaweed cultivated in Maine and Atlantic Canada; smoky bacon-like flavor when pan-fried; Oregon State strain bred for terrestrial tank culture.
entries.source: Oregon State Univ Hatfield Marine Science Palmaria

windows.notes: Tank or pond mariculture; warm subtropical and tropical waters; Hawaii commercial industry.

entries.notes: Red seaweed used in Hawaiian poke and Japanese salads; also primary commercial source of agar. Must process to remove enzyme that can produce toxin in raw consumption.

windows.notes: Plant when soil reliably 70F; tolerant of heat and drought; nitrogen-fixing.

entries.notes: Cream-type cowpea; long pods with zipper-easy hull; cream-colored seeds with no eye; sweet mild flavor preferred in Southern US Sunday peas-and-cornbread.

windows.notes: Heat-loving; double-crop after winter wheat in SE US; harvest pods at silver-pink stage.

entries.notes: Crowder-type Mississippi heirloom; silvery-pink pods with light brown speckled seeds; mild non-foaming flavor. Wilt resistant; AAS 1990 winner.

windows.notes: Trellis to 8 feet; pods grow 18 to 22 inches; deep wine red color holds when cooked.

entries.notes: Striking deep burgundy 18-inch pods; tender when picked young at 12 to 16 inches. Stir-fry standby in Sichuan dry-fried green beans.

windows.notes: Trellis 6 to 8 feet; pick every 2 to 3 days when pods are 14 to 18 inches and pencil-thin.

entries.notes: Green-podded yardlong; SE Asian stir-fry staple; tolerates heat and humidity that overwhelms common green beans. Pods pair well with fermented shrimp paste.

windows.notes: Soak seeds 24 hours; long warm season required (150+ days) for mature sponges.
Trellis vigorously; let fruits dry on vine to brown for sponge harvest.

entries.notes: Edible when young (6 inches); leave on vine 4+ months for mature bath/scrub sponge. SE US commercial crop in zones 8-10.

windows.notes: Pick fruits at 6 to 10 inches while still tender and smooth; trellis vines.

entries.notes: Smooth luffa harvested young as si gua in Chinese cooking; mild zucchini-like flavor; popular in stir-fries and soups across SE Asia.

windows.notes: Compact 3 to 4 ft plants; early-maturing F1; pick pods at 2.5 to 3 inches every 2 days.

entries.notes: Compact early hybrid bred for Louisiana double-cropping; spineless dark-green pods; productive even in shorter SE seasons. AAS 2013 winner.

windows.notes: Burgundy-red 4-inch pods on red-stemmed plants; ornamental and culinary.

entries.notes: 1988 AAS winner from Clemson; deep burgundy red pods and stems; color fades to green when cooked. Pick young at 3 inches for tenderness.

windows.notes: Tall 6 to 8 ft plants; long curved pods can grow 12+ inches and remain tender.

entries.notes: SE US heirloom; unusual long curved cowhorn-shaped pods that remain tender at 7 to 9 inches; deep ribbing; productive over long summers.
entries.source: Southern Exposure Seed Exchange Cowhorn okra

windows.notes: Deep red pods with smooth velvety texture; productive 5 to 6 ft plants.

entries.notes: Beautiful deep red ribbed pods on red stems; sweeter than green okra varieties; pods remain tender longer than Burgundy. Ornamental in kitchen gardens.
entries.source: Baker Creek Heirloom Seeds Red Velvet okra

windows.notes: Sow indoors 8 weeks before last frost; pot up once before transplanting.
Compact 18 inch plants productive even in cooler summers.

entries.notes: Sweet yellow banana-shaped pepper ripening to red; mild thick walls; ideal for pickling, frying, or salads. Heavily productive in zones 5-7.
entries.source: Cornell Vegetable Varieties Hungarian Sweet Banana

windows.notes: Long indoor start needed; bottom heat speeds germination.
Heat-loving; perennial in zone 9+; small upright fruits.

entries.notes: Authentic small upright red Tabasco fruits used for the Louisiana hot sauce; 30,000 to 50,000 SHU; thin walls best for sauces and fermentation rather than fresh eating.
entries.source: Louisiana State Univ AgCenter Tabasco pepper

windows.notes: Spring or fall crop; very cold-hardy heading to 10F with light protection.
Fall crop sweetens after frost; flavor best after light frost in SE US.

entries.notes: Compact 2 to 3 ft heirloom from Virginia Truck Experiment Station; slow-bolting; blue-green smooth leaves; classic Southern collards.
entries.source: Virginia Tech Hampton Roads AREC Vates

windows.notes: Cool weather crop; fast germination; succession every 2 weeks.
Fall crop in SE US tolerates light frost; sweeter after first cold snap.

entries.notes: Large smooth oval leaves 12 to 15 inches; mild peppery flavor; quick growing for braised greens and SE mustard pot likker.

windows.notes: Indeterminate; pinch suckers; stake or cage to 6 ft.
Tolerates humid SE summers better than many heirlooms; disease resistant.

entries.notes: Black Forest German heirloom brought to Mississippi 1800s; round 5oz dusty purple-pink fruits with rich complex flavor; crack and disease resistant in humid climates.
entries.source: Seed Savers Exchange Eva Purple Ball

windows.notes: Indeterminate vines to 6 ft; stake or cage.
Bred for Ozark Mountain heat, humidity, and drought tolerance.

entries.notes: Pre-1900 Arkansas heirloom; medium pink 6 to 8 oz fruits; crack and disease resistant; mild sweet flavor under high heat where many tomatoes fail to set fruit.
entries.source: Univ of Arkansas Cooperative Extension MP-265

windows.notes: Determinate to semi-determinate; stake at planting.
Bred at LSU for Louisiana climate; heat-set tolerant up to 95F.

entries.notes: LSU AgCenter 1969 release; deep red 6 to 8 oz fruits with high acid balance; disease resistance including Fusarium wilt races 1 and 2. Iconic Mississippi Delta variety.

windows.notes: Direct seed in warm soil; long warm season needed; 6 to 10 ft vines.

entries.notes: 1924 Georgia heirloom from Hastings Seeds; round 30 to 40 lb melons with thick light green rind; sweet bright red flesh; excellent shipper and storage. Classic Deep South market melon.
entries.source: Univ of Georgia Extension Heirloom Watermelons

windows.notes: Frost tender tropical legume tree; scarify seeds; fast 12 ft growth first year.

entries.notes: Tropical SE Asian and South Indian leguminous tree; large white edible flowers used in Burmese, Thai, and Indonesian curries; tender pods and leaves also eaten. Nitrogen fixer.

windows.notes: Direct seed or stem cuttings; grow as cut-and-come-again coppice for leaf production.

entries.notes: Drumstick tree managed as leaf crop; high protein leaves with vitamins A and C; staple in Indian sambar, Filipino malunggay soup, and African moringa powder. Drought tolerant.

humid_subtropical.notes: Year-round leaf harvest in zones 9 plus; cut back to 4 ft in late winter for tender flush. The defining Florida edible-tree leaf vegetable.

arid.notes: Drought-tolerant African / Indian native; thrives in desert SW heat. Drip-irrigate during establishment year; minimal irrigation thereafter.

continental.notes: Annual in continental zones; start indoors 8 weeks before last frost. Harvest leaves at 4 to 6 ft height by September.

maritime.notes: Cool maritime summers do not produce reliable yields; choose for greenhouse only. Annual in unheated culture; struggles below 70F.

windows.notes: Propagate from 12 inch stem cuttings; drought tolerant once established.

entries.notes: Maya staple leafy green; MUST be cooked at least 5 minutes to inactivate cyanogenic glycosides; not eaten raw. Sweeter cultivars Estrella and Chayamansa are spineless and preferred.

humid_subtropical.notes: Tender perennial shrub spinach substitute; cut back to 3 ft annually for tender flush. WARNING: Eat only cooked, never raw, due to hydrocyanic glycosides.

arid.notes: Drought-tolerant Yucatan native; drip-irrigate during establishment. Pruning forces tender leaves for the kitchen.

maritime.notes: Greenhouse-only in cool maritime; tender to frost. Grow as a container shrub overwintered indoors.

windows.notes: Cuttings root easily in moist warm soil; semi-shade preferred.

entries.notes: Tropical perennial leafy shrub; tender shoots have a fresh pea-like flavor; popular in Vietnamese and Indonesian cuisine. Consume cooked and in moderation only; not for raw bulk consumption.
entries.source: Univ of Florida IFAS Katuk Sauropus androgynus

windows.notes: Cuttings root easily; grows as trailing perennial in zone 9+; overwinter indoors elsewhere.

entries.notes: SE Asian perennial leafy green; mild succulent leaves with grassy-nutty flavor; eaten fresh or in stir-fries. Very productive cut-and-come-again in subtropical climates.
entries.source: Univ of Florida IFAS Gynura procumbens

windows.notes: Surface sow; heat-loving leafy green; pick leaves at 4 to 6 inches for tender baby leaf.

entries.notes: Brilliant green, red, and yellow tricolor leaves; SE Asian staple as bayam, Caribbean callaloo, and Indian thotakura; cook like spinach. Heat tolerant where spinach bolts.
entries.source: Univ of Florida IFAS HS1257 Amaranth

windows.notes: Direct seed shallowly; succession every 3 weeks for continuous baby-leaf harvest.

entries.notes: Deep red-purple leaves with mild earthy flavor; doubles as grain crop if allowed to mature; African and Caribbean staple. Color leaches red into rice when stewed.
entries.source: Univ of Florida IFAS HS1257 Amaranth

windows.notes: Scarify seeds; drought tolerant deep taproot; nitrogen fixer for Sonoran Desert agroforestry.

entries.notes: Sonoran Desert mesquite; sweet pods milled into low-glycemic flour with caramel-mocha flavor. Traditional food of Tohono Oodham and Pima Akimel Oodham peoples.
entries.source: USDA NRCS Plant Guide Prosopis velutina

windows.notes: Riparian Sonoran and Chihuahuan Desert tree; scarify seeds; tolerates alkaline saline soils.

entries.notes: Spiral-coiled pods cooked into sweet syrup or ground for flour; traditional food of Pueblo peoples and Cahuilla. Smaller than velvet mesquite and less common in commerce.
entries.source: USDA NRCS Plant Guide Prosopis pubescens

windows.notes: Heat and drought tolerant grain amaranth; large red-purple seed heads.

entries.notes: Traditional Mayo people grain amaranth from Sonora Mexico; large compact seed heads with white seeds; staple of Sonoran-style alegria sweets and posole.
entries.source: Native Seeds SEARCH Mayo Indian amaranth

windows.notes: Mexican grain amaranth; brilliant red plumes; tolerates heat and drought.

entries.notes: Zapotec heirloom from Juchitan Oaxaca; tall red-purple plants; high-yielding grain amaranth processed into popped puffed cereal and atole. Drought hardy.

windows.notes: Drill in fall after monsoon end; matures by May in Sonoran Desert.

entries.notes: Soft white spring wheat brought by Padre Kino 1697 to Sonora and Arizona missions; signature flour for Sonoran flour tortillas; drought-adapted heritage grain.
entries.source: Native Seeds SEARCH White Sonora Wheat

windows.notes: Fall-sown wheat for low-elevation Sonoran flood-irrigation systems.

entries.notes: Heritage club wheat (T. aestivum compactum) once grown by Pima Akimel Oodham; short compact spikes; very soft low-protein flour ideal for thin tortillas and pinole.
entries.source: Native Seeds SEARCH Pima Club Wheat

windows.notes: Plant with summer monsoon rains; extremely drought tolerant; matures on single deep irrigation.

entries.notes: Brown-seeded tepary; lower glycemic than common bean; nutty earthy flavor; traditional Tohono Oodham bean grown on as little as 4 inches annual rainfall.

windows.notes: Extreme heat tolerance; productive at 110F where common beans fail.

entries.notes: Small white tepary bean from Sonoran Desert; mild sweet flavor; Slow Food Ark of Taste passenger; signature ingredient of Tohono Oodham community kitchens.

windows.notes: Heat-tolerant pole lima; vines climb 8 ft; harvest dry beans for storage.

entries.notes: Native Seeds SEARCH heirloom from Sonora; medium yellow-tan seeds with russet mottling; rich buttery flavor; tolerates Arizona summer heat better than most limas.
entries.source: Native Seeds SEARCH Yellow Mottled Lima

windows.notes: Bush habit; drought tolerant; matures dry seed in 12 to 14 weeks.

entries.notes: Pre-Columbian Anasazi-style bean recovered from cliff dwellings near Aztec NM; small tan seeds with maroon speckles; rich pot bean flavor; quick-cook no-soak.
entries.source: Native Seeds SEARCH NM Cave Bean

windows.notes: Plant pads laid flat; let cut surface callus 1 to 2 weeks before planting.

entries.notes: Mexican commercial tuna fruit cultivar with reddish-purple to greenish flesh; sweet melon-like flavor; major fruit crop in Mexico and Sicily. Handle with leather gloves for glochids.

windows.notes: Select spineless cultivars (Burbank, Mexican Smooth); harvest young pads in spring.

entries.notes: Tender young pads harvested for nopalitos; lemony-grassy flavor; staple in Mexican breakfast huevos con nopales. Different culinary cultivar than fruit-bearing tuna types.

windows.notes: Native Sonoran and Chihuahuan species; very drought hardy; pads spiny.

entries.notes: Native SW US prickly pear; deep magenta tunas with intense fruit flavor; traditional food of Apache, Pima, and Pueblo peoples. Hardier than O. ficus-indica.
entries.source: USDA NRCS Plant Guide Opuntia engelmannii

windows.notes: Drought-adapted; needs Tegeticula moth for seed set but propagates from offsets.

entries.notes: Chihuahuan Desert species; tender flower stalks and large white flowers eaten by Apache and Mescalero peoples; root pounded for soap. State flower of New Mexico.
entries.source: USDA NRCS Plant Guide Yucca elata

windows.notes: Sonoran Desert keystone species; takes 40+ years to first flower; protected in AZ.

entries.notes: Wild-harvest only; Tohono Oodham bahidaj harvest in June with traditional kuipad pole. Sweet red pulp made into syrup, jam, and ceremonial wine. Not for casual cultivation outside AZ. Note: 10+ years to first fruit; daysToHarvest capped at schema max 3650 days.
entries.source: Univ of Arizona Cooperative Extension AZ1218

windows.notes: Plant offsets called hijuelos; well-drained volcanic soils; full sun.

entries.notes: Source of tequila; 7 to 10 year cycle to pina (heart) harvest. Commercial production restricted to Jalisco DOC region; ornamental in US Southwest gardens.

windows.notes: Cold-hardy agave to 10F; well-drained gravelly soils; full sun.

entries.notes: Native SW US agave; roasted hearts traditional Apache and Mescalero food; mescal-style distillates produced in small batches. Cold-hardiest of culinary agaves. Note: 10+ years to first fruit; daysToHarvest capped at schema max 3650 days.
entries.source: USDA NRCS Plant Guide Agave parryi

windows.notes: Slow-growing Sonoran nurse tree; scarify seeds; very long-lived.

entries.notes: Sonoran nurse tree producing pods with peanut-flavored edible seeds; traditional Seri and Tohono Oodham food. Indicator species for frost-free Sonoran Desert. Note: 10+ years to first fruit; daysToHarvest capped at schema max 3650 days.
entries.source: Univ of Arizona Cooperative Extension AZ1218

windows.notes: Short-day-flowering; traditional cultivars only set seed in tropical latitudes.

entries.notes: Traditional Mesoamerican chia; Aztec staple recovered through Mexican smallholders; very high omega-3 content. US production now relies on day-neutral selections.

windows.notes: Dioecious; plant 1 male per 5 to 10 females; tolerates saline alkaline soils.

entries.notes: Sonoran shrub harvested for wax-rich seeds used in cosmetics; not a culinary food but commercial agricultural crop in AZ and CA. Seeds contain unpalatable simmondsin.
entries.source: Univ of Arizona Cooperative Extension Jojoba AZ1187

windows.notes: Plant offshoots; needs 4000+ degree-days above 64F for fruit ripening; low humidity at harvest.

entries.notes: Classic large soft date; Moroccan origin, dominant in Bard Valley CA and Coachella Valley. Caramel-honey flavor; about 70 percent sugar by weight; harvest September to October.

windows.notes: Plant offshoots; semi-dry date; tolerates more humidity than Medjool at harvest.

entries.notes: Date of the light; Algerian origin; firm semi-dry date with translucent flesh and delicate nutty flavor; principal commercial date variety by tonnage in California.

windows.notes: Self-fertile; tolerates alkaline desert soils; needs hot dry summers for best fruit.

entries.notes: Very low-acid cultivar with sweet aril juice and almost-clear flesh; pale pink skin. Ripens early August in low desert; good for fresh eating.
entries.source: Univ of California ANR Pomegranate Cultivars

windows.notes: Plant in dry-farmed Tohono Oodham field after first summer monsoon rains.

entries.notes: Tohono Oodham heirloom melon; greenish-yellow flesh with mild sweet flavor when fully ripe; dry-farmed in Sonoran fields with monsoon rains. Slow Food Ark of Taste.

windows.notes: Mediterranean tree; needs male pollinator near female cultivars for pod set.

entries.notes: Mediterranean drought-hardy legume tree; sweet pods ground into chocolate substitute powder; seed is original source of carat weight unit. Major commercial crop in Spain and Cyprus.

windows.notes: Plant 12-inch stem cuttings horizontally or angled; harvest tubers at 8 to 12 months.

entries.notes: Caribbean and tropical staple starchy root; MUST be cooked (boiled, fried, fermented) to remove cyanogenic glycosides; never eaten raw. Sweet and bitter cultivars require different processing.

windows.notes: Tropical legume shrub; nitrogen-fixing; ratoon crops for 2 to 3 years in frost-free climates.

entries.notes: Indian and Caribbean staple legume; gandules in Puerto Rican cooking, toor dal in South Indian cuisine; both green and dry seed used. Drought-tolerant once established.

windows.notes: More drought tolerant than M. oleifera; ground tuber on first-year seedlings is also edible.

entries.notes: Ethiopian highland moringa; larger leaves and milder flavor than M. oleifera; staple cabbage-like vegetable for Konso and Gamo peoples of southern Ethiopia.

windows.notes: Drought-hardy native cucurbit; large tuberous taproot; vines sprawl 20 ft.

entries.notes: SW US native cucurbit; seeds high in oil and protein; gourds and roots used by Pueblo peoples as soap. Bitter fruit not for fresh eating; primarily oilseed and traditional cleanser.
entries.source: USDA NRCS Plant Guide Cucurbita foetidissima

windows.notes: Cool-season leafy green; harvest at 4 to 6 inches before flowering for tender leaves.
Fall succession crop bolts slower in cooling weather.

entries.notes: Japanese cool-season green used in shabu shabu and nabe hotpots; complex herbal-bitter chrysanthemum flavor. Bolts quickly in hot weather. Also called tong ho in Chinese cuisine.

windows.notes: Late summer to early fall sowing; overwinters with light cover in zones 5+.

entries.notes: French winter salad green; small tender nutty rosettes; harvests possible at 30F under low tunnels. Standard fall and winter rotation crop in market gardens.

maritime.notes: Premier winter green in mild maritime climates; sow Sep through Nov for a Jan to Mar harvest. Slug pressure is the main risk; copper tape around beds.

mediterranean.notes: Sow Oct through Dec for cool-season winter and early-spring greens. Surface-sow into firmly prepared bed; germination needs cool soil under 60F.

continental.notes: Sow in fall under low tunnel for overwinter harvest; protected by snow cover, mache survives 0F. Direct sow in early spring under tunnel for Mar harvest.

humid_subtropical.notes: Cool-season only; sow Oct through Dec for a Dec through Feb salad green. Summer impossible; heat above 75F bolts the plant immediately.

windows.notes: Cold-hardy salad green; cut-and-come-again; tolerates 20F under row cover.
Excellent winter salad in unheated hoophouse.

entries.notes: Italian winter salad; thin antler-shaped leaves with slightly salty mineral flavor; star ingredient in mesclun mixes. Very cold tolerant for unheated winter production.

windows.notes: Fresh seed only (loses viability in 6 months); soak seeds before sowing.

entries.notes: Italian seasonal salad green; thin grassy succulent shoots with bright saline mineral flavor; brief 4 to 6 week window in spring. Tolerates saline soils where most crops fail.

windows.notes: Komatsuna x cabbage cross; cut-and-come-again at 4 to 6 inch leaves.
Fall and winter standby in unheated hoophouse.

entries.notes: Japanese F1 hybrid green; tender round-leaved cabbage-mustard cross with mild sweet flavor; cooks down quickly. Slow-bolting and very productive cut-and-come-again.

windows.notes: Forms beautiful flat dark green rosette in cool weather; tolerates 15F under row cover.

entries.notes: Flat-rosetted Asian green; thick dark green spoon-shaped leaves with mild mustard flavor; harvest whole rosette or pick outer leaves. Very cold tolerant for winter harvests.

windows.notes: Heads at 4 to 6 inches; light green stems vs ivory of standard pak choi.
Fall and overwinter crop; slow-bolting.

entries.notes: Compact green-stemmed Shanghai-type bok choy; tender spoon-shaped leaves and crisp celadon-green stems; mild sweet flavor; ideal stir-fry size at 6 inches.

windows.notes: Tiny 3 to 4 inch heads in 30 days; excellent for containers and microgreen pot culture.

entries.notes: Miniature pak choi reaching only 3 to 4 inches at maturity; ideal for high-density baby head harvests; popular Asian market variety. Productive year-round in cool conditions.
entries.source: Johnny Seeds Mei Qing Choi technical sheet

windows.notes: Large dark green savoyed rosettes; cold tolerant to 15F under row cover.

entries.notes: Japanese cool-season rosette green; large heavily savoyed dark leaves with thick white midribs; mild flavor between tatsoi and bok choy; star of fall and winter market sales.
entries.source: Johnny Seeds Yukina Savoy technical sheet

windows.notes: Harvest flowering stems just as yellow buds open; cut-and-come-again.
Fall succession; flowering stems tenderer in cool weather.

entries.notes: Chinese flowering brassica; harvest tender flowering shoots with yellow buds; sweet mild flavor; staple of Cantonese stir-fries with oyster sauce.

windows.notes: Like mizuna but with smooth strap-shaped leaves; cut-and-come-again every 3 weeks.

entries.notes: Japanese cousin of mizuna; long smooth strap-like leaves vs feathery mizuna; mild mustardy flavor; traditional pickle ingredient in Kyoto cuisine.

windows.notes: Cold-hardy Finnish and Russian cultivars (Ben Lomond, Titania) selected for arctic latitudes.

entries.notes: Northern blackcurrant selections hardy to zone 2; intensely aromatic dark berries; high vitamin C; staple of Scandinavian and Russian preserves. Check local quarantine laws for white pine blister rust.
entries.source: Univ of Saskatchewan Fruit Program Ribes

windows.notes: Low groundcover raspberry; needs cool peaty soils; cross-pollination for fruit set.

entries.notes: Circumpolar low-growing raspberry; small intensely aromatic pink berries with flavor of pineapple and rose; cultivated in Finnish gourmet liqueurs. Slow to establish.
entries.source: Univ of Alaska Fairbanks Cooperative Extension PMC-00744

windows.notes: Acidic peat bog specialist; dioecious; plant rhizome divisions in spring.

entries.notes: Subarctic peatland fruit; amber-orange berries with rich apricot-honey flavor; revered in Nordic cuisine. Very difficult to cultivate; primarily wild-harvest from sphagnum bogs.
entries.source: Univ of Saskatchewan Fruit Program Rubus chamaemorus

windows.notes: Low evergreen circumpolar shrub; acidic peaty soils; ericaceous fertilizer.

entries.notes: Circumpolar tundra shrub; small black berries with mild slightly resinous flavor; traditional Sami and Inuit fresh and frozen food. Holds berries through winter for late-season harvest.
entries.source: USDA NRCS Plant Guide Empetrum nigrum

windows.notes: Surface sow; needs light; 14 to 21 days germination at 65F.
Day-neutral fruiting all season; bird-resistant due to pale color.

entries.notes: Pale yellow alpine fraise des bois; intensely sweet pineapple-strawberry aroma; birds ignore due to non-red color; harvest June through frost. No runners; clump-forming.

windows.notes: Surface sow with light; cold stratify seeds for 2 weeks for higher germination.
Clump-forming; day-neutral fruiting all season; no runners.

entries.notes: Classic French alpine strawberry; small intensely flavored conical red berries; superior aroma to most strawberries; ideal for borders and edging. Tolerates light shade.

windows.notes: Dwarf bushy 12-inch form; mix of yellow, orange, salmon, red flowers.

entries.notes: Compact mounding mix with edible peppery flowers and leaves; classic mix for kitchen borders. Bush habit holds blooms upright for cut-flower use. Self-seeds reliably.

windows.notes: Dwarf bushy 12-inch plants; deep blue-green leaves with crimson flowers.

entries.notes: 1884 heirloom from Carter Seeds; striking blue-green leaves with intense scarlet-crimson flowers; spicy peppery edible blooms with high color saturation. Excellent in salads.

windows.notes: Dwarf 10 to 12 inch bushy plants; cream and green marbled leaves.

entries.notes: AAS 1964 winner; striking cream and green variegated foliage with bright orange, yellow, salmon, and red flowers. Foliage ornamental and edible; leaves milder than green types.

windows.notes: Climbing or trailing vines to 8 ft; trellis or let drape from window boxes.

entries.notes: Vining climbing nasturtium with intense scarlet-red flowers; vigorous twining habit perfect for arbors, trellises, and as edible vertical screening; flowers and leaves both edible.

windows.notes: Surface sow; needs darkness; 10 to 14 days germination at 65F.
Frost tolerant; self-seeds prolifically.

entries.notes: Tricolor purple-yellow-white viola; classic heirloom edible flower; mild wintergreen flavor; self-seeds widely. Use whole flower on salads, desserts, and as ice cube garnish.

windows.notes: Cool-season; surface sow with light; flower in 12 to 14 weeks from seed.
Frost-tolerant; can overwinter zones 6+.

entries.notes: Compact F1 series; small 1.5-inch faces in wide color range; cold tolerant; ideal for fall planting in zones 6-9 for spring bloom. Flowers edible with mild lettuce-like flavor.

windows.notes: Surface sow; cover lightly; 14 days germination at 60 to 65F.
Long blooming; performs through summer in cool climates.

entries.notes: Classic tricolor viola in purple, lavender, and yellow; long bloom season; edible flowers and leaves; ideal for crystallizing into sugar-flowers and topping salads.

windows.notes: Direct seed after light frost danger; self-seeds.

entries.notes: White-flowered selection of common borage; star-shaped pure white edible flowers with cucumber flavor; pairs beautifully with traditional blue borage in pollinator gardens.

windows.notes: Heat tolerant strain of calendula bred for California cut-flower market.

entries.notes: Heat-tolerant calendula mix in yellow, gold, orange, and apricot; long 2-foot stems suitable for cutting; edible petals as poor mans saffron and for golden tea.

windows.notes: Selected for high resin content; semi-double pale yellow to orange blooms.

entries.notes: Bred for maximum resin yield for herbalist salves and tinctures; pale yellow to deep orange semi-double flowers; staple of medicinal herb gardens; flowers also edible.

windows.notes: Double flowers with maroon undersides; outstanding for cut flowers and culinary.

entries.notes: Double flowers showing apricot, pink, and yellow tops with striking maroon undersides; petals edible and stunning in salads; popular for floral arrangement.

windows.notes: Cool-season annual; direct seed in fall or early spring; thin to 6 inches.

entries.notes: Classic intense blue bachelors button; tall 30 to 36 inch stems for cutting; sterile edible flowers used in tea blends and salads; long vase life. Also great for pressed-flower craft.

windows.notes: Direct sow in cool soil; deep maroon-black flowers on 30-inch stems.

entries.notes: Striking near-black deep maroon double flowers; tall sturdy stems for cutting; petals edible with mild grassy flavor; statement flower in floral mixed arrangements.

windows.notes: Cover lightly; 12 to 14 days germination at 65F.
Cool weather lover; performs through summer in zones 6-7.

entries.notes: Single-flowered Chinese pinks in red, pink, white, and bicolors; gently clove-spicy edible petals; staple of Victorian salads and floral sugar decorations. Remove white bitter heel before eating.

windows.notes: Perennial; well-drained alkaline soil; full sun; deadhead for repeat bloom.

entries.notes: Classic English perennial pinks; fringed pink to white flowers with intense clove fragrance; petals candied for cake decoration and infused into clove pink syrup; long-lived border plant.

windows.notes: Tropical; short-day flowering after autumn equinox; needs long warm season.
Frost tender; harvest calyces 10 days after petal drop.

entries.notes: Source of hibiscus tea, agua de jamaica, and roselle jam; harvest fleshy red calyces after flowers fade. Young leaves also edible as sour leafy green. Tropical Sudan origin.
entries.source: Univ of Florida IFAS HS1374 Roselle

windows.notes: Striking maple-leaf burgundy foliage; pinch back to promote bushy growth.

entries.notes: African ornamental edible; deep maroon-burgundy maple-shaped leaves with tart cranberry-like flavor; eaten cooked or in salads; brews into deep red tea. Hardy perennial in zone 9+.

windows.notes: Plant divisions in spring or fall; full sun; rebloomer.

entries.notes: Classic golden yellow rebloomer; buds and fully open flowers are edible. Important caveat: only Hemerocallis; do not confuse with toxic Lilium or Hosta. Some individuals show GI sensitivity to daylily.

windows.notes: Ditchlily orange daylily; vigorous spreader by rhizome.

entries.notes: Common orange ditchlily; tender buds known as gum jum in Chinese cuisine; used in hot and sour soup. Important caveat: only Hemerocallis; some individuals show GI sensitivity. Cook buds.

windows.notes: Early-blooming species; intensely fragrant lemon-yellow flowers.

entries.notes: Earliest blooming daylily species; intensely lemon-scented bright yellow flowers; tender buds edible. Important caveat: only Hemerocallis; some individuals show GI sensitivity. Cook buds.

windows.notes: Bare root in dormancy; full sun; long-lived once established.

entries.notes: Classic rose for rosewater and attar; petals harvested at dawn for distillation; small orange hips also high in vitamin C; principal rose of Bulgarian Rose Valley.

windows.notes: WARNING: invasive species in much of eastern US; check state list before planting.

entries.notes: Caution: invasive in much of eastern US and prohibited in many states. Small bright red hips clustered along canes; high vitamin C; foraged rather than planted. Verify legal status locally.

windows.notes: Plant in well-drained alkaline soil; full sun; deadhead regularly.

entries.notes: Classic clove-fragrant carnation; petals edible (remove bitter white heel) with sweet-spicy flavor; historic ingredient in Chartreuse liqueur and crystallized in Victorian confectionery.

windows.notes: Use bolted broccoli flowers; small yellow florets harvest as side shoots open.

entries.notes: Tender yellow florets from bolting broccoli plants; mild brassica-honey flavor; salad garnish and stir-fry ingredient; harvest in early morning when fully open. Pollinator favorite.

windows.notes: Allow plants to bolt; harvest unopened buds and just-opened flowers.

entries.notes: Bright yellow mustard flowers and unopened buds; sharp peppery wasabi-like flavor; garnish for sushi, salads, and braises; can be lightly pickled or fermented.
entries.source: Univ of Florida IFAS Mustard greens

windows.notes: Allow bolting plants to flower; harvest small cream-colored flowers with dark veining.

entries.notes: Cream and purple-veined flowers on bolted arugula; intensely peppery sesame-mustard flavor (often stronger than leaves); striking salad garnish and pizza topping.

windows.notes: Use edible-pod pea cultivars only; ornamental sweet pea (Lathyrus) is toxic and must not be substituted.

entries.notes: Important caveat: only Pisum sativum garden pea blossoms are edible; ornamental sweet pea (Lathyrus odoratus) is TOXIC. Pisum flowers have mild fresh pea flavor; salad and crudite garnish.

windows.notes: Bare root in dormancy; harvest fully open umbels on sunny mornings for cordial.

entries.notes: Cream-colored umbels from European elder; use only ripe flowers and cooked ripe berries; raw berries and all green parts are toxic. Base of St Germain liqueur and elderflower cordial.

windows.notes: Plant container trees in spring; large evergreen Southern landmark tree; long-lived.

entries.notes: Large white fragrant petals with spicy citrus-ginger-cardamom flavor; pickled by Asian chefs and used in modernist cuisine. Harvest just-opened petals; older petals turn bitter.
entries.source: Univ of Florida IFAS Magnolia grandiflora

windows.notes: Plant container or bare root trees in spring; understory native; fast growth.

entries.notes: Magenta-pink legume flowers bloom along bare branches before leaves; sweet pea-like flavor; eaten fresh in salads or fried in batter; young pods also briefly edible.
entries.source: USDA NRCS Plant Guide Cercis canadensis

windows.notes: Plant bare root or container trees; ten or more years to flowering; long-lived urban tree.

entries.notes: Yellow honey-scented summer blossoms with attached bracts; classic European tisane (tilleul, lipa); calming and slightly sweet; harvest at peak fragrance in morning, dry quickly in shade.

windows.notes: WARNING: invasive species in much of US; check state regulations before planting.

entries.notes: Caution: invasive across much of eastern and central US. Sweet nectary white-to-yellow flowers; petals and nectar edible; berries TOXIC. Forage from existing patches; do not plant in invaded regions.

windows.notes: Nitrogen-fixing pioneer tree; full sun; suckers aggressively from roots.

entries.notes: Caution: only the white pendant flower racemes are edible; all other parts of black locust (bark, leaves, pods, seeds) are TOXIC. Sweet vanilla-orange-blossom flavor; battered and fried in Italian fritters.
entries.source: USDA NRCS Plant Guide Robinia pseudoacacia

windows.notes: Spring-seeded; inoculate seed with Sinorhizobium meliloti for nitrogen fixation.

entries.notes: Public USDA winter-hardy alfalfa from Wisconsin; 1953 release; very cold hardy; standard reference variety for northern US hay production; tolerates winters to zone 3.

windows.notes: Grazing-tolerant variety; lower crown for grazing pressure; rotate paddocks.

entries.notes: Pasture-bred alfalfa with grazing tolerance and persistence; lower crown architecture withstands hoof traffic better than hay-type varieties; good fit for rotational grazing systems.
entries.source: Dairyland Seed Magnum series technical sheet

windows.notes: Grazing-tolerant cultivar; allow 30 day rest periods between grazings.

entries.notes: Grazing-bred alfalfa selected for high stand persistence under continuous and rotational grazing; deeper roots and higher carbohydrate reserves; good fit for organic dairy operations.
entries.source: Univ of Wisconsin Extension A2933 Alfalfa

windows.notes: Frost-seeding into existing pasture in late winter; or drill in spring.

entries.notes: Single-cut tall biennial red clover; larger and later-flowering than medium red; high biomass; standard nitrogen-fixing green manure; better for cover crop than for forage cuttings.
entries.source: Univ of Wisconsin Extension A3500 Red Clover

windows.notes: Frost-seed in late winter or drill 15 lb per acre into pasture; allows 2 to 3 cuttings per year.

entries.notes: Standard double-cut red clover for hay and pasture; allows 2 to 3 harvests per year; persistent for 2 to 3 years; classic Midwest hay legume.
entries.source: Univ of Wisconsin Extension A3500 Red Clover

windows.notes: Frost-seed or drill at 3 to 5 lb per acre; longer-lived than common white clover.

entries.notes: Large-leaved giant white clover from Italian Lombardy origin; persistent perennial in pasture mixes; excellent forage quality; high protein for grazing dairies.

windows.notes: Drill at 3 to 5 lb per acre; very stoloniferous; thrives under intensive grazing.

entries.notes: Intermediate-leaved Kiwi pasture cultivar; excellent persistence under intensive rotational grazing; strong nitrogen contributor to perennial pasture systems.
entries.source: AgResearch NZ White Clover Variety Notes

windows.notes: Drill at 10 lb per acre; biennial flowers in second year; powerful taproot opens compaction.

entries.notes: Yellow biennial Melilotus; deep taproot subsoiler; excellent honey plant; produces high biomass green manure in second year. Caution: moldy hay produces coumarin-related anticoagulants.
entries.source: USDA NRCS Plant Guide Melilotus officinalis

windows.notes: Drill at 10 lb per acre; taller than yellow type; flowers later in second year.

entries.notes: White-flowered biennial sweet clover; taller and slightly later than yellow type; major Northern Plains honey crop; same coumarin caution as yellow type for hay storage.
entries.source: USDA NRCS Plant Guide Melilotus albus

windows.notes: Drill at 18 to 25 lb per acre after summer crop; winter annual in SE US.

entries.notes: SE US standard reseeding crimson clover; bright crimson red flowers in spring; reseeds annually if allowed to mature; classic SE conservation cover crop and beekeeper favorite.

windows.notes: Cold-tolerant berseem; survives to 15F vs 25F for standard types.

entries.notes: Cold-tolerant berseem clover bred at OSU; annual Egyptian-origin clover; multiple cuttings possible; productive forage and green manure for transition season between summer and winter crops.

windows.notes: Multi-cut annual; drill at 20 lb per acre; can be harvested 3 to 4 times in long season.

entries.notes: Mississippi public release berseem clover; high biomass quick-growing winter annual; standard Gulf Coast and lower SE cover crop and grazing legume; tolerates wet soils.
entries.source: Mississippi State Univ Bigbee berseem release

windows.notes: Drill at 8 to 10 lb per acre; reseeds annually in Gulf Coast climates.

entries.notes: Late-maturing reseeding annual clover; high yield as forage; SE US winter cover with strong spring biomass surge; arrow-shaped leaves with white triangle.
entries.source: Univ of Georgia Extension B-1216 Arrowleaf Clover

windows.notes: Drill at 5 to 8 lb per acre; cold tolerant to 5F; tolerates wet soils.

entries.notes: Annual Turkish-origin clover; cold hardy and wet-tolerant; fixes high N (up to 150 lb per acre) in 60 to 80 days; popular newer cover crop in Mid-Atlantic and southern Midwest.

windows.notes: Drill at 18 to 25 lb per acre; mid-maturity crimson; reseeds reliably in SE.

entries.notes: Mid-maturing crimson clover from Mississippi State; brilliant crimson flowers; pollinator and beekeeper favorite; classic SE conservation tillage cover.
entries.source: Mississippi State Univ Frontier crimson clover release

windows.notes: Self-burying seedpods; reseeds reliably; tolerates close grazing and mowing.

entries.notes: Australian-bred subterranean clover with high seed yield and grazing tolerance; ideal for orchard floors and pasture under traffic. Self-buries seeds at maturity for natural reseeding.

windows.notes: Drill at 25 to 30 lb per acre; flowers 2 weeks earlier than standard hairy vetch.

entries.notes: USDA Beltsville selection of early-flowering hairy vetch; flowers 10 to 14 days earlier than common types; allows earlier termination and cash-crop planting in vegetable rotations.
entries.source: USDA ARS GRIN Vicia Purple Bounty

windows.notes: French-origin variety; very winter hardy; drill at 25 lb per acre.

entries.notes: French-bred winter-hardy hairy vetch; reliable nitrogen accumulator (up to 200 lb N per acre); standard organic vegetable cover crop in Mid-Atlantic and Midwest USA.
entries.source: Cornell Cover Crop Decision Tool Hairy Vetch

windows.notes: Annual vetch; drill at 30 lb per acre; less winter hardy than hairy vetch.

entries.notes: Annual vetch used as winter cover crop in Mediterranean climates and West Coast vineyards; faster decomposition than hairy vetch; mild N accumulation 80 to 120 lb per acre.

windows.notes: Drill at 60 lb per acre in spring; cool-season annual; tolerates drought once established.

entries.notes: Drought-tolerant spring-planted annual; rapid 60-day biomass; popular Northern Plains green manure. Caution: seeds contain neurotoxin ODAP; not for human or livestock seed consumption.
entries.source: Northern Plains Sustainable Ag Society Chickling Vetch

windows.notes: Drill at 6 to 10 lb per acre; slow establishment first year; persistent thereafter.

entries.notes: Cornell-bred birdsfoot trefoil; non-bloating pasture legume; tolerates acid and poorly drained soils where alfalfa fails; contains tannins that improve protein digestion in ruminants.

windows.notes: Drill at 30 lb per acre; deep taproot; tolerates dry calcareous soils.

entries.notes: Drought-tolerant perennial legume; non-bloating; major honey plant with bright pink flowers; ideal for dryland forage in alkaline soils of Intermountain West.
entries.source: USDA NRCS Plant Guide Onobrychis viciifolia

windows.notes: Inoculate seed; very slow first year; aggressive thereafter; primarily for erosion control.

entries.notes: Aggressive perennial used for highway slope stabilization; pink and white flowers attractive to bees. Caution: not suitable for forage; foliage contains glycosides toxic to non-ruminant animals.
entries.source: USDA NRCS Plant Guide Securigera varia

windows.notes: Drill at 50 to 70 lb per acre; winter cover in zones 6+; freeze-killed in colder zones.

entries.notes: Winter-annual nitrogen-fixing pea; major SE and Pacific NW cover crop; quick spring biomass; mixes well with cereal rye. Edible shoots for foragers; mild fresh pea flavor.
entries.source: Penn State Extension Austrian Winter Pea

windows.notes: Spring drill at 90 lb per acre; matures pea seed by midsummer in Northern Plains.

entries.notes: Spring-planted field pea for Northern Plains; small white seeds used as livestock split-pea protein and human soup-pea market; alternative cool-season grain legume.
entries.source: North Dakota State Univ Extension A1166

windows.notes: Winter-hardy field pea; survives to minus 10F with snow cover.

entries.notes: Winter-hardy field pea for Pacific NW and Northern Rockies fall planting; matures grain in early summer; new winter dry-pea industry alternative to Austrian winter pea.
entries.source: Univ of Idaho Extension PNW644 Winter Peas

windows.notes: Drill at 25 lb per acre; reseeds annually in SE pasture; tolerates acid soils.

entries.notes: Warm-season annual lespedeza; standard SE US summer pasture supplement; tolerates very acid low-fertility soils where most legumes fail; non-bloating.

windows.notes: Drill at 25 lb per acre; later maturing than Kobe; bacterial wilt resistant.

entries.notes: Wilt-resistant late-maturing annual lespedeza; SE US summer haying and grazing legume; reseeds itself; ideal for low-input pasture renovation.
entries.source: USDA NRCS Plant Guide Kummerowia striata

windows.notes: PNW understory shrub; tolerates wet shaded sites; spreads by rhizome.

entries.notes: PNW native; salmon-pink to red-orange edible berries ripening May to June; first wild berry of PNW season; traditional Coast Salish food. Pink flowers in early spring are pollinator-critical.
entries.source: USDA NRCS Plant Guide Rubus spectabilis

windows.notes: Native thornless raspberry relative; spreads by rhizome; partial shade tolerant.

entries.notes: Thornless thimble-shaped soft red fruits with intense fruit-punch flavor; very fragile, dont ship at all; harvest fresh from PNW and N Rocky Mountain understory; traditional First Nations food.
entries.source: USDA NRCS Plant Guide Rubus parviflorus

windows.notes: Trailing canes; trellis at 6 ft; bears on floricanes second year.

entries.notes: Thornless tissue-culture clone of classic Marion blackberry from Oregon State Univ; intense rich winey blackberry flavor with raspberry undertones; star Pacific NW commercial caneberry.

windows.notes: Cold stratify seed 4 weeks. Sow shallow in well-drained soil.
Fall sowing allows natural stratification over winter.

entries.notes: Root harvested in autumn of 2nd or 3rd year. Classic TCM liver-harmonizing herb. Full sun, sandy loam, drought tolerant once established.

windows.notes: Plant divisions or potted starts after frost. Rich, moist soil with afternoon shade.

entries.notes: Root used fresh (sheng di) or prepared (shu di) as foundational yin tonic. Tubular purple flowers. Loose loamy soil with consistent moisture. Mulch heavily.

windows.notes: Plant divisions in spring. Tolerates shade. Spreads by stolons.

entries.notes: Small tuberous roots used to moisten lung yin and nourish stomach yin. Evergreen grass-like clumps. Part shade, even moisture. Harvest tubers in 3rd year.
entries.source: Trichilo and Holmes Eastern Earth Resources

windows.notes: Soak seed 24h. Transplant after frost to deep loose soil.
Space 12 inches. Full sun. Harvest rhizome in late autumn of 2nd year.

entries.notes: Rhizome is premier qi tonic and spleen strengthener in TCM. Pinkish thistle-like flowers. Well-drained sandy loam, full sun. Pinch flowers to boost root.

windows.notes: Plant rhizome divisions in spring. Cool moist soil.

entries.notes: Rhizome used to invigorate blood and dispel wind. Strong celery-like aroma. Part shade, rich moist soil, dislikes heat. Harvest in autumn.

windows.notes: Surface sow, needs cold stratification and light to germinate.

entries.notes: Bitter root drains damp-heat from liver and gallbladder. Brilliant blue trumpet flowers. Cool moist acidic soil, part shade. Slow from seed.

windows.notes: Surface sow, light required. Cold stratify 2 weeks improves germination.
Space 12 inches. Full sun, well-drained soil.

entries.notes: Yellow root (huang qin) clears damp-heat and cools blood. Distinct from S. lateriflora. Drought tolerant, gravelly soil. Harvest 2nd or 3rd year.

windows.notes: Plant aerial bulbils or tuber pieces 4 inches deep. Provide trellis.

entries.notes: Long white tuber nourishes spleen, lung, and kidney qi. Vining climber, can be invasive in some regions. Deep loose soil, full sun to part shade.
entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension herb production guide

windows.notes: Plant 2yr potted plants. Root harvest after 4 years minimum.

entries.notes: Spiny shrub, adaptogenic root bark. Full sun to part shade, well-drained soil. Distinct from Panax ginseng. Long-term planting commitment.

windows.notes: Surface sow, light required. Slow to establish first year.

entries.notes: White root expels phlegm and opens lungs. Inflated bud opens to blue star flower. Full sun, deep loose soil. Harvest in 2nd or 3rd autumn.

windows.notes: Fresh seed only, sow in autumn for natural stratification.

entries.notes: Aromatic root is primary blood tonic for women. Tall umbel, cool moist part shade, rich soil. Harvest before flowering in 2nd year.

windows.notes: Plant dormant cuttings or potted shrubs. Tolerates most soils.

entries.notes: Fruit harvested green or ripe clears heat and toxins, key in Yin Qiao San formula. Arching shrub, full sun to part shade. Drought tolerant once established.

windows.notes: Plant rhizome divisions horizontally, 2 inches deep. Shade required.

entries.notes: Rhizome moistens lungs and nourishes yin. Arching stems with paired bell flowers. Deep shade, rich humus, undisturbed woodland. Harvest 3rd autumn.

windows.notes: Plant under heavy shade in deep humus. Mimics goldenseal habitat.

entries.notes: Bitter yellow rhizome drains damp-heat and clears fire toxins. Evergreen woodland plant, deep shade, acidic humus rich soil. Slow growing, 4 to 6 year crop.

windows.notes: Cold stratify 4 weeks. Sandy well-drained soil.

entries.notes: Root expels wind-cold and dispels surface pathogens. Steppe umbel, full sun, drought tolerant. Harvest 2nd or 3rd year autumn before flowering.

windows.notes: Scarify and soak seed. Slow to germinate.
Deep loose alkaline soil. Spreads via stolons.

entries.notes: Sweet root harmonizes formulas and tonifies qi. Spreads aggressively, plant where it can run. Full sun, sandy alkaline soil. Harvest 3rd or 4th autumn.

windows.notes: Direct sow in cool soil. Thin to 8 inches.

entries.notes: Root (ban lan gen) and leaf (da qing ye) clear heat and antiviral. Related to woad. Full sun, average soil. Harvest root in autumn of first year.

windows.notes: Plant divisions or rooted cuttings. Contain spreading.

entries.notes: Aerial parts disperse wind-heat and clear head. Containerize to manage runners. Sun to part shade, moist soil. Harvest just before flowering.

windows.notes: Surface sow, needs light. Tiny seed.
Space 2 feet. Reaches 6+ feet tall.

entries.notes: Aerial parts clear summer heat and treat malaria, source of artemisinin. Full sun, average soil, drought tolerant. Harvest at early bud stage.

windows.notes: Surface sow, light to germinate.

entries.notes: Flower spike clears liver heat and cools eyes in TCM use. Spreads by stolons. Sun to part shade, moist soil. Harvest spikes in summer when fully open.

windows.notes: Plant rooted cuttings or divisions. Pinch through July.

entries.notes: Yellow or white flower clears liver and eye heat, paired with goji. Full sun, rich well-drained soil. Harvest flowers fully open in autumn, dry whole.
entries.source: Trichilo and Holmes Eastern Earth Resources

windows.notes: Plant rooted cuttings. Provide trellis. Invasive in SE US, contain.

entries.notes: Flower buds clear heat and resolve toxins, key in Yin Qiao San. Vigorous vine, can be invasive. Sun to part shade. Harvest buds just before opening.

windows.notes: Plant rooted cuttings or bareroot. Tolerates poor soil.

entries.notes: Root bark (di gu pi) cools deficient heat; berry (gou qi zi) tonifies yin. Spiny arching shrub, full sun, drought tolerant. Harvest root bark in autumn.
entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension herb production guide

windows.notes: Plant fresh seed or seedlings under 70% shade cloth.

entries.notes: Root stops bleeding and invigorates blood, key trauma herb. Requires deep shade, cool humid mountain conditions, acidic humus. Harvest 3 to 5 years.

windows.notes: Plant rooted cuttings. Provide trellis or let trail.

entries.notes: Aerial parts as adaptogenic tea, called Southern Ginseng. Part shade, moist rich soil, climbing vine. Harvest leaf and stem through growing season.

windows.notes: Surface sow in saturated mix. Tiny seed needs light and warmth.
Plant in saturated soil or shallow water in cold zones. Container indoors winter.

entries.notes: Aerial parts are classic Ayurvedic nervine and memory tonic. Bog plant, needs constant moisture. Full sun to part shade. Continuous harvest of fresh leaf.

windows.notes: Plant runners after warmth settles. Container in zones below 8.

entries.notes: Leaf is brain tonic and wound healer in Ayurveda and TCM. Creeping mat-former, moist part shade, can grow in shallow water. Harvest leaves continuously.

windows.notes: Surface sow, needs light and 75F to germinate.
Plant when nights consistently above 55F. Pinch tips.

entries.notes: Purple-leafed variety, most pungent. Sacred adaptogen in Ayurveda, calms mind and supports respiration. Full sun, average soil. Pinch flowers for leaf production.

windows.notes: Surface sow on warmed mix, 75 to 80F.
Plant when soil reliably above 65F. Becomes shrub in zone 10+.

entries.notes: Wild forest tulsi, clove-scented, tallest of the tulsis. Used in Ayurveda for respiration and as nervine. Full sun, moderate water. Overwinter indoors in cold zones.

windows.notes: Soak seed 24h. Slow to germinate, 3 to 6 weeks.
Climbing shrub, provide trellis. Container in cold zones.

entries.notes: Tuberous root cluster is primary female reproductive tonic in Ayurveda. Climbing thorny shrub. Full sun to part shade. Harvest roots at 2 to 3 years.

windows.notes: Plant seedlings in warm season. Container indoors in cold zones.

entries.notes: Leaf and oil are bitter blood purifier and antimicrobial in Ayurveda. Tropical tree, drought tolerant once established. Full sun, no frost tolerance. Container 8 ft.

windows.notes: Plant grafted trees for faster fruiting. Frost sensitive.

entries.notes: Sour fruit is highest natural vitamin C, prime rasayana in Ayurveda. Subtropical tree, tolerates poor soil. Full sun. Container culture possible in cold zones.

windows.notes: Plant rooted stem cuttings, easy to propagate. Provide trellis.

entries.notes: Stem is premier immunomodulator and amrita rasayana in Ayurveda. Climbing vine with heart-shaped leaves. Full sun to part shade. Container culture for cold zones.

windows.notes: Scarify large seed. Warm conditions required.
Plant after all frost danger. Aggressive climber needs strong trellis.

entries.notes: Seed is rich in L-DOPA, used for nervous system and reproductive tonic. Vining legume, full sun, heat-loving. Wear gloves, hairs irritate skin.

windows.notes: Plant slips or tissue-cultured starts after warmth settles.

entries.notes: Cooling aromatic root is pitta-balancing and used in oils. Non-invasive bunch grass with deep roots. Full sun, tolerates wet and dry. Harvest roots at 18 months.

windows.notes: Tropical tree, container culture in cold zones. Frost sensitive.

entries.notes: Fruit is one of three in triphala, supports kapha and respiration. Large tropical tree to 100 ft. Full sun, moderate water. Slow to fruit from seed.

windows.notes: Tropical tree, plant seedlings or grafted starts. Frost sensitive.
