entries.notes: Fruit is king of medicines in Ayurveda, balances vata and supports digestion. Tropical deciduous tree. Full sun, well-drained soil. Container only in cold zones.

windows.notes: Tropical riverbank tree. Plant in warm season. Frost sensitive.

entries.notes: Bark is primary cardiac tonic in Ayurveda. Tall riverside tree, prefers moisture. Full sun, alluvial soil. Bark harvested from mature trees at 10+ years.

windows.notes: Surface sow in warm mix, 70 to 80F.
Heat-loving sprawler. Container in colder zones.

entries.notes: Whole plant is diuretic and reduces kapha edema in Ayurveda. Spreading herb with tiny pink flowers. Full sun, well-drained soil. Tropical to subtropical.

windows.notes: Plant tuberous root divisions in warm soil.

entries.notes: White tuberous roots are rasayana and male reproductive tonic. Tuberous-rooted herb similar to spider plant. Sun to part shade. Harvest in autumn dieback.

windows.notes: Surface sow, fine seed, warmth required.
Plant after warmth settles. Self-sows readily.

entries.notes: Whole plant supports liver and kidney function in Ayurveda. Small annual herb to 18 inches. Full sun to part shade, moist soil. Often weedy in tropics.

windows.notes: Plant rooted cuttings in warm soil. Vining habit.

entries.notes: Aromatic root is cooling alterative for blood and skin. Twining vine with milky sap. Part shade, moist rich soil. Container in cold zones.

windows.notes: Fresh seed only, surface sow, light to germinate.

entries.notes: Leaf, stem, and yellow sap rich in chalcones, longevity tonic in Japan. Tall umbel, part shade, moist rich soil. Continuous leaf harvest, hence the name tomorrow's leaf.

windows.notes: Plant grafted starts in warm season. Frost protect in cold zones.

entries.notes: Sour fruit balances pitta and is part of triphala. Subtropical tree to 60 ft. Full sun, moderate water. Bears in 4 to 6 years from grafted stock.

windows.notes: Plant rooted cuttings in warm humid conditions. Container culture in cold zones.

entries.notes: Catkin fruit is warming digestive and respiratory rejuvenative in Ayurveda. Climbing tropical vine, needs humidity. Part shade. Greenhouse only outside zone 10+.

windows.notes: Surface sow, fine seed, warmth and light required.
Heat and moisture lover. Self-seeds in warm climates.

entries.notes: Whole plant is hair tonic and liver rejuvenative in Ayurveda. Sprawling herb with small white daisy flowers. Full sun, wet soil. Often weedy near ricefields.

windows.notes: Plant rooted cuttings or seedlings, slow establishment.

entries.notes: Berries are classic Ayurvedic anthelmintic and digestive. Climbing or scandent shrub, tropical understory. Part shade, moist soil. Endangered in wild.

windows.notes: Plant rhizome divisions in mud or shallow water.

entries.notes: Aromatic rhizome is brain and speech tonic in Ayurveda, called the speaker. Wetland marginal, grows in 6 inches water or saturated soil. Full sun.

windows.notes: Plant in cool moist conditions. High elevation native, sensitive to heat.

entries.notes: Aromatic rhizome is nervine sedative and meditation aid in Ayurveda. Endangered Himalayan native. Cool moist part shade, well-drained gravelly soil.

windows.notes: Direct sow when soil reaches 60F. Thin to 6 inches.

entries.notes: Thyme-scented seed is digestive carminative, similar to thymol. Slender umbel, full sun, well-drained soil. Harvest seed heads when brown but before shatter.

windows.notes: Surface sow in autumn for natural cold stratification.
Plant rooted divisions in cool conditions. Avoid heat.

entries.notes: Root and rhizome are adaptogen for cold mountain environments. Succulent rosette of glaucous leaves. Full sun, gravelly well-drained soil. Suffers in heat and humidity.

windows.notes: Fall sow for stratification. Or stratify 4 weeks for spring sow.
Plant divisions in spring. Harvest root in 3rd autumn.

entries.notes: Selected for large horizontal pink rays, immune tonic root. Full sun, well-drained soil, drought tolerant. Harvest 3 year old roots in late autumn.

windows.notes: Cold stratify 8 to 12 weeks. Fall sow ideal.

entries.notes: Native prairie species, taproot is traditional Plains Indians remedy for snake bite and infection. Full sun, dry sandy soil. Harvest 4 year roots.

windows.notes: Cold stratify 6 to 10 weeks. Fall sow ideal.

entries.notes: Drooping pale pink rays, taproot for immune use. Full sun, dry well-drained soil, drought tolerant once established. Harvest root 3rd or 4th year.

windows.notes: Cold stratify 4 to 8 weeks. Distinct upturned rays.

entries.notes: Once federally endangered, narrow-rayed coneflower from cedar glades. Full sun, alkaline well-drained soil. Harvest root in 3rd year for medicine.

windows.notes: Fall sow for stratification. Or stratify 4 weeks for spring sow.

entries.notes: North American native, aerial parts in flower are nervine and bitter. Tall candelabra of blue spikes. Sun, moist soil, meadow plant. Harvest at full bloom.

windows.notes: Surface sow, light required. May benefit from stratification.

entries.notes: Classic European bitter nervine, aerial parts in flower. Wiry stems with tiny pale lilac flowers. Full sun, average soil. Sacred herb of Druids and Romans.

windows.notes: Surface sow, light required. Tolerates cool soil.

entries.notes: Annual chamomile, flower heads for digestion and sleep. Hollow conical receptacle, distinguishes from Roman. Full sun, average soil. Self-sows readily.
entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension herb production guide

windows.notes: Cool weather crop, prefers spring or fall. Self-sows aggressively.
Fall sow for winter and early spring harvest.

entries.notes: Whole plant fresh, lymphatic mover and cooling demulcent. Star-shaped white flowers, single line of hairs on stem. Cool moist part shade, rich soil.

windows.notes: Fall sow allows winter germination. Self-sows readily.

entries.notes: Whole plant fresh, lymphatic alterative and kidney support. Sticky velcro-like stems, whorled leaves. Part shade, cool moist edges. Harvest before seeds form.

windows.notes: Plant rhizome divisions in cool soil. Spreads aggressively, contain.

entries.notes: Flower and leaf are demulcent expectorant. Yellow flowers emerge before hoof-shaped leaves. Sun to part shade, moist heavy soil. Contains PAs, use with knowledge.

windows.notes: Surface sow, light required. Drought tolerant once up.

entries.notes: Bitter aerial parts are classic respiratory and digestive herb. Woolly silver foliage, square stems. Full sun, poor dry soil, drought tolerant. Cut in flower.
entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension herb production guide

windows.notes: Cold stratify, fall sow ideal. Surface, light required.
Plant starts in moist part shade. Wetland edge native.

entries.notes: North American native, aerial parts in flower are nervine relaxant. One-sided racemes of small blue flowers. Part shade, moist rich soil. Distinct from baicalensis.

windows.notes: Plant rhizome divisions horizontally, 2 inches deep, eye up.

entries.notes: Rhizome is moistening connective tissue tonic, prized for joints and tendons. Arching woodland stems, paired bell flowers. Deep shade, humus rich soil.

windows.notes: Plant divisions in cool soil. Self-spreads by runners.

entries.notes: Leaf and flower are lymphatic mover and demulcent. Fragrant purple flowers in early spring, heart-shaped leaves. Part shade, moist humus soil.

windows.notes: Plant rhizome pieces 3 inches deep. Provide trellis.

entries.notes: Native eastern woodland vine, rhizome is antispasmodic and source of diosgenin. Heart-shaped leaves, twining left-handed. Part shade, rich moist soil. Endangered wild.

windows.notes: Plant balled or bareroot, ideally fall or early spring.

entries.notes: Leaf and bark are astringent, traditional skin and vein toner. Yellow ribbon flowers in late autumn. Understory shrub, part shade, moist acidic woods.

windows.notes: Surface sow, light to germinate. Or plant divisions.

entries.notes: Aerial parts are nervine and grounding tonic for digestion, prized for solar plexus. Rosette of scalloped leaves, pink spike. Full sun to part shade, average soil.

windows.notes: Surface sow, light required. Or take cuttings.

entries.notes: Bitter aerial parts for digestion and parasites, basis of absinthe. Silver finely divided foliage. Full sun, poor well-drained soil, drought tolerant.

windows.notes: Cold stratify 4 weeks. Tap root resents transplanting.

entries.notes: Taproot is diaphoretic and expectorant for lung conditions, traditional Eclectic remedy. Orange milkweed flowers, monarch host. Full sun, dry sandy soil.

windows.notes: Surface sow on acidic soil. Spreads aggressively by rhizome.

entries.notes: Whole plant fresh or dried, key in Essiac formula, cooling and astringent. Arrowhead-shaped leaves, tart taste. Full sun, acidic poor soil. Aggressive spreader.

windows.notes: Cold stratify, fall sow or stratify 6 weeks.

entries.notes: Aerial parts in flower are source of salicylates, antacid and anti-inflammatory. Frothy cream flower clusters, almond scent. Moist meadow, sun to part shade.

windows.notes: Surface sow, light required. Or plant divisions. Aggressive spreader.

entries.notes: Aerial parts, base of moxibustion (ai ye in TCM). Silver-backed lobed leaves, dark green above. Sun, average soil, drought tolerant. Contain spread.

windows.notes: Cold stratify 4 weeks, fall sow ideal.

entries.notes: Aerial parts in flower are astringent for digestion and urinary tract. Tall yellow flower spikes, pinnate leaves. Full sun to part shade, average soil.

windows.notes: Surface sow, light to germinate. Self-sows freely.

entries.notes: Aerial parts in flower are nervine and carminative, classic children's herb for fevers. Gray-green serrated leaves. Full sun, well-drained soil. Cats love.
entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension herb production guide

windows.notes: Plant divisions in wet alkaline soil or shallow water.

entries.notes: Root and leaf are aromatic antimicrobial used by Southwest herbalists for mucosal infections. Wetland plant of alkaline desert seeps. Full sun, saturated soil.

windows.notes: Fresh seed only. Cold moist stratification 12+ weeks. Difficult to cultivate.

entries.notes: Root is at-risk Rocky Mountain bear medicine for respiratory infections. Symbiotic with conifer mycorrhizae, near impossible to cultivate below subalpine. Do not wildcraft.

windows.notes: Cold stratify 4 weeks. Or plant divisions.

entries.notes: Root is traditional urinary tract herb for kidney stones. Tall whorled leaves, dome of dusty pink flowers. Sun to part shade, moist rich soil. Pollinator magnet.

windows.notes: Surface sow, needs light and warmth 70F+.
Plant when warm. Pinch for bushier plants.

entries.notes: Flower buds cause tingling buzz on tongue, immune stimulant and antimicrobial. Tropical sprawler, full sun, rich moist soil. Continuous flower harvest.
Japanese komatsuna × cabbage interspecific hybrid; sweet thick bright green leaves on quick rosette. Faster than collards, milder than mustard.
entries.source: Kitazawa Seed Company variety notes; Johnny's Selected Seeds

entries.notes: Cantonese flowering brassica harvested at the bud stage; tender stem and small yellow flowers. The defining green for chinese stir-fry oyster sauce dishes.
entries.source: Kitazawa Seed Company variety notes; UH Manoa Cooperative Extension

entries.notes: Kyoto traditional pickling green; narrow strap-like leaves on dense rosette, mild mustard flavor. Cold-hardy, harvest individual leaves cut-and-come-again.
entries.source: Kitazawa Seed Company variety notes; Kyoto Prefectural University specialty greens notes

entries.notes: Cantonese-Hong Kong cooking green; longer flowering stems than choy sum, harvested at full size. Bright sweet stem with mild brassica leaf.
entries.source: Kitazawa Seed Company variety notes; UH Manoa Cooperative Extension

entries.notes: Japanese mustard spinach; tender mild spinach-mustard flavor. Cold-hardy through 25 F; the standard Japanese pickling green.
entries.source: Kitazawa Seed Company variety notes; Cornell vegetable variety database

entries.notes: Japanese rosette green; flat spoon-shaped dark green leaves. Cold-hardy to 15 F; the standard salad-mix winter green.
entries.source: Johnny's Selected Seeds variety notes; Kitazawa Seed Company

entries.notes: Cantonese broccoli relative grown for thick tender stems and small flower clusters. Bittersweet flavor; the defining green of yum cha gai lan dishes.
entries.source: Kitazawa Seed Company variety notes; UH Manoa Cooperative Extension

entries.notes: Japanese / Korean edible chrysanthemum; aromatic leaves with sweet-bitter notes. The defining green of Japanese sukiyaki and Korean ssam.
entries.source: Kitazawa Seed Company variety notes; Cornell vegetable variety database

entries.notes: Tropical climbing vine with red-purple stems and thick succulent leaves; heat-tolerant spinach substitute. Productive once warm; the summer spinach.
entries.source: UF/IFAS tropical greens notes; Kitazawa Seed Company

humid_subtropical.notes: Premier heat-tolerant Southern spinach substitute; vines spread to 8 ft in Florida summers. Trellis vertically for clean leaves.

continental.notes: Tender perennial vine; treat as annual after last frost. Harvest leaves at 2 to 3 inches for tenderness.

arid.notes: Drought-tolerant once established; the defining summer green for desert SW kitchens. Drip-irrigate twice weekly.

maritime.notes: Marginal in cool maritime; choose only in long warm summers and use black plastic with greenhouse start. Growth slows below 70F.

entries.notes: Asian ridged luffa; eat at 6 to 8 inches like zucchini. Past 12 inches fiber develops and they become sponge gourds. The defining Vietnamese muop khia.
entries.source: Kitazawa Seed Company variety notes; UF/IFAS tropical cucurbits

entries.notes: Pale-green smooth-skinned bitter gourd; milder than the warty Indian type. The defining vegetable of stir-fried bitter melon and Okinawan champuru.
entries.source: Kitazawa Seed Company variety notes; UH Manoa Cooperative Extension

entries.notes: Large pale waxy melon, 20 to 50 lb; mild cucumber-like flesh used in soup, stir-fry, traditional Chinese banquet sculptures. Stores 6+ months at room temperature.
entries.source: Kitazawa Seed Company variety notes; UH Manoa Cooperative Extension

entries.notes: Small fuzzy-skinned summer-harvested winter melon relative; tender flesh used in soup and stir-fry. The young harvest of the winter melon family.
entries.source: Kitazawa Seed Company variety notes; UH Manoa Cooperative Extension

entries.notes: South Asian climbing cucurbit; 3 to 6 ft long pale green snake-like fruits, cucumber-like flesh. The defining vegetable of South Indian pudalangai kootu.
entries.source: UF/IFAS tropical cucurbits notes; Indian Agricultural Research Institute records

windows.notes: Plant after corn reaches 6 inches in Three Sisters polyculture

entries.notes: Haudenosaunee Six Nations heirloom climbing bean; companion grown with corn and squash in the traditional Three Sisters polyculture. Mottled dry beans.
entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension Iroquois Three Sisters research; USDA-ARS heritage cultivars

entries.notes: Cherokee multicolored flint corn selected by Carl Barnes from his Pawnee, Osage, and Cherokee heritage lines. Translucent jewel-toned kernels; flour or popcorn use.
entries.source: Native Seeds/SEARCH Carl Barnes archive; Seed Savers Exchange

entries.commonName: Cherokee Trail of Tears Pole Bean
entries.notes: Carried by the Cherokee on the 1838 Trail of Tears forced removal; deep purple pods, shiny black dry beans. SSE preservation listing.
entries.source: Seed Savers Exchange Cherokee heritage seed collection

entries.notes: Plains Arikara people upper Missouri river heritage Cucurbita pepo; yellow-orange flattened pumpkin, sweet drying flesh. Repatriated through Dream of Wild Health and Seed Savers Exchange.
entries.source: Dream of Wild Health seed sovereignty project; Seed Savers Exchange

entries.notes: Northern Minnesota Anishinaabe heritage flint corn; 90-day short-season ultra-cold-tolerant grain corn. Multicolored hard kernels for hominy and parched corn.
entries.source: Seed Savers Exchange Anishinaabe heritage seed; Dream of Wild Health

entries.notes: Mandan / Hidatsa Missouri River heritage soft flour corn; multicolored short ears, traditional bread and flour corn. Repatriated through Native Seeds/SEARCH and Dream of Wild Health.
entries.source: Native Seeds/SEARCH; Dream of Wild Health seed sovereignty project

entries.notes: Jamaican / Trinidadian green; tender amaranth leaves stir-fried or stewed. The defining green of Jamaican Saturday-soup callaloo. Cut-and-come-again.
entries.source: UF/IFAS tropical greens notes; UPR Mayaguez specialty crops

humid_subtropical.notes: Ideal climate; sow May through Sep for continuous cut-and-come-again leaf harvest. Tolerates Florida summer heat where Western spinach fails.

continental.notes: Direct sow after soil hits 60F; harvest leaves through frost. The summer spinach substitute for zones 5 to 7.

arid.notes: Drought-tolerant once established; drip-irrigate twice weekly through summer heat. The defining heat-tolerant green for desert SW kitchens.

maritime.notes: Marginal in cool maritime; choose for ornamental and as a slow-growing kitchen green. Cool summers limit production compared to true heat zones.

entries.notes: Jamaican national fruit; the yellow arils of the ripe (only fully open) fruit. WARNING: unripe ackee contains hypoglycin A and is fatally toxic. The defining ingredient of ackee and saltfish.
entries.source: UF/IFAS Tropical Research and Education Center; FDA ackee import standards

windows.notes: Annual outside zone 9; tender perennial shrub in zones 9 plus

entries.notes: Caribbean / Indian / African short-lived shrub legume; the defining peas of rice and peas (Jamaica), gandules (Puerto Rico), and toor dal (India). Nitrogen-fixing, drought tolerant.
entries.source: UF/IFAS Tropical Research; UPR Mayaguez specialty crops

windows.notes: Stem cuttings 10 to 12 inches; plant horizontally

entries.notes: High-cyanide cassava varieties for industrial flour and gari; requires fermentation/cooking processing to detoxify. The defining starch of West African and Caribbean cuisines.
entries.source: UF/IFAS Tropical Research; FAO cassava processing guidelines

entries.notes: Caribbean / Latin American aroid tuber; nutty earthy flavor distinct from taro. The defining root vegetable of yuca con mojo and sancocho.
entries.source: UPR Mayaguez tropical roots research; UF/IFAS Tropical Research

windows.notes: Plant the entire fruit at 45 degree angle with shoulder exposed

entries.notes: Spiny-fruited chayote strain; same Sechium edule species as smooth chayote with spines that scrub off. Vigorous vine; the defining cho-cho of Caribbean cooking.
entries.source: UF/IFAS Tropical Research; UPR Mayaguez specialty crops

entries.notes: Mexican domestic ancestor of quinoa; tender immature seed-flower stalks battered and fried as the classic Lent dish tortitas de huauzontle. Mature seed also eaten.
entries.source: Native Seeds/SEARCH Mexican Chenopodium collection; UNAM Etnobotanico

entries.notes: Guatemalan / Salvadoran legume shrub leaf vegetable; tender leaves used in tamales de chipilin and chipilin con bolitas. Nitrogen-fixing.
entries.source: Native Seeds/SEARCH; UC ANR small-scale specialty crops

entries.notes: Oaxacan culinary herb related to papalo; thin needle-like leaves with sweeter milder cilantro-aniseed flavor. The defining herb of memelas and clayudas.
entries.source: Native Seeds/SEARCH; Oaxaca culinary tradition records

entries.notes: West African ancient grain; fastest-maturing grain in the world. Tiny tan seeds, mild nutty couscous-like cook. Drought tolerant.
entries.source: FAO traditional African grains program; Native Seeds/SEARCH

entries.notes: West African Vigna; flowers pollinate above-ground then bury, producing groundnuts in the soil. Drought-adapted, high-protein.
entries.source: FAO traditional African legumes program; USDA-ARS plant introduction collection

entries.notes: West African seed-melon; bitter inedible flesh, but the seeds are ground to thicken egusi soup. Drought tolerant relative of watermelon.
entries.source: FAO traditional African crops; USDA-ARS plant introduction collection

entries.notes: Ethiopian Brassica carinata leaf cabbage; cool-season collards substitute. The defining green of gomen wat.
entries.source: USDA-ARS Plant Introduction; UF/IFAS Brassica carinata trial

windows.notes: Surface-sow tiny seed; firm-press into prepared bed

entries.notes: Ethiopian tef cultivar with cream-colored seed; the defining grain of injera flatbread. Drought tolerant; gluten-free traditional grain.
Mesoamerican heritage grain amaranth; deep purple-magenta seed heads, white seeds. Both leaves (young) and grain are eaten; the defining amaranth of pre-Columbian Mexican cuisine.
Cultivated golden purslane; large succulent yellow-green leaves, lemony omega-3-rich. The defining wild green domesticated for European salads.
entries.source: Cornell vegetable variety database; OSU Extension foraged greens

entries.notes: Cultivated giant lambsquarters; 6 to 8 ft plant with magenta-pink new growth, spinach-like leaves. The premier giant Chenopodium green.
entries.source: Seed Savers Exchange; Cornell vegetable variety database

entries.notes: Botanically a chicory (Cichorium intybus) not a true dandelion; red-veined Italian leaf vegetable. The defining green of cicorietta and Roman risotto.
entries.source: Cornell vegetable variety database; Italian heritage seed catalogs

windows.notes: Year-round indoor production on supplemented hardwood sawdust

entries.notes: Italian black-poplar mushroom; clustered brown caps with white stems, savory peppery-nutty flavor. The defining culinary mushroom of central Italian forest cuisine.
entries.source: Cornell Small Farms Program; Field & Forest Products cultivar notes

windows.notes: Outdoor woodchip bed inoculation in shaded garden corners

entries.notes: Outdoor wood-chip-bed gourmet mushroom; burgundy caps the size of dinner plates. The premier garden-edge edible mushroom; suppresses soil pathogens.
entries.source: Cornell Small Farms Program; Paul Stamets 'Mycelium Running'

entries.notes: Cold-fruiting Pleurotus ostreatus strain producing dense blue-gray clusters; the fastest gourmet mushroom to fruit (2 to 4 weeks from inoculation).
entries.source: Field & Forest Products strain notes; Cornell Small Farms Program

windows.notes: Federally regulated noxious weed in many US states; check local rules before planting

entries.notes: Southeast Asian semiaquatic green; the defining vegetable of pad pak boong and rau muong. Federally listed as a noxious weed in most US states; check local rules.
entries.source: USDA APHIS noxious weed list; Kitazawa Seed Company variety notes

windows.notes: Likes wet feet; pond edge or constantly moist soil

entries.notes: Korean / Japanese / Vietnamese semiaquatic herb; thin parsley-like leaves with sweeter celery-carrot flavor. The defining herb of Korean minari-jeon and Vietnamese rau can.
entries.source: Kitazawa Seed Company variety notes; UPR Mayaguez tropical herbs

windows.notes: Mix typically winter rye + hairy vetch + crimson clover + Austrian winter pea

entries.notes: Standard fall-planted nitrogen-fixer plus biomass cover crop mix; rye for biomass and soil structure, legumes for nitrogen. Terminate at flowering for max nitrogen contribution.
entries.source: SARE Cover Crops handbook; Penn State Cover Crop Council

windows.notes: Mix typically buckwheat + cowpea + sorghum-sudangrass + sunn hemp

entries.notes: Quick summer cover crop blend; buckwheat for fast canopy and phosphorus, cowpea and sunn hemp for nitrogen fixation, sorghum-sudangrass for biomass. Terminate at 60 days for max benefit.
entries.source: SARE Cover Crops handbook; UMass Extension Vegetable Program

entries.notes: Floral cover crop mix for native bees and butterflies; mix typically buckwheat + phacelia + crimson clover + sunflower + dill + cilantro. Let bloom fully before terminating.
entries.source: Xerces Society pollinator cover crop guidelines; SARE Cover Crops

entries.notes: Daikon-radish cover crop selected for fast deep taproot; biological tillage breaks hardpan. Winterkills below 20 F in zone 7 north, decomposes in spring.
entries.source: SARE Cover Crops handbook; Penn State Cover Crop Council

entries.notes: California native Phacelia; cobalt-blue lacy flowers, the highest-rated honeybee forage cover crop. Non-legume, contributes biomass and pollinator habitat.
entries.source: Xerces Society pollinator cover crops; UC ANR

entries.notes: Tropical legume cover crop; 6 ft fast-growing nitrogen-fixing biomass producer, nematode-suppressive. The premier warm-season cover for the deep South.
entries.source: USDA-NRCS Plant Materials; SARE Cover Crops handbook

entries.notes: Asian / African tropical legume cover crop; aggressive nitrogen-fixing vine producing 8000 lbs dry biomass per acre. Smothers weeds. Frost-tender.
entries.source: FAO tropical cover crops; UF/IFAS sustainable agriculture notes
